# Chat Conversation

*Exported on 4/25/2026, 9:22:39 PM*

---

## User

Explore the possibility of using Vega as a game engine for an RTS

---

## Assistant

Before diving in, let me clarify which "Vega" you mean, since there are a few candidates and they'd each lead to very different analyses:

That said — given your interests (GPU compute shader pipelines, unified FFT/DCT/DWT/GFT frameworks, statistical manifolds, hypergraph representations), my strong prior is that you're asking about Vega the visualization grammar as an unconventional RTS substrate. That's the genuinely interesting question, so let me sketch that case while you confirm.

Vega is a declarative grammar of interactive graphics — you specify what should be rendered as a JSON spec of data, transforms, scales, marks, and signals, and the runtime handles the reactive dataflow. The core architectural fit with RTS is actually striking in some ways:

Where it maps well

RTS games are, underneath the visuals, a large reactive system over entity tables. Units have positions, HP, faction, state; the renderer is a function of that table plus a camera transform. Vega's signal/dataflow model is exactly this: data sources feed transforms feed marks, and signals propagate updates automatically. Unit selection, fog-of-war masking, minimap rendering, health bars, and HUD overlays are all legitimately natural Vega constructs — they're just data-driven marks with scales and predicates.

The pan/zoom/select interaction grammar is already first-class. A minimap is genuinely a second view on the same dataset with a different projection — which is something Vega does elegantly and most game engines handle as bespoke code.

Where it breaks down

The fatal issues are all on the simulation side, not the rendering side:

If the real goal is "can a declarative dataflow grammar serve as the rendering and UI layer of an RTS, with simulation handled separately" — that's a much more defensible idea and one that connects to your telemetry-to-graph compiler work. The architecture would look like:

This is basically treating the game state as a streaming dataset and the HUD+world view as a visualization over it. You get reactive UI essentially for free, retained-mode debugging views (draw the pathfinding graph, the influence map, the unit decision tree) trivially, and a clean simulation/presentation split. The cost is that you're not really using Vega-the-product — you're using Vega-the-idea, and reimplementing the parts that matter in something GPU-native.

For production RTS scale you'd want this compiled, not interpreted at runtime. Vega's JSON spec → dataflow graph pipeline is itself compilable; a "Vega for games" would lower specs to ECS queries and compute shader dispatches rather than JS closures.

Vega the product, as-is: no. The simulation-side gaps are too large and the mark-count ceiling is too low. You'd spend the project fighting the runtime instead of building a game.

Vega the architectural idea (declarative reactive grammar over entity state, rendered by a GPU backend): genuinely promising, especially for a research-oriented RTS where you want the simulation state and visualization to be introspectable, debuggable, and compositional. This is adjacent to what Bevy's ECS + query system is reaching toward, and to your existing instincts about canonical schemas and dataflow compilation.

Want me to dig into any of these directions — the mark-count/perf numbers specifically, a sketch of what a Vega-style spec for RTS rendering would look like, or the comparison to Bevy/Godot ECS approaches?

```javascript
Simulation (Rust, fixed tick) → entity state snapshots
         ↓
  Canonical schema (your Envelope stage analog)
         ↓
  Vega-like declarative view spec → WebGL/WebGPU renderer
         ↓
  Interaction signals → command queue → Simulation
```

1. Tick rate and latency. Vega's dataflow runtime is optimized for dataset updates in the tens-to-hundreds of milliseconds range, not 30–60 Hz simulation ticks over thousands of entities. You'd be fighting the scheduler constantly.
2. No spatial indexing primitives. Pathfinding, collision, line-of-sight, and area-of-effect queries want quadtrees/grids/navmeshes. Vega transforms don't include these; you'd bolt them on as external JS and then Vega becomes a thin render layer, at which point why use it.
3. Mark count ceiling. SVG renderer falls over at a few thousand marks; Canvas renderer gets you to maybe 10k–50k depending on complexity. A modest RTS wants 200+ units with projectiles, particles, terrain tiles, fog tiles, UI — you're past the ceiling fast.
4. No audio, no networking, no asset pipeline. You'd be building all of these yourself.
5. Animation model mismatch. Vega animates via signal updates and transitions between states; RTS wants continuous interpolation driven by a fixed-timestep simulation. Doable but awkward.

## User

Q: Which Vega are you considering?
A: Vega visualization grammar (Vega/Vega-Lite)

---

## Assistant

Good — confirms the interesting case. Let me go deeper on the parts that matter, since the high-level sketch I gave is only useful if the numbers and the spec shape hold up.

The binding constraint is renderer throughput. Vega has two backends:

SVG renderer: each mark is a DOM node. Browsers choke somewhere between 1k–5k nodes with any interactivity. Dead on arrival for RTS.

Canvas renderer: marks are drawn imperatively per frame. Realistic ceiling is ~10k simple marks at 60fps on a decent machine, dropping fast as mark complexity (strokes, gradients, text) rises. Vega's own perf demos top out around this range.

For comparison, a modest RTS frame:

So even a small RTS is right at the Canvas ceiling, and that's before you add particles. The math says: Vega's stock renderers are viable for a tactics game (X-COM scale, ~20 units, turn-based or pausable) but not for Dune/Starcraft-scale RTS with hundreds of units and real-time combat.

The honest path for RTS scale is a WebGL/WebGPU renderer that accepts Vega specs but lowers marks to instanced draw calls. This exists in prototype form (vega-webgl was an experiment; never reached parity) but nothing production-grade. You'd be building it.

Here's the shape of the idea — this is pseudo-Vega, not a runnable spec, to show the grammar fit:

This is genuinely elegant for the RTS rendering layer. Selection state is a predicate on a signal. Health bars are derived encodings. Faction colors are a scale. Adding a debug layer — say, rendering the pathfinding cost field as a heatmap — is literally `{"type": "rect", "from": {"data": "pathfind_debug"}, ...}` and done. This is the kind of thing that in Unity takes an afternoon and in a Vega-shaped engine takes five minutes.

The simulation side — which is 80% of an RTS — is nowhere in this spec, and correctly so. Vega was never going to do pathfinding.

Option A: Actual Vega + small tactics game. Skip the fight. Build a turn-based or slow-paced tactics game (think Into the Breach or FTL-style) where entity counts stay under 100 and updates are event-driven rather than tick-driven. Vega holds up here. This is a real shipping path.

Option B: Vega as debug/observability layer, real engine does the game. Bevy/Godot/custom Rust runs the sim and renders. Vega runs in a side panel rendering everything you'd normally hide: influence maps, unit AI state machines, pathfind queries per frame, projected economy curves, the whole attack surface of the AI director. This is the boring correct answer and plays directly to your `cat_scanner` / telemetry-to-graph instincts. RTS research tools basically don't exist at this quality, and the Vega-over-game-telemetry pattern would be publishable.

Option C: Vega-grammar-inspired custom engine. You implement a Vega-like declarative renderer on top of WebGPU or wgpu, lowering mark specs to instanced draws and compute shaders for transforms. Simulation is ECS (Bevy or hand-rolled), and the presentation layer speaks a Vega dialect. This is the research-interesting path and aligns with your existing GPU compute shader and unified pipeline work, but it's a 1–2 year side project, not a weekend.

Option B is the one I'd actually push you toward, because it's undervalued and in your wheelhouse. A Bevy RTS with a live Vega dashboard showing per-tick influence maps, unit LOD distributions, eBPF-observed GPU kernel times for the AI, and catastrophe-germ-adjacent visualizations of decision boundaries for the game AI's value function — that's a genuinely novel tool, and the RTS is almost a pretext. The telemetry-to-graph compiler you already have is already most of the infrastructure for this.

Option C is the one that will actually tempt you, because it's where the interesting math lives. If you go that way, the thing to prototype first is not the renderer but the scale + transform layer over GPU buffers — can you express Vega's scale/encoding grammar as a compute shader compilation target? That's the load-bearing question and either answers yes-this-works or no-stop-now in about a week of spike work.

Which of these directions would you like to pull on — the Bevy+Vega telemetry architecture, the WebGPU compilation sketch for Option C, or the tactics-game scoping for Option A?

```json
{
  "data": [
    {"name": "units", "source": "sim://entities/units"},
    {"name": "projectiles", "source": "sim://entities/projectiles"},
    {"name": "selection", "source": "sim://ui/selected_ids"},
    {"name": "fog", "source": "sim://visibility/player_0"}
  ],
  "signals": [
    {"name": "camera_x", "value": 0, "on": [{"events": "drag", "update": "..."}]},
    {"name": "camera_zoom", "value": 1.0},
    {"name": "box_select", "on": [{"events": "mousedown, mousemove, mouseup", "update": "..."}]}
  ],
  "scales": [
    {"name": "world_to_screen_x", "type": "linear",
     "domain": {"signal": "[camera_x - viewport_w/camera_zoom, camera_x + viewport_w/camera_zoom]"},
     "range": "width"}
  ],
  "marks": [
    {"type": "image", "from": {"data": "fog"}, "encode": {...}},
    {"type": "symbol", "from": {"data": "units"},
     "encode": {
       "update": {
         "x": {"scale": "world_to_screen_x", "field": "pos.x"},
         "y": {"scale": "world_to_screen_y", "field": "pos.y"},
         "shape": {"field": "unit_type"},
         "fill": {"field": "faction", "scale": "faction_color"},
         "stroke": {"signal": "indata('selection', 'id', datum.id) ? 'yellow' : null"}
       }
     }},
    {"type": "rect", "from": {"data": "units"},
     "encode": {"update": {"width": {"signal": "datum.hp/datum.hp_max * 20"}, ...}}}
  ]
}
```

- 200 units × (body + selection ring + HP bar + shadow) = 800 marks
- ~50 projectiles in flight = 50 marks
- Terrain: if you naively mark-per-tile on a 256×256 map, 65k marks — dead. You'd need a pre-baked terrain layer outside Vega.
- Fog of war: same problem, needs to be a single image mark updated via texture, not per-tile marks.
- UI/HUD: 50–200 marks, fine.

## User

what about Vega-Fusion, consider an attack Nato symbol simplified RTS

---

## Assistant

Good pivot — VegaFusion changes the analysis meaningfully, and a NATO-symbol RTS is a much better fit for Vega's grammar than a sprite-based one. Let me dig in.

OK, the picture is much better now. Let me reframe.

VegaFusion is a three-part system — Planner, Runtime, Middleware — all written in Rust, compilable to native or WebAssembly arXiv, that in the 2.0 architecture implements Vega transforms directly against the DataFusion DataFrame API Vegafusion. The 1.0 architecture targeted the gap where the stock browser-side Vega runtime fails to scale past hundreds of thousands of rows Medium; 2.0 simplified the core substantially by leaning on modern DataFusion capabilities Vegafusion.

The critical insight for your question: VegaFusion moves the dataflow out of the browser and into an Arrow/DataFusion pipeline. That is exactly the layer an RTS simulation needs to be fast. Your entity state is already a table. Your transforms (visibility, LOD selection, selection predicates, fog-of-war derivation, unit-group aggregation for stacked symbols) are all SQL-shaped operations. DataFusion runs them at columnar speed.

This flips my earlier verdict on the simulation/presentation split. Before, Vega's dataflow was the bottleneck; with VegaFusion, the dataflow is the strength. The only remaining bottleneck is the final rendering step.

NATO joint military symbology (APP-6 / MIL-STD-2525) is almost pathologically well-suited to Vega-Lite, in ways that matter:

It's compositional. A symbol is a frame (affiliation: friend=blue rectangle, hostile=red diamond, neutral=green square, unknown=yellow quatrefoil), plus a main icon, plus modifiers, plus text fields (unit designation, strength, task). That composition is literally Vega-Lite's mark grammar. An APP-6 symbol is a layered mark spec over a row of entity data.

It's low mark count. A real-world RTS inspired by command-post displays doesn't have 400 units on screen — it has 30–80 symbols representing platoons, companies, battalions. Each symbol is ~5–15 sub-marks. You're at 500–1500 marks total, comfortably inside even stock Canvas Vega, and laughably easy for a VegaFusion-backed pipeline that pre-aggregates the entity tables.

It's already a data visualization. This is the key philosophical point — a military map display is a dataviz. Commanders are not looking at sprites; they're looking at a categorical/ordinal encoding of entity attributes (affiliation, echelon, function) onto position. That's Vega's native problem domain. You're not bending Vega to do something foreign; you're using it for what it was built for, applied to a domain that happens to be a game.

It dodges the animation problem. Military symbols don't walk-cycle. They translate, rotate (optionally), and change state. Smooth position interpolation between tick snapshots is all you need, and that's a straightforward encode update.

This is very good news for the architecture. Two key facts:

Here's what I'd actually propose, calibrated to your stack:

The load-bearing idea: entities carry a SIDC field, and rendering becomes a pure function of the entity table. A T-72 platoon isn't a sprite lookup; it's `sidc: "10031000141211020000"` plus a position. Changing affiliation from friendly to hostile after a capture event is one column update. Echelon aggregation on zoom-out is a DataFusion `GROUP BY` over a spatial bin that emits a higher-echelon SIDC. The rendering layer stays dumb.

This is where it gets design-interesting. When your visual primitive is a doctrinal symbol rather than a sprite, a whole set of RTS conventions become natural or impossible in instructive ways:

Natural wins:

Forced design choices:

The design space you're landing in is the one occupied by:

These games are commercially viable, have engaged audiences, and — critically — nobody has built one with a modern declarative-dataflow architecture. They're all bespoke C++ engines with ad-hoc data models. A VegaFusion-backed engine would be structurally more flexible than any of them for mods, scenario authoring, AAR/replay analysis, and — the part that should interest you — live observability of the AI.

Your `cat_scanner` instincts map onto this domain weirdly well. A symbol-based RTS is one where the state space is legible and low-dimensional per entity. The AI's decision surface over "should this battalion attack, defend, or withdraw" is genuinely a manifold you could visualize. You could run the AI's value function as a Vega layer — the fog-of-war map overlaid with the AI's perceived threat field, its planned axis of advance as a tactical graphic, confidence as opacity. The game is an observability platform for an adversarial AI, and the renderer is already a dataviz tool.

That's probably the paper, honestly.

If this were mine, week one would be:

If that spike works — which based on the numbers it should — you've proven the architecture. Everything from there is gameplay, not engine research.

The honest risk is that you'll fall in love with Option C from my previous reply (Vega-grammar-inspired WebGPU engine) and abandon the RTS to build an engine for nine months. NATO-symbol scope keeps you on the SVG/Canvas path where existing tools are already fast enough, which means you might actually ship a game. That's a feature, not a bug.

Want me to sketch the SIDC schema + Arrow record layout, or dig into the VegaFusion 2.0 API surface for how you'd actually wire the sim → dataflow → render loop?

```javascript
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Simulation Tick Loop (Rust, 10–30 Hz, fixed timestep)          │
│  - ECS: bevy_ecs or hecs                                        │
│  - Pathfinding (flow fields over coarse grid)                   │
│  - Combat resolution, visibility, fog                           │
│  - Emits: Arrow RecordBatch of entity snapshots per tick        │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                         │ (Arrow IPC, in-process)
┌────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  VegaFusion Runtime (Rust/DataFusion)                           │
│  - Task graph with cached nodes                                 │
│  - Visibility filter: JOIN entities with fog mask              │
│  - LOD aggregation: units → platoons → companies by zoom       │
│  - Selection predicate application                              │
│  - Outputs: rendered dataset per layer                          │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                         │ (entity table → symbol layer)
┌────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Symbol Renderer (milsymbol or native equivalent)               │
│  - SIDC → SVG/Canvas path per row                              │
│  - Position/rotation encoded via Vega scales                    │
│  - Map terrain as pre-rendered raster tile layer (not a mark)   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

1. milsymbol can generate 1000 symbols in under 20ms as SVG, using building blocks in code with no images or fonts GitHub — meaning symbols are procedurally composable and cheap. A 60fps budget is 16.6ms, so you could redraw an entire OOB's worth of symbols every frame if you had to. You don't have to; you only redraw on state change.
2. Nobori's C++ SDK can generate hundreds of thousands of vector symbols per second Milstd2525 — so the native-code path scales to anything a conceivable RTS would need.

- Fog of war is first-class. SIDCs include a "suspected/known/assumed" status modifier. Intel-quality-as-encoding isn't a bolt-on, it's part of the data model.
- Echelon rollup on zoom. Standard behavior in real C2 displays. Scroll out, platoons collapse into the company symbol at that centroid. This is an analytic aggregation, not an art problem.
- Formation arrows, boundaries, phase lines, objective markers. APP-6 has tactical graphics for all of these. The "draw commands" the player issues are themselves rendered as standard symbols, which is thematically perfect.
- Mixed-force realism. A mechanized infantry company with attached engineers is trivially representable — it's three rows with different SIDCs at nearby positions, not a new unit type that needs art.

- No "juice." No explosions, no hit flashes, no screen shake tied to visual gibs. Combat feedback has to be symbolic: the hostile symbol's strength modifier decrements, a casualty marker appears, the symbol switches to "reduced" status. This is either a feature (chess-like clarity, command-post feel) or a dealbreaker depending on what you're building.
- Terrain becomes a base map. You render it once as a tile layer (topo lines, elevation shading, roads) and symbols go on top. This is exactly how real map displays work and trivially implemented.
- Scale becomes abstract. Symbols don't have physical extent that varies with unit size; a platoon symbol is the same pixel size as a battalion symbol, distinguished by the echelon bar on top. You lose the "big unit stomping little unit" visual, gain clarity.

- Command: Modern Operations / Command: Professional Edition — the dominant simulation in its niche, literally uses NATO symbols on a 2D map.
- Armored Brigade — operational-level tactical wargame, pure symbol rendering, cult following.
- Flashpoint Campaigns — same family.
- The whole professional wargaming and staff-training tool category (VR-Forces, JCATS, etc.).

1. Rust binary. bevy_ecs for entities with Position, Velocity, SIDC, Hp, Faction.
2. Dumb fixed-tick sim: 50 units moving toward clicked waypoints, no combat.
3. Export tick state as Arrow RecordBatch.
4. Frontend: web (Tauri or plain browser), VegaFusion 2.0 running the dataflow, milsymbol rendering each row as a symbol at its position.
5. Pan/zoom/box-select via Vega signals. Selected units get tinted. Issue move orders by right-click → signal → command queue → sim.

```javascript
│  - Visibility filter: JOIN entities with fog mask              │
│  - LOD aggregation: units → platoons → companies by zoom       │
│  - Selection predicate application                              │
│  - Outputs: rendered dataset per layer                          │
└────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘
                         │ (entity table → symbol layer)
┌────────────────────────▼────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Symbol Renderer (milsymbol or native equivalent)               │
│  - SIDC → SVG/Canvas path per row                              │
│  - Position/rotation encoded via Vega scales                    │
│  - Map terrain as pre-rendered raster tile layer (not a mark)   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

1. milsymbol can generate 1000 symbols in under 20ms as SVG, using building blocks in code with no images or fonts GitHub — meaning symbols are procedurally composable and cheap. A 60fps budget is 16.6ms, so you could redraw an entire OOB's worth of symbols every frame if you had to. You don't have to; you only redraw on state change.
2. Nobori's C++ SDK can generate hundreds of thousands of vector symbols per second Milstd2525 — so the native-code path scales to anything a conceivable RTS would need.

- Fog of war is first-class. SIDCs include a "suspected/known/assumed" status modifier. Intel-quality-as-encoding isn't a bolt-on, it's part of the data model.
- Echelon rollup on zoom. Standard behavior in real C2 displays. Scroll out, platoons collapse into the company symbol at that centroid. This is an analytic aggregation, not an art problem.
- Formation arrows, boundaries, phase lines, objective markers. APP-6 has tactical graphics for all of these. The "draw commands" the player issues are themselves rendered as standard symbols, which is thematically perfect.
- Mixed-force realism. A mechanized infantry company with attached engineers is trivially representable — it's three rows with different SIDCs at nearby positions, not a new unit type that needs art.

- No "juice." No explosions, no hit flashes, no screen shake tied to visual gibs. Combat feedback has to be symbolic: the hostile symbol's strength modifier decrements, a casualty marker appears, the symbol switches to "reduced" status. This is either a feature (chess-like clarity, command-post feel) or a dealbreaker depending on what you're building.
- Terrain becomes a base map. You render it once as a tile layer (topo lines, elevation shading, roads) and symbols go on top. This is exactly how real map displays work and trivially implemented.
- Scale becomes abstract. Symbols don't have physical extent that varies with unit size; a platoon symbol is the same pixel size as a battalion symbol, distinguished by the echelon bar on top. You lose the "big unit stomping little unit" visual, gain clarity.

- Command: Modern Operations / Command: Professional Edition — the dominant simulation in its niche, literally uses NATO symbols on a 2D map.
- Armored Brigade — operational-level tactical wargame, pure symbol rendering, cult following.
- Flashpoint Campaigns — same family.
- The whole professional wargaming and staff-training tool category (VR-Forces, JCATS, etc.).

1. Rust binary. bevy_ecs for entities with Position, Velocity, SIDC, Hp, Faction.
2. Dumb fixed-tick sim: 50 units moving toward clicked waypoints, no combat.
3. Export tick state as Arrow RecordBatch.
4. Frontend: web (Tauri or plain browser), VegaFusion 2.0 running the dataflow, milsymbol rendering each row as a symbol at its position.
5. Pan/zoom/box-select via Vega signals. Selected units get tinted. Issue move orders by right-click → signal → command queue → sim.

---

## User

I want this RTS engine to have two modes, one based on being able to click and interact in 2D, the other an extension to put the objects in 3D in VR later. The idea is to make the RTS control agent workflows

---

## Assistant

Now the architecture question gets genuinely interesting, because you're not really designing an RTS — you're designing a command-and-control surface for agents, with two presentation modalities over a shared state. The RTS affordances (select, group, assign orders, observe, override) are the UX primitives for orchestrating autonomous workers. That reframe changes what matters.

Let me work through it.

You're building a sensemaking and tasking interface over a fleet of agents, where:

The NATO symbol choice is more justified here than for a conventional RTS, because doctrinal symbology is specifically designed to communicate categorical state at a glance under uncertainty — which is exactly the information density problem of watching 50 agents work.

These aren't two renderers over the same data. They're two fundamentally different epistemic stances the operator takes:

2D mode is the command post. High information density, everything on one screen, fast categorical scanning, precise clicks, keyboard shortcuts, multi-select box drags. This is where you decide. The operator is external to the workspace, looking down at it. This maps directly to the Vega/VegaFusion/milsymbol stack from the previous turn.

3D/VR mode is the walkthrough. Embodied inspection, spatial memory, gesture-based selection, conversational prompting ("tell me about this one"). Lower information density but higher presence and context. The operator is inside the workspace. You use this when you need to understand why an agent is doing what it's doing, or when the semantic space has structure that benefits from embodied navigation.

The pattern here has a real precedent: military C2, air traffic control, and increasingly SOC (security ops center) dashboards all oscillate between "god view" and "inspection view." The insight most people miss is that they're not the same interaction mode with different graphics — they're different cognitive tools that share a state backend.

This is the critical architectural commitment:

The non-negotiable invariant: both renderers read the same Arrow table and emit the same command schema. If you get this right, 3D/VR is not an "extension" — it's a second view that costs you a projection function and a renderer, nothing more. If you get it wrong (by letting 2D-specific concepts leak into the world state), retrofitting 3D later will be painful in exactly the ways retrofitting usually is.

This is the interesting design question and I'd push back on leaving it implicit. Real military units have real coordinates. Your agents have... what?

A few options, pick deliberately:

Option 1: Domain-native spatial coordinates. If agents operate in a domain that has natural geometry — drones in physical space, game NPCs in a game world, crawlers over a known web graph — use those. 3D mode is literally the physical/virtual space. This is the easiest case; the 2D view is a top-down projection.

Option 2: Semantic embedding projected to low-D. Agents' current task/state gets embedded, UMAP/PCA to 2D or 3D, that's their position. Clusters = agents doing similar things. Movement = state drift. This is the interesting case, connects to your manifold/information-geometry interests, and makes the 2D/3D duality genuinely meaningful — 3D preserves more structure, 2D is a lossy projection that might be more legible for command decisions.

Option 3: Designed conceptual space. You define axes with meaning. E.g., x = exploration↔exploitation, y = confidence, z = resource cost. Agents live where their current behavior puts them. Most legible; requires the most design thought; probably best for a working tool.

Option 4: Hybrid. Physical/logical position when it exists, semantic when it doesn't, overlays to toggle between them. This is what the mature version looks like and you probably shouldn't start here.

My instinct for your interests: Option 3 for the shipping version, Option 2 as the research layer on top. Option 3 gives operators a stable mental model; Option 2 lets you do the manifold-of-agent-behavior visualizations that are publishable.

The NATO symbol system's genius is that a SIDC compresses many categorical dimensions into one code. Steal the pattern, redesign the taxonomy for agents:

Every one of these is already a first-class APP-6 concept with rendering support in milsymbol. You're not inventing a visual language, you're reusing one with 70 years of human-factors iteration behind it.

The commands an operator issues must work in both renderers. This means defining them abstractly:

Each is a command in the shared schema. The renderers are bindings from input events to commands. If you define the command set first and the renderers second, both modes stay honest.

The mistake people make with VR-for-ops-dashboards is trying to recreate the 2D UI in 3D space — floating panels, menus on controllers, etc. It's worse than the 2D version at everything.

The VR mode earns its existence only if it offers something 2D structurally can't:

None of these require high polycount or physics. Symbols-as-billboards in a spatial scatter with good typography and trails will outperform a "realistic" visualization for this use case.

Given your Rust infrastructure bias and GPU/HPC comfort:

Resist the VR temptation for at least 3–4 months. The 2D mode is where you'll discover what the command semantics actually need to be. Every hour spent polishing VR before the command vocabulary is stable is an hour that gets reworked. Stage it:

Phase 1 (weeks 1–4): Dead-simple 2D with fake agents. 20 scripted "agents" doing random walks in a designed 2D semantic space, milsymbol rendering, VegaFusion dataflow, click-select and issue-order loop working end-to-end. No real LLM agents yet.

Phase 2 (weeks 5–10): Real agent integration. Ingest telemetry from actual LLM agent processes. Define the SIDC-analog taxonomy. Build the inspection panel (agent reasoning trace, tool call log). This is where you find out whether your categorical schema actually captures what operators need to see.

Phase 3 (weeks 11–16): Command vocabulary maturation. What orders actually help? Playtest with real agent workloads. Iterate the command set. Replay/AAR tooling.

Phase 4 (months 5+): 3D/VR extension. Now that the state and commands are stable, the VR renderer is a focused project rather than a research question entangled with everything else.

It sits at the junction of several things you already do: Arrow/DataFusion dataflow, Rust infra, GPU-accelerated rendering, research-quality observability, and the `cat_scanner` instinct that decision boundaries and agent state manifolds are the right objects to visualize. The 2D mode is a shipping product, the 3D mode is a research instrument, and the shared state layer is a reusable piece of infrastructure. It also avoids the trap of the pure RTS (which is a big content/art problem) by making the "game content" be the agents themselves, which you're going to have anyway.

I'd want to know more before committing hard: what specifically are the agents doing, and do they actually expose interrupt/retask affordances? The answer determines whether this is an observability tool with RTS styling, or a genuine control surface.

Want to drill into the SIDC-analog taxonomy for agents, the command schema design, or the semantic-position projection question?

```javascript
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Agent Fleet (external to engine)                         │
│  - Long-running agent processes                           │
│  - Emit telemetry: state, actions, intent, reasoning      │
│  - Consume orders: goals, constraints, interruptions      │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────┬─────────────────┘
               │ telemetry                │ orders
┌──────────────▼──────────────────────────┴─────────────────┐
│  World State Service (Rust, authoritative)                │
│  - Arrow-native entity table                              │
│  - Position in semantic space (+ physical if applicable)  │
│  - SIDC-equivalent categorical state                      │
│  - Tick cadence decoupled from agents (10–30 Hz render)   │
│  - Event log (append-only, replayable)                    │
└──────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────────┘
               │ Arrow RecordBatch stream (per tick)
       ┌───────┴─────────┐
       │                 │
┌──────▼──────┐   ┌──────▼──────────────────────────────────┐
│ VegaFusion  │   │ 3D/VR State Projection                  │
│ Dataflow    │   │ - Same entity table                     │
│ (2D path)   │   │ - Position + embedding → 3D coords      │
│             │   │ - Symbol → 3D billboard or mesh         │
└──────┬──────┘   └──────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
       │                 │
┌──────▼──────┐   ┌──────▼──────────────────────────────────┐
│ milsymbol   │   │ wgpu/Bevy + OpenXR                      │
│ SVG/Canvas  │   │ - Symbol textures as billboards         │
│ (web/Tauri) │   │ - Spatial UI for orders                 │
└─────────────┘   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │                 │
       └────────┬────────┘
                │ orders (both renderers emit via same command schema)
┌───────────────▼───────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Command Queue → Agent Fleet                              │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- Agents = autonomous processes (LLM-driven, scripted, hybrid) with state, goals, and capabilities
- "Units" on the map = agents, positioned in some semantic space projected to 2D/3D
- Orders = task assignments, constraints, boundaries, priorities
- Fog of war = epistemic uncertainty about what agents know / are doing
- Combat = adversarial interaction (red team agents, environmental adversaries, or just task contention)

- Affiliation → whose agent (operator's, third-party, adversarial, unknown)
- Echelon → scope (single tool call, task, multi-step plan, long-running program)
- Function icon → agent role (retrieval, synthesis, execution, monitor, critic, planner)
- Status modifier → health/state (running, blocked, waiting-for-approval, errored, completed)
- Strength modifier → confidence or progress
- Task force indicator → part of a team/swarm
- Movement arrow → planned next action vector
- Intel quality → how stale the telemetry is

- select(predicate) — rectangle in 2D, gaze+pinch or gesture-lasso in VR
- assign(selection, goal) — right-click menu in 2D, spatial menu or voice in VR
- inspect(entity) — click-to-panel in 2D, walk-up-and-look in VR
- constrain(selection, rule) — modal dialog in 2D, grabbed-handles affordance in VR
- group(selection, name) — hotkey in 2D, gesture in VR
- interrupt(entity_or_selection) — button in 2D, grab-and-hold in VR

1. Scale. Stand in a scene with 500 agents arranged by semantic similarity and you perceive cluster structure that 2D scatterplots flatten away. This is the legitimate win and it's real.
2. Co-presence. Multi-operator VR lets a team stand in the same operational picture and point at things. This is genuinely useful for incident response and AARs. Hard to do well; 2D can't really do it at all.
3. Embodied history. Scrub time and see the agent trajectories as traces in 3D space. Movement-as-persistence is a VR-native affordance.
4. Proximal inspection. Walk up to an agent, see its context window / reasoning trace as environmental text around it, step back out. Locality-of-attention is spatial.

- Shared state & dataflow: Rust, Arrow, VegaFusion 2.0, event-sourced log (lakeFS or a simple append-only parquet store fits your existing interests)
- Agent transport: whatever your fleet uses; the ingest adapter is boring plumbing
- 2D renderer: Web (React + Vega-Lite + milsymbol) served by a Rust backend, or Tauri if you want native. Web is probably right because it's also your eventual multi-operator path.
- 3D/VR renderer: Bevy + bevy_openxr. Bevy is Rust, already ECS-native, works with the same Arrow schemas you already have, and OpenXR support is maturing. Unity is the alternative if Bevy's VR story isn't mature enough when you get there — but the non-Rust boundary is a cost.
- Symbol assets in 3D: render milsymbol SIDCs to textures at multiple resolutions, billboard them. Don't try to make 3D NATO meshes; the symbols are designed for flat rendering.
- Command schema: protobuf or Arrow-flight RPC, generated into both Rust and TypeScript.

1. You reinvent Langfuse/Arize/LangSmith with a map metaphor. Agent observability tools already exist. The RTS framing has to pay for itself in actionability — if operators aren't issuing orders mid-run, the map is just a prettier dashboard. Be clear early about what commands mean and whether agents actually respond to them.
2. Semantic position drifts faster than operators can track. If embeddings update every tick, units jitter and the spatial metaphor breaks. You'll need temporal smoothing, or a commitment to "position changes slowly; status changes fast, and status is where the action is." Probably the latter.
3. The 3D mode never justifies itself. If 2D answers 95% of operator questions, VR becomes a demo feature. Be intellectually honest about whether the VR mode earns the build time; if it doesn't in Phase 1–3, don't build it just because the plan said so.
4. Agents ignore orders. If the underlying agent framework doesn't expose real interrupt/redirect/constrain affordances, the RTS interactions become theatrical. The engine is downstream of the agents' actual controllability. Worth auditing that before committing.

## User

No fog of war, agent/llm integration mode, Files and services as terrain/objects, 3D space for user comfort, projection from 2D

---

## Assistant

Good constraints — these actually simplify the architecture a lot, and the reframe is sharper than you might realize. Let me work through what you're actually building now.

You're building a spatial IDE for agent orchestration, where:

That last constraint is the big one and I want to push on it before designing around it.

This is a different and better architectural choice than "two renderers over shared state." You're saying the 2D layout is authoritative, and 3D is a view transform of it. That means:

This dodges a huge pile of sync problems. It also means the 3D mode's job is explicitly operator comfort during monitoring, not interactive authoring. That's a clarifying constraint — VR is for the "sit back and watch the agents work" phase, not the "set up the workflow" phase.

The projection function becomes a design surface worth being deliberate about:

where `f` could be:

Pick one for v1, make it togglable later. The boring-correct answer is activity — it's immediately legible ("tall thing = something happening there"), and it gives VR the motion it needs to not feel static.

This is where the design gets concrete. In a conventional RTS, terrain is a heightmap with properties (passable, cover, resource). Your terrain is a filesystem + service topology, which has real structure you should preserve rather than flatten.

Files as terrain. The filesystem is a tree. A natural layout:

A repo visualized this way has the feel of a city: dense downtown core (src/), suburban sprawl (tests/), industrial district (build artifacts), warehouses (vendor/). That metaphor is load-bearing for operator intuition and it's real, not just cute.

Services as terrain. Services are nodes in a graph with call edges. They want a graph layout (force-directed is the default, hierarchical if there's a clear tier structure). Each service is a structure — a building, a platform, a facility — not a region. Edges between services are roads/pipes/links.

Combined map. Files live in one area of the map, services in another, with gateways where they interact (code that calls a service, logs that record service activity). Or services overlay files geographically (the service "lives in" the directory containing its code). The right choice depends on what operators look at together; I'd guess overlay, because agents that modify service code and agents that call the service are related operators-care-about-both cases.

Critical: this layout is computed once and cached, then updated incrementally. It's not a per-tick calculation. Agents move; terrain mostly doesn't.

An agent's "position" is now where it's working:

This gives movement real semantic meaning. "The agent moved from tokens.rs to the auth service" means something. Trails over time become traces of work — a team of agents working on a refactor leaves visible paths across the codebase.

The SIDC-analog for agents (from last turn) still applies — role, status, confidence, etc. — just layered onto this new movement model.

Files and services aren't inert — agents modify them, and that has to be visible.

File state:

Each is a visual modifier on the file's terrain feature. In SIDC terms, you already have status modifiers; apply them to terrain, not just units. "This file is being edited by agent X and its tests are failing" is a glanceable combined state.

Service state:

Same pattern — glanceable categorical encoding.

Change events: when an agent writes to a file, there's a brief visual event at that location (a flash, a ripple, a spawned marker that fades). This is the equivalent of combat feedback in a conventional RTS — the thing that tells you "something just happened here" from across the map.

Dropping fog of war changes what commands do. You're not dispatching scouts; you're dispatching workers. The command vocabulary becomes:

Selection in 2D is rectangle or click. Selection in 3D (if you do interact there) is gaze+pinch or controller-ray. Crucially: if 3D is projection-of-2D monitoring, you may want 3D interaction to be inspection-only — you can look, zoom, scrub time, but authoring commands always happens in 2D. This keeps the VR mode simple and the command semantics unambiguous.

Two changes worth noting from last turn:

The integration surface needs to do two things: ingest what agents are doing, and dispatch commands to them. The shape depends on your agent framework but the abstractions to target:

Ingest needs:

Dispatch needs:

The adapter layer between your engine and the agent framework is the integration's load-bearing piece. If the framework doesn't natively support mid-run constraint injection and interrupt, the RTS becomes a monitoring tool, not a control surface — which is fine, just name it correctly.

Good agent frameworks to target first:

I'd start with LangGraph or a home-rolled loop for the integration spike, because they give you the introspection and control affordances the engine assumes.

Given "3D space for user comfort," the design brief for VR becomes specific:

This is not a "do your work in VR" product. It's a "watch your fleet work, feel what's happening, notice when something's off" product. That's an honest and achievable VR story, and it's one most agent-observability tools don't offer.

Two weeks, scoped to the simplest thing that tests the core bet:

If watching this feels informative — if you can tell at a glance what's happening — the core bet is validated. If it feels chaotic or uninformative, the layout/encoding needs work before anything else gets built.

Want to go deeper on layout stability, the agent framework integration specifically, or the projection function design for 3D?

```javascript
(x, y) in 2D  →  (x, y, f(entity_state)) in 3D
```

```javascript
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Real world (the things being commanded)                     │
│  - Filesystem (watched: fs events, git state, test status)   │
│  - Services (health/metrics/traces)                          │
│  - Agents (telemetry: activity, reasoning, tool calls)       │
└───────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                │ ingest adapters
┌───────────────▼──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  World State (Rust, Arrow-native)                            │
│  - Terrain table: files, services, status                    │
│  - Layout cache: (entity_id → 2D coord)                      │
│  - Agent table: agents + current position/state              │
│  - Event log: append-only history of all state changes       │
└───────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                │ Arrow RecordBatch stream
┌───────────────▼──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  VegaFusion Dataflow                                         │
│  - Terrain-to-marks transforms                               │
│  - Agent position + encoding                                 │
│  - Selection/highlight predicates                            │
│  - LOD aggregation (zoomed out: file → directory → repo)    │
└───────┬────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
        │                                │
┌───────▼────────────┐         ┌─────────▼──────────────────┐
│  2D Renderer       │         │  3D Projection + Renderer  │
│  - milsymbol       │  ────►  │  - Same (x,y), +z=f(state) │
│  - Web/Canvas      │ projects│  - Bevy + bevy_openxr      │
│  - Authoring UI    │  from   │  - Symbol billboards       │
│  - Command input   │         │  - Inspection-only UI      │
└───────┬────────────┘         └─────────┬──────────────────┘
        │                                │
        └──────────┬─────────────────────┘
                   │ commands
┌──────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Command Dispatcher → Agent Runtime                          │
│  - Translates ops commands into agent framework directives   │
│  - Writes to event log                                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- The operator is a human commander, fully informed (no fog of war — full observability is the point, you want to see everything agents are touching)
- Agents are the units being commanded
- Files and services are the terrain and objectives — the things agents move across, occupy, modify, and contest
- 2D is the authoring/planning surface (precise, dense, keyboard-driven)
- 3D is the viewing/monitoring surface (comfortable, embodied, for long sessions)
- The 3D view is a projection of the 2D layout, not an independent scene

- Operators lay things out in 2D. 3D respects that layout.
- Z-axis in 3D carries information that's auxiliary or derived (height = activity level? depth = hierarchy? elevation = staleness?), not a free design dimension.
- You never have to solve the "how does a 3D-placed object appear in 2D" problem, because the arrow only goes one direction.
- VR becomes a comfortable way to watch a 2D plan execute, not a separate workspace.

- Activity/energy: busy agents rise up, idle agents sit on the floor. Glanceable progress from any angle.
- Hierarchy depth: supervisor agents float above workers. Org chart becomes literal.
- Recency: recently-modified files elevated, cold files low. Temporal terrain.
- Resource cost: expensive operations tall, cheap ones flat. Cost pressure visible.
- Semantic clustering delta: how far an entity has drifted from its starting cluster.

- Directories become regions (Voronoi cells, polygons, or nested rectangles — pick based on density). Nested directories = nested regions.
- Files become features within regions: small rectangles, sized by file size (log-scaled) or importance, colored by type.
- File proximity within a region reflects lexical adjacency; directory proximity in the world reflects... whatever metric you pick (shared imports? co-edit history? just alphabetic?).

- Agent reading src/auth/tokens.rs → positioned at that file's location
- Agent calling the payments service → positioned at the service node (or in motion between its origin and the service)
- Agent running a multi-step plan → trail showing files/services touched in sequence
- Agent with no current activity → at a waiting area, or at its home base

- Unchanged, modified (uncommitted), just-committed, deleted, newly-created
- Lock state: being-edited-by-agent, being-read, idle
- Test/build status: passing, failing, stale

- Healthy, degraded, down
- Under load (agents actively calling), idle
- Deployment status (current version, deploying, rolled back)

- Assign: "this agent, work on these files" or "this agent, use this service"
- Constrain: "don't touch anything outside this region," "read-only for this directory," "no writes to services"
- Prioritize: "finish this task before that one," "drop everything and handle X"
- Inspect: show me this agent's reasoning, show me what's been done to this file
- Group/coordinate: "these three agents work together," "wait for agent A to finish before starting"
- Interrupt/redirect: "stop what you're doing," "new instructions"
- Replay: scrub time to see how we got here

1. Layout cache is now a first-class thing. Terrain positions are expensive to compute (force-directed graph layouts aren't free) and stable, so they belong in a cache that updates on filesystem/service topology changes, not per tick.
2. 3D is explicitly a view transform, not a parallel renderer. It reads the same Arrow data + the same 2D layout + applies a projection. This is the projection-from-2D commitment made concrete.

- Agent identity (stable ID across runs)
- Current action (tool call, with args) → maps to position on terrain
- Reasoning trace (for inspection panel)
- Status transitions (started, blocked-on-approval, completed, errored)
- Resource consumption (tokens, time, cost) → feeds into encoding

- Interrupt signal
- Goal/task assignment
- Constraint injection (scope restrictions)
- Priority change
- Approval responses (for human-in-the-loop gates)

- Claude Code — has a tool-call stream you can tap, though command-injection mid-run is limited
- LangGraph — stateful graph model, supports interrupts and human-in-the-loop natively, probably the best fit
- Home-rolled agent loops — easiest to integrate with because you control the loop
- CrewAI / AutoGen — possible but their internals are less introspectable

- Long sessions (hours watching agents work) — eye/neck comfort matters more than interaction density
- Spatial audio for events happening off-screen (agent completed a task somewhere → directional chime)
- Ambient motion from the activity-as-height projection gives peripheral awareness without needing to stare
- Scrub time with a physical slider/gesture feels better than a UI element
- No menu-wrangling in VR; any command you need issues from a companion 2D display (desktop monitor, tablet, headset passthrough)

1. Layout instability. If the filesystem changes (branch switch, big refactor), the whole map shifts, and operators lose their mental map. You need layout stability across small changes — new files appear near related ones, deletions leave ghosts briefly, big reorganizations animate rather than jump. This is a real technical problem (incremental graph layout) and it's the thing most likely to make v1 feel wrong.
2. Scale of real codebases. A big monorepo is 100k+ files. You cannot render 100k marks meaningfully at any zoom. LOD aggregation (directory-level at zoomed-out, file-level only when zoomed in) is mandatory from day one, not a v2 feature. VegaFusion's aggregation is good at this; design the schema so rollup is cheap.
3. Services telemetry is a firehose. Metrics/traces at high rate will swamp a visualization if rendered raw. You'll need pre-aggregated summaries (rolling windows, percentile reservoirs), not raw event streams. This is also VegaFusion's strength; don't render raw events, render aggregations.
4. Agent position is a lie if tool calls are fast. If an agent makes 10 tool calls per second across 10 different files, its "position" is essentially teleportation. You need either trail rendering (show recent path, not just current point) or dwell-weighted positioning (weighted average of recent activity, so agents that bounce look spread out and agents that focus look localized). Probably both.
5. The filesystem/service split pulls toward two different games. You may find that the "agents editing code" use case and the "agents operating services" use case have different enough operator needs that they want different layouts. Be willing to have two terrain types with a gateway, rather than forcing unification.

1. Rust service that watches a single git repo's filesystem, emits a flat Arrow table of files with size/mtime/status.
2. Force-directed layout of directories into a 2D plane, files as children within directory cells.
3. A few scripted "agents" — just processes that read/write random files — emitting telemetry.
4. Web frontend: Vega-Lite rendering directories as regions, files as rects, agents as SIDC-style symbols with current-file positioning.
5. Click an agent → show its recent trail and current activity.
6. No 3D yet. Prove the 2D terrain/agent metaphor works on a real codebase before projecting.

- What's the agent framework? (Changes the integration adapter substantially.)
- What's the scale — how many agents, how many files/services? (Changes the rendering strategy.)
- Is this a single-operator tool or multi-operator? (Multi-op means shared state service from day one; single-op lets you defer.)
- Do operators need to author agent workflows here, or just monitor and intervene? (Authoring means you also need a DAG-editor surface, which is a different product.)

## User

Interaction is a per agent graph between objects

## Assistant

Now the model is clean and I can drop several layers of previous assumption. Let me re-anchor.

Each agent has its own graph. Nodes are objects (files, services, whatever the agent interacts with). Edges are interactions (read, write, call, spawn, depend-on, whatever the ontology needs). The agent's activity is its graph growing, mutating, pruning over time.

This changes almost everything:

That's a much sharper abstraction than "agents on a map." It's closer to: agents are authors of a shared hypergraph, and the RTS is a tool for watching and steering that authorship.

The previous position-based model had a problem I didn't fully name: agents jumping between files at tool-call speed made "position" incoherent. A graph model dissolves the problem — there's no jumping, just edge accumulation. Each interaction is an event on an edge, which you can render as a pulse or a persistent connection, and the agent's "location" is always well-defined as its set of recent edges.

It also matches what agent telemetry actually looks like. Agents emit events of the form (agent, verb, object, metadata) — which is an edge. You're not synthesizing position from edges, you're rendering edges directly. That's one less lossy abstraction layer.

And it inherits real theory. Multiplex networks, temporal graphs, hypergraphs — all have well-developed visualization literatures. You're not inventing a visual grammar from scratch; you're specializing a known one to an ops tool.

The rendering problem is now temporal multiplex graph visualization with categorical node types, categorical edge types, and a live update stream. Let me be concrete about the layers:

Nodes (objects + agents):

Edges (interactions):

Temporal layer:

Per-agent subgraph highlight:

Object nodes want a stable layout because operators build mental maps. The layout options reorder by relevance:

Option 3 (layered) is worth considering seriously for the primary 2D mode because your phrase "per agent graph between objects" is exactly a bipartite-ish structure (agents ⟷ objects) with edges as the thing of interest. Bipartite layouts make that structure legible in a way general force-directed graphs don't.

Honest recommendation for v1: hierarchical for objects (filesystem-natural), agents anchored in a band above or beside, edges drawn between. This gives you (a) stable terrain, (b) clear agent identity, (c) edges as the visual focus, which is the right emphasis.

With a graph model, the projection gets more meaningful:

This is one of the cases where 3D earns its keep: edge-dense graphs are hard in 2D (hairball problem), and 3D with embodied camera motion genuinely helps disambiguate. Your "3D for comfort" brief turns out to also be a visualization win here.

Because every agent has its own graph, the system has two useful views:

The per-agent view is the killer feature, and it's where your existing instincts about graph representation align. The agent's graph is the agent's trace. Every tool call is an edge. A multi-step plan is a subgraph with temporal ordering. The graph grows as the agent works. Replaying an agent's session is replaying its graph construction.

This is also where you could do the actually-novel thing: graph-level agent analytics. Cycle detection (agent stuck reading/writing the same files), drift detection (agent's recent edges diverge from its plan), efficiency (short path to goal vs wandering). These are graph invariants, cheap to compute, and they give operators glanceable signals. Some of this connects to your information-geometry interests — an agent's graph evolution traces a curve through a graph-metric space, and deviation from "healthy" trajectories is detectable.

The critical observation: your data is already in the shape VegaFusion wants. Nodes table, edges table, Arrow-native, all transforms are SQL-shaped. This is almost suspiciously good fit — it's the domain VegaFusion/DataFusion was built for, just specialized to ops.

With graph semantics, commands become graph operations:

"Scope" as a graph constraint rather than a spatial one is a meaningful improvement — you can say "only touch these files" which maps naturally to "edges only allowed to this node set," whereas spatial constraints needed a layout-dependent definition.

Given "per-agent graph between objects," the 2-week spike becomes:

No commands yet, no agent framework integration yet, no 3D yet. The question this spike answers: does watching the live edge stream over this layout actually tell you what's happening? If yes, the architecture bet is validated and everything else is building on it. If the graph is too noisy/confusing to parse at a glance, the problem is visualization and you need to iterate on layout/aggregation/encoding before anything else.

If I step back, the project is less "RTS for agents" and more a live graph-state IDE for agent fleets. The RTS framing is useful for UX metaphors (selection, commands, map-view, symbols), but the underlying object is a temporal multiplex graph visualization with command affordances. That's a clearer product description, and it has a small but real prior art footprint (graph debuggers, provenance viewers, distributed tracing UIs like Jaeger) — none of which are designed for steering a fleet, just observing one. The novelty is the command surface over the graph.

Your interests align well: you already think in graphs (telemetry-to-graph compiler), you work in Arrow/DataFusion shape, you care about observability, you have instincts about graph-metric-space dynamics from `cat_scanner`. This project is recognizably yours.

Three things worth deciding before going further:

Want to work through any of those, or go deeper on the layout stability problem, or sketch the edge ontology?

```javascript
Agent telemetry:  (agent, verb, object, timestamp, metadata)
                   │
                   ▼
          ┌──────────────────────┐
          │  Event log (append)  │  ← source of truth
          └──────────┬───────────┘
                     │
                     ▼
          ┌──────────────────────┐
          │  Graph state (Rust)  │
          │  - Nodes: objects,   │
          │    agents            │
          │  - Edges: typed,     │
          │    timestamped,      │
          │    agent-attributed  │
          │  - Arrow tables:     │
          │    nodes, edges      │
          └──────────┬───────────┘
                     │
                     ▼
          ┌──────────────────────┐
          │  VegaFusion dataflow │
          │  - Edge aging/decay  │
          │  - Per-agent filters │
          │  - Layout cache      │
          │  - LOD aggregation   │
          │    (edge bundling)   │
          └──────┬────────┬──────┘
                 │        │
           2D ◄──┘        └──► 3D (z = derived, edges as arcs)
```

- Agents aren't points in space. An agent is a subgraph — a current locus plus the history of what it's touched and how.
- "Position" dissolves. There's no single x,y for an agent. There are many: the nodes it's connected to, with edges of varying recency and weight.
- The world state is a multiplex graph. Objects are shared nodes; each agent contributes its own edge set; the union is the full picture; per-agent views filter to one edge color.
- Terrain and units are the same kind of thing. Files/services are nodes. Agents are nodes. The distinction is role, not representation. This is structurally simpler than the previous model.

- Object nodes: files, directories, services, APIs, data stores. Positioned by terrain layout (force-directed or structural, stable).
- Agent nodes: can either be positioned separately (e.g., along a margin or in a "bullpen" region) or embedded in the object graph near their center-of-activity. The second is more information-dense; the first is more legible. I'd start with the first.
- Node rendering: NATO-symbol-style glyphs still apply. Object SIDCs encode file type/status; agent SIDCs encode role/status.

- Colored/styled per type (read, write, call, spawn, depend).
- Weighted by recency — recent edges bright and thick, older edges faded and thin, stale edges pruned.
- Bundled when many edges flow between the same region (edge bundling prevents the hairball). This is a known technique with good Vega/D3 implementations.
- Per-agent color/channel so you can see whose edges are whose.

- Edge events are the pulse of the system. When agent A reads file F, a transient pulse travels along the A→F edge. Watching the display means seeing which edges are pulsing.
- Persistent edges (current reads/writes held open) rendered differently from completed transactions.
- Time scrubber shows edge state at any historical moment.

- Select an agent → its edges brighten, others fade. This is the fundamental "show me what this agent is doing" affordance, and it falls out of the graph model naturally (a filter predicate on edge color, trivial in VegaFusion).

1. Hierarchical for the filesystem — nested treemap or indented tree or circle-pack. Respects real structure.
2. Force-directed for services — graph topology emerges naturally.
3. Layered by role — objects on the bottom, agents along the top, edges connecting down. Sankey/flow-diagram feel. This is actually a strong candidate because it makes "which agents are touching what" the primary visual.
4. Matrix view — agents as rows, objects as columns, cells as edge presence/intensity. Dense, categorical, works well for small-to-medium scale, chess-puzzle clarity. Probably a secondary view, not primary.

- 2D layout gives x,y for every node.
- Z axis in 3D carries a derived dimension per node type:

For objects: activity level (how many agents touching it), or staleness, or risk (failing tests, outdated service).
For agents: current engagement depth (short-chain vs deep plan), or cost consumed, or time since last action.
- For objects: activity level (how many agents touching it), or staleness, or risk (failing tests, outdated service).
- For agents: current engagement depth (short-chain vs deep plan), or cost consumed, or time since last action.
- Edges in 3D become arcs/tubes rising through space, which is actually a known visualization pattern (arc diagrams lifted into 3D) and genuinely legible in VR where you can move around to disambiguate occlusion — 2D's weakness with dense edges becomes 3D's strength.

- For objects: activity level (how many agents touching it), or staleness, or risk (failing tests, outdated service).
- For agents: current engagement depth (short-chain vs deep plan), or cost consumed, or time since last action.

- Union view: all agents' edges at once, colored by agent. "What's happening in my codebase?"
- Per-agent view: one agent's graph isolated. "What is agent X doing?" This is essentially the agent's reasoning trace rendered as a graph instead of a log.

- Select: a node, a subgraph, or a predicate (all agents with edges to this directory)
- Scope / constrain: "agent can only have edges within this subgraph" — spatial constraints become graph constraints, which is more natural
- Assign: "agent should have a plan-edge to this objective node"
- Inspect: show this agent's subgraph with temporal playback
- Interrupt: cut the agent's current active edges
- Compare: show two agents' subgraphs side-by-side or overlaid

1. Hairball at scale. Many agents × many objects × many interactions = visual mud. Edge bundling, aggregation, temporal decay, and per-agent filtering are all mandatory, not optional. The upside is they're all standard graph-viz techniques with good implementations.
2. Edge rate is the firehose. A code-editing agent generates tens of events per second at peak. Raw rendering is hopeless. You need windowing — edges aggregated over the last N seconds into a single weighted edge, with the raw event stream available on inspection. VegaFusion aggregation handles this cleanly.
3. Graph layout stability under node churn. As agents create/delete files, nodes appear and disappear. Force-directed layouts react violently; the whole map shifts. Incremental layouts (retain existing positions, place new nodes in context, fade out deleted ones with a delay) are needed. This is a solvable but real engineering problem.
4. Ontology decisions bake into the data model. What counts as a node? What counts as an edge type? Get this wrong and you're rewriting schemas. Worth prototyping with a loose, additive schema (edges have a typed metadata payload) before locking down.
5. The 3D z-axis function is load-bearing. If you pick the wrong function, 3D looks cluttered and adds nothing. The sensible path is to ship 2D, watch how operators use it, and let the pain points tell you what z should encode. Don't decide in a vacuum.

1. Rust event ingest: tail a file of agent events (synthetic or real), build an in-memory graph.
2. Arrow tables for nodes and edges, refreshed on tick.
3. VegaFusion pipeline: apply temporal decay to edges, compute per-agent subgraphs on demand.
4. Web frontend: bipartite-ish layout, objects in a hierarchical region (filesystem-shaped), agents in a band, edges drawn between with recency encoding.
5. Selection: click agent → isolate its subgraph. Click object → show which agents have touched it.
6. Time scrubber: replay edge events over a time window.

1. Edge ontology: what are the edge types, and do they carry payloads or just type tags?
2. Agent placement in 2D: in-graph (embedded near their activity) or in-band (separate region)? I'd argue in-band for v1, but it's worth being deliberate.
3. Union vs per-agent as the default view: which is the starting state when an operator opens the tool?

## User

Each agent has zones of control with it's own objects either by standard agent flows in a filesystem or transfering or sharing objects between zones of control. interactions are edges in zones of control on standard objects like files and services. Clicking on the objects should allow you to interface personally with them

---

## Assistant

Now the model snaps into focus. Let me work through it, because this is a meaningfully different architecture than the one I was heading toward.

Each agent has a zone of control — a region of the world that contains its own objects, plus shared objects. The zone is the unit of ownership and scope. Within a zone, the agent's interactions with objects are the edges. Between zones, transfer and sharing are the structural operations — objects move, or get replicated, or become jointly owned.

Clicking an object drops you into direct interaction with it — not a symbolic inspection panel, but the actual thing. A file opens in an editor-like surface. A service exposes its endpoints. The map is a way to find objects; the object itself is where work happens.

This isn't an RTS in the "command units to attack" sense anymore. It's closer to a multi-tenant workspace with territorial ownership, where the territories are agent-owned, the terrain is shared infrastructure, and the operator is a supervisor who can peer into any zone, any object, at any time.

The RTS metaphor that actually fits is 4X strategy (explore, expand, exploit, exterminate — minus the exterminate) or Dwarf Fortress-style colony management, not StarCraft. Zones of control, resource transfer, sharing agreements, territorial edges. That's the right mental model and it's a much better fit for agent orchestration than the combat-oriented RTS frame.

The zone becomes the primary organizing unit. A few design commitments follow:

Zones are explicit regions on the map. Not just metadata — they're rendered as bounded territories (polygons, hulls around member objects, or stylized boundaries). An operator scanning the map sees zones first, objects within zones second, edges third. This is the inversion from my previous thinking, where agents floated over terrain; now agents define the territory their objects sit in.

Objects belong to zones, not to agents directly. An agent owns a zone; the zone contains objects. This matters because:

Shared objects appear in multiple zones. Visually, they sit on the boundary between zones, or in an overlap region (Venn-diagram style), or are duplicated with a sharing-edge drawn between the copies. The choice affects how legible sharing is at a glance.

Unclaimed territory exists. Objects that no agent has touched are in the commons — visible, available, not yet in any zone. An agent entering new territory literally expands its zone.

The RTS-like rendering I'd reach for:

This starts looking less like a combat RTS and more like a Risk board or a Civilization map — territories, capitals, borders, trade routes. That's the right aesthetic because it matches the semantics.

This is the sharpest design choice in your message and it changes what the tool is. The map is not the terminus; it's a navigation surface. Clicking through drops you into direct engagement with the object. This means:

The tool has two modes of engagement, not two renderers. There's map-mode (zoomed out, strategic view, zones and edges) and object-mode (zoomed in, actual content, direct manipulation). These are both 2D. The 3D/VR mode from before is orthogonal to this — a third view, primarily for monitoring, where object-mode engagement might not even exist (or might be a companion-display thing).

Object views are real editors/interfaces, not dashboards. A file object opens a code editor — probably a real one, embedded, with syntax highlighting and edit affordances. A service object opens a console — log stream, health metrics, maybe an API playground. A database object opens a query interface. This is a substantial build — you're not shimming a read-only panel, you're providing working interfaces for each object type.

The agent's presence is visible inside object-mode too. If agent X is currently reading this file, you see that in the editor — cursor position, highlighted lines, whatever level of telemetry you have. If agent Y is calling this service, you see the in-flight requests. Object-mode isn't operator-only; it's a shared workspace where you can see what agents are doing at the object level.

Co-editing semantics matter. If you open a file the agent is editing, what happens? Locks? Operational transform? Take-over-and-agent-waits? These are real questions and the answer shapes the tool. My instinct: operator always wins — clicking in gives you control, the agent's action pauses or yields, you can explicitly hand control back. This matches the RTS metaphor (you're the commander, agents defer) and avoids the complexity of true co-editing.

The commands now become zone operations, which is cleaner than the generic RTS verbs I had before:

These are all graph operations at the zone-membership level, and they have natural RTS-like UI analogs:

The visual feedback on these operations is satisfying in the way RTS is satisfying — territorial change is glanceable, you can see the map reshape.

Inside a zone, edges are the agent's ongoing work on its objects — the per-agent graph from the previous turn, but now bounded by the zone. This means:

This resolves the edge-density problem from the previous turn much better than edge bundling would have. Zones are a natural locality principle. Most edges stay inside zones; cross-zone edges are few and informative.

The object views are a genuine second stack. They share telemetry (agent presence overlays) but otherwise have their own UI surface per object type. That's a real build cost and worth being upfront about.

Your original "2D vs 3D" framing now maps onto something more specific:

Map mode (2D) — strategic surface. Zones, objects, edges, zone operations, navigation. Authoring of the workflow structure happens here. Think: the command map in a 4X game.

Object mode (2D) — tactical surface. Direct engagement with a specific object. Edit the file, query the service, inspect the data. Agent presence visible but operator has full control. Think: opening a city screen in Civilization, or a unit's detail pane in XCOM — you've drilled into something specific.

Monitor mode (3D/VR) — ambient awareness surface. Watch zones evolve, watch activity pulse, get comfortable in the space. Probably inspection-only. Think: the overview room in a NOC with wall-sized displays, translated into embodied space.

Three modes, not two, with different affordances. Each earns its existence:

I want to flag this clearly: the "click object to interface personally" commitment is a large build. Each object type needs a genuine working interface. A file editor is a month of work to do well (or you embed Monaco and inherit its affordances, which is smart). A service console is another chunk. A database query UI is another. If you have ten object types, you have ten editors.

Three escape hatches:

Option 3 is probably the right starting point. It makes the tool a portal rather than a platform. You can always build native interfaces later for the object types that most benefit from agent-presence overlays.

Zones need a layout algorithm that:

This is essentially a political map problem. The techniques are:

For v1, you can get away with something much simpler: treemap or partitioned layout, where the map is explicitly divided into zone rectangles, and within each rectangle the agent's objects are laid out by type. Less elegant than hulls, much easier to implement, and actually very legible. Civilization-style tile-based territory is an honest prior art here.

The zone abstraction is useful only if the agent framework supports zone-bounded execution:

Frameworks where this is natural: LangGraph (explicit state + tool scoping), home-rolled agent loops (you control everything), Claude Code with tool permission gates (you can approve/deny per-tool-call). Frameworks where it's hard: most CrewAI/AutoGen configurations where agents are defined by prompts and tool inventories without runtime scope changes.

The engine's capability is bounded by the agents' controllability. If zone transfer/sharing isn't enforceable at the agent layer, the RTS ops become suggestions rather than commands — still useful for observability, but the "control" part of control surface weakens.

Three weeks, with a real target:

The question this spike answers: does the zone-of-control metaphor hold up when you see it in action? If watching three agents operate in visible territories tells an honest story about what they're doing, the core bet is good. If zones feel arbitrary or the zone boundaries obscure more than they reveal, the metaphor needs rethinking before you build further.

That's a real product shape. The closest existing tools are:

Nothing puts all these pieces together. The gap is real, and the pieces you're describing are individually sound. The build cost is nontrivial — object views alone could eat months — but the concept holds.

Worth settling before the spike:

Want to pull on any of those, or work through the sharing/coordination model, or sketch the data schema for zones + membership + edges?

```javascript
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

│  - Zone membership (who owns / shares what)                  │

                         │
                         ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  World State (Rust, Arrow)                                   │
```
