## User

ok instead of namespaces just have a chroot engine

---

## Assistant

Good simplification. `chroot` is the boring, obvious version of what I was overcomplicating with mount namespaces and Plan 9 aesthetics, and for your use case it's probably the right call. Let me work through what changes and what you should actually know before committing.

The core thing you need is: each agent should only see its zone, and attempting to access anything outside should fail at the OS level, not at a hook layer that could be bypassed or miscoded.

`chroot` gives you exactly that with one syscall. The agent process sees its chroot directory as `/`. Paths outside are unreachable — not "forbidden," but literally non-existent from the agent's perspective. Zone violation becomes a file-not-found error, which is the cleanest possible failure mode because the agent's tool frameworks already handle it gracefully.

No mount namespaces, no FUSE, no 9P server, no per-process namespace graph. Just: `chroot("/rts/zones/alpha")`, `exec("claude")`, done.

This also dodges a bunch of complexity I was waving at:

The result: the zone is the agent's filesystem root. "Transfer file between zones" is `cp` or `mv` between two chroot directories. "Share file" is a hard link. "Fence" is a narrower chroot. Zone membership is `ls`.

Worth being clear-eyed, because chroot has well-known limitations:

It gives you:

It doesn't give you:

For your use case — friendly coding agents you're running yourself, not adversarial untrusted code — most of these don't matter. The agents aren't trying to escape; you just want zone structure to be enforced so that the permission model is real. Chroot handles that.

If you later want stronger isolation: containers (Docker, Podman, systemd-nspawn) are "chroot plus more namespaces" and you can upgrade without changing the conceptual model. The agent still sees `/` as its zone; the container just also isolates network, PIDs, etc.

The engine is the thing that sets up, populates, and tears down chroots per agent. Responsibilities:

Chroot setup. Given a zone spec, create a directory tree at `/rts/zones/<agent>/` and populate it with what the agent needs:

Agent launch inside the chroot. Spawn the agent process, `chroot` into the zone, drop to a non-root UID, exec the agent binary. The agent lives inside.

Zone operations as filesystem operations. Transfer, share, fence, merge, split — all become `cp`/`mv`/`ln`/`rm` operations on the chroot directories. The operator's UI actions translate to these.

Teardown. On agent exit, optionally snapshot the zone (for replay/audit), clean up the chroot.

Shared substrate management. There's one "commons" or "shared" directory that gets bind-mounted into multiple zones as needed. This is the `~shared` region from the previous turn, realized as a real directory you control.

This is the first thing that bites. A chroot directory needs enough inside it for the agent to run. For a Node-based CLI like Claude Code or OpenCode, this means Node itself, its dependencies, system libraries, CA certs, DNS resolution, etc. Naive chroot setup means copying or bind-mounting all of this.

Three approaches, ordered by laziness:

Option A: Bind-mount the host. The chroot contains bind mounts of `/usr`, `/lib`, `/lib64`, `/etc`, `/bin` (read-only) from the host. The agent uses host binaries and libraries. Zone contents (the agent's writable workspace) are a separate subtree that's genuinely per-zone.

Pros: trivial setup, works with whatever the host has installed, no duplication.
Cons: agent can read host system files. That's fine for your trust model but worth acknowledging — the zone is "the agent's workspace is isolated, the system is shared."

Option B: Build a minimal rootfs. Use debootstrap, alpine's apk, or a static-linked tarball to construct a small rootfs tree, copy it into each zone. Agent has its own system view.

Pros: genuinely isolated, reproducible, snapshotable.
Cons: disk cost per zone, more setup work, version drift between host and chroot.

Option C: Use overlayfs. Lower layer is a shared base rootfs (constructed once). Upper layer is per-agent writable overlay containing just the zone contents. Agent sees a merged view; its writes go to its own upper.

Pros: shared storage, per-agent mutations, trivially snapshotable (the upper layer is the agent's changes).
Cons: Linux-specific, slightly more complex setup.

Recommendation: A for v1, C for v2. Bind-mount the host for the first build because it's ten lines of setup code and gets you running. Migrate to overlayfs when you want proper snapshots and reproducibility.

Within the chroot, the agent has a workspace — the files it's allowed to edit, create, and call its own. Structurally:

The agent sees `/` and inside it `workspace/`, `shared/`, `control/`. Its world is simple. The operator's UI provides views into any of these, but underneath it's just files.

With this model, every zone operation from earlier turns gets a one-line implementation:

All of these are visible to the operator as "moving files around on a map." The map view is a rendering of the chroot filesystem layout. Drag a file from zone A to zone B → `mv`. Right-click → "share with beta" → `ln`. No abstraction layer between the UI and the filesystem.

The policy file lives inside the chroot at `/control/policy` (readable by the agent, writable by the RTS). But — critically — with chroot, the policy is mostly redundant for filesystem operations. The chroot itself is the policy: the agent can only touch what's in its chroot. The policy file then serves a narrower role:

This is a cleaner separation: filesystem enforcement is OS-level via chroot, semantic enforcement is policy-file-level via hooks. Two layers, each doing what it's best at, no overlap.

Chroot doesn't isolate network. The agent can still hit any URL. For your use case this might be fine — if agents should be able to call services, let them — but you probably want some control.

Options:

For v1, I'd go with accept it, log everything (the agent's tool-call stream already tells you what URLs it hit). If you need enforcement later, the proxy approach is usually the right trade.

The process-attribution problem from several turns ago (fanotify, eBPF, etc.) substantially dissolves under chroot. Because:

You still want process-tree tracking for shell-outs inside the chroot (agent runs `grep`, you want to know what `grep` touched), but cross-agent attribution is essentially free. The `pwd` of a process tells you the zone. That's much simpler than the PID-correlation dance.

The engine is a small Rust library. The launcher is a short supervised-subprocess routine. Everything else (map, policy, event log) already exists from earlier turns; chroot just becomes the thing that makes the zone structural.

One thing worth saying plainly: chroot is not a security boundary. It's a structural convenience. A sufficiently determined or malicious process can escape it, especially if it runs as root inside the chroot. Known escapes exist; it's not a sandbox in the sense that seccomp or gVisor is.

For your use case (running LLM coding agents you control, that aren't trying to escape), this is fine. They're not trying to hack out. The chroot makes zone violation impossible by construction in the normal operation case, which is what you need.

If you ever want to run untrusted agents (third-party, adversarial red-team, etc.), upgrade to containers (Podman with rootless + user namespaces is the modern clean answer) or microVMs (Firecracker). The conceptual model stays the same — the agent sees its zone as `/` — so the upgrade path doesn't churn your architecture.

Comparing against the Plan 9 / namespace version:

About two-thirds of the Plan 9 aesthetic gone. The remaining third — "everything the agent sees is a file, including its control surface" — preserved, because that's the genuinely useful part. Plan 9's insight "treat interfaces as files" still applies to the control directory inside the chroot; you just don't need Plan 9's global namespace infrastructure to realize it.

The Urbit bits (event log, stable identity, state-as-function-of-log) are orthogonal to chroot and should stay. Those were about how state evolves, not about the filesystem structure.

Same two-week budget, simpler:

Week 1: chroot engine + one agent.

Week 2: zone operations + second agent.

End state: two isolated agents, file-level zone enforcement, live operator control, zone operations working. That's a credible demo and a defensible architecture.

Chroot is the right call. It's the OS's built-in "zone of control," and using it instead of inventing a higher-level namespace abstraction makes everything more concrete:

You lose Plan 9's composability (union mounts, per-process namespaces that can diverge arbitrarily) and Urbit's universal identity scheme, but you keep the useful philosophical commitments (files as interfaces, namespace-as-structure, event log as history) and you build on kernel primitives that have been stable for decades.

The engine is a weekend's worth of Rust for the core, a week for polish, and it slots cleanly into the rest of the architecture. Everything from earlier turns (VegaFusion dataflow, SIDC-style agent symbols, map visualization, 3D projection, ACP/Claude Code/OpenCode integrations, event log) stays intact — chroot just replaces the more elaborate namespace mechanism I was reaching for.

Decisions worth making before building:

Want to work through the chroot engine's Rust API, the launcher's privilege-dropping sequence, the control-file protocol (what files and what formats), or the bind-mount recipe for Node-based agents?

```javascript
/rts/zones/alpha/              <- chroot root
├── bin -> host /bin           (bind mount, ro)
├── lib -> host /lib           (bind mount, ro)
├── usr -> host /usr           (bind mount, ro)
├── etc/                       (partial copy + /etc/resolv.conf, /etc/ssl/certs)
├── tmp/                       (writable, agent-private)
├── proc/                      (bind mount or not, depending on isolation needs)
├── workspace/                 <- the actual zone contents
│   ├── project/               <- the code the agent is working on
│   └── ...
├── shared/                    <- shared substrate, bind-mounted
│   ├── libs/
│   └── services/
└── control/                   <- RTS-writable, agent-readable
    ├── prompt                 (file the operator writes messages to)
    ├── policy                 (current zone policy, live-readable)
    └── events                 (agent writes events here; RTS tails)
```

```javascript
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Chroot Engine (Rust)                                      │
│                                                           │
│  ZoneSpec → create chroot tree                            │
│           → bind-mount host runtime                       │
│           → bind-mount shared substrate                   │
│           → populate control files                        │
│           → return ready-to-launch zone                   │
│                                                           │
│  ZoneOp (transfer, share, fence, etc.)                    │
│           → filesystem operations                         │
│           → emit event to log                             │
│                                                           │
│  ZoneTeardown → optionally snapshot                       │
│               → unmount                                   │
│               → remove tree                               │
└──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Launcher                                                  │
│                                                           │
│  fork → setuid (non-root) → chroot → chdir → exec(agent)  │
│                                                           │
│  Captures stdio; plumbs control files into agent's env    │
└──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Agent Process                                             │
│                                                           │
│  Sees /workspace, /shared, /control                       │
│  Emits events via SDK hook or stdio parsing               │
│  Reads prompt file for operator messages                  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- No namespace syscalls (which are Linux-specific and require CAP_SYS_ADMIN or user namespaces)
- No custom filesystem code
- No synthesizing interfaces as files (you can still do this later, but it's not load-bearing now)
- Works on any Unix — macOS, *BSD, Linux all have chroot

- Filesystem isolation at the path level
- Cross-platform support (POSIX)
- No special privileges needed beyond root at setup time (you can drop privileges after chroot)
- Trivial mental model — the agent's world is a directory

- Network isolation (agent can still reach any network resource)
- PID isolation (agent sees all host processes in /proc if /proc is mounted in its chroot, none if it isn't)
- Resource limits (CPU, memory, disk)
- Protection against root escapes (a root process inside a chroot can escape; well-known attack)
- IPC isolation (System V IPC, abstract Unix sockets still shared)

- The agent's own working files (its zone contents)
- Minimal runtime: /bin, /lib, /usr/lib, /etc/ssl/certs, etc. — enough for the agent CLI to execute
- Any shared objects bind-mounted in (for the "share" operation)
- A temporary writable area (/tmp)

- Transfer file from zone A to zone B: mv /rts/zones/alpha/workspace/foo.py /rts/zones/beta/workspace/foo.py. Agent A sees the file disappear; agent B sees it appear.
- Share file between zones: ln /rts/shared/foo.py /rts/zones/alpha/workspace/foo.py && ln /rts/shared/foo.py /rts/zones/beta/workspace/foo.py. Both agents see the same inode; writes from either are visible to both.
- Fence: remove subtrees from the chroot. What's not in the chroot doesn't exist.
- Merge zones: combine the chroot contents of A and B into one.
- Split zone: create a new zone, move a subset of files.
- Snapshot: cp -r (or btrfs snapshot if available) of the zone's writable portion.
- Rollback: restore from snapshot.

- Network and service restrictions (still not enforced by chroot, so policy decides what hostnames are allowed)
- Intent declaration (the agent's current task, its scope, what it's allowed to do within its chroot)
- Tool-level permissions (e.g., "no rm -rf, no git push") — still hook-enforced at the SDK layer

- Accept it. Agents can hit the network. The zone is filesystem-only. Name this openly.
- Network namespace (Linux-specific, adds kernel namespace complexity). Each zone gets its own network namespace with a whitelist of allowed endpoints. This is real containerization territory.
- Proxy-based. Run a local HTTP proxy that the agent's environment variables point to; the proxy enforces allowed destinations. Works cross-platform, gives you observability for free (you see every service call).
- DNS-based. Custom /etc/resolv.conf inside the chroot pointing to a DNS server you control, which refuses to resolve disallowed hostnames. Weaker but very simple.

- Agent A runs inside /rts/zones/alpha/. Any filesystem event whose path starts with that is attributed to A.
- The chroot guarantees the agent can't write to paths outside its zone, so there's no attribution ambiguity to resolve for writes.
- For reads, the agent can only read what's mounted into its chroot, so reads are also attributable by path.

- Rust crate that creates a chroot directory, bind-mounts host /usr, /bin, /lib, etc., creates workspace/ from a source directory, creates control/ with prompt, policy, events files.
- Launcher forks, drops privileges, chroots, execs Claude Code with its permission hooks pointing to a URL on the RTS (or writing to the control/events file).
- RTS tails events, sees tool calls, renders them as edges on the map.
- Operator writes to prompt file → agent receives it as a user message.

- Implement transfer, share, fence as RTS commands that do filesystem operations on the chroot trees.
- Add a second agent (OpenCode via ACP, or another Claude Code instance) in its own chroot.
- Test sharing: a file hard-linked into both chroots, edits from either visible to both.
- Test transfer: map drag → mv → both agents' views update.
- Validate that the two agents are genuinely isolated: agent A has no path to agent B's workspace.

1. Host runtime drift. Bind-mounting /usr means agents get whatever system libraries you have. If you update your host libc, agent behavior might change. Option C (overlayfs with a frozen base) fixes this but adds complexity. For a personal tool, not a problem; for anything reproducible, a real issue.
2. /proc and /dev handling. Many tools break without these. You probably need /proc mounted (bind or fresh) and a minimal /dev (just /dev/null, /dev/urandom, /dev/tty). Getting this minimal-but-complete is fiddly the first time.
3. Agent CLIs that phone home or write to unexpected paths. Node-based agents often want ~/.npm, ~/.cache, etc. In a chroot, $HOME is wherever you point it (typically /workspace or /tmp). You may need to set env vars carefully. Expect to iterate on what the agent needs.
4. Auth tokens and secrets. The agent needs API keys to reach Anthropic/OpenAI. These go in the chroot via env vars (simplest) or a mounted secrets file. Be careful about what leaks into the zone.
5. Root required for setup. chroot needs root (or CAP_SYS_CHROOT). The engine either runs as root and drops privileges per agent, or runs as a user with the capability granted. For a personal tool, running a privileged launcher is fine; for a shared service, use a setuid helper or a systemd unit with capabilities.
6. Cross-platform story weakens. chroot exists on macOS and BSD, but the bind-mount-the-host trick is Linux-idiomatic. On macOS, you'd need to copy files or use different tricks. Still buildable, just different. If Linux-first is acceptable, chroot is fine.

- Zones are directories.
- Zone operations are file operations.
- Policy is a file.
- Control is a file.
- Events are a file.
- The agent's reality is /, which is the zone.
- The map is a view over the chroot filesystem.

1. Host bind-mounting vs. minimal rootfs vs. overlayfs — I'd start with host bind-mounting.
2. Root-owned launcher or user-level with capabilities — probably root launcher for simplicity, drop privileges before exec.
3. Network policy — accept for v1, proxy later.
4. /proc and /dev setup — research the minimum once, codify it.
5. Snapshot strategy — does v1 have snapshots at all, or is that a later feature?

---

## User

It sounds like chroot does almost everything we need, is there a fancier chroot?

---

## Assistant

Yes — several, and they form a clear ladder. "Fancier chroot" is actually one of the most well-explored territories in Linux systems engineering, because chroot's limitations have been known for decades and every subsequent isolation primitive is some variation on "chroot plus more." Let me walk the ladder, because picking the right rung matters more than people usually think.

Each step adds specific isolation dimensions while preserving the "agent sees its zone as /" mental model.

Strictly more capable than `chroot`, barely more complex. `pivot_root` swaps the root filesystem of a mount namespace, rather than just changing the current process's idea of where `/` is. Key differences:

If you want "chroot but without the well-known escapes," `pivot_root` inside an unshared mount namespace is the minimum upgrade. It's 20 lines of Rust more than plain chroot. No new concepts, just better primitives.

When to pick it: if plain chroot is your instinct, do this instead. It's strictly better, and the mental model is identical.

This is where "fancier chroot" actually starts paying for itself. Linux has seven namespaces you can unshare per-process:

Put these together and you have a real isolation model. This is what containers actually are under the hood — there's no container kernel primitive; "container" is just a marketing term for "a process with several namespaces unshared and its root pivot_rooted into an isolated filesystem."

The Rust path: `nix::sched::unshare`, `nix::sched::clone`, or `nix::sched::CloneFlags`. Direct syscalls, no runtime dependency. A small Rust crate can be the "make a container from scratch" layer, and that's closer to your style than pulling in Docker.

When to pick it: when you want real per-agent isolation (network rules, PID tree, resource limits) but don't want to adopt a container runtime. You're writing ~200 lines of Rust instead of 20, but you've built your own minimal container engine that does exactly what you need and nothing else. For your project, this is probably the right rung.

A small, focused C tool (used under the hood by Flatpak) that exposes Linux namespaces through a clean command-line interface. Think of it as "namespaces, but you describe the sandbox as CLI args."

That's it. Full per-agent isolation, declaratively specified, no runtime required beyond bubblewrap itself. Mount bind, ro-bind, tmpfs, namespace unsharing, all via flags.

Pros: battle-tested (Flatpak relies on it), small (one static binary), stable CLI, very fast startup (< 10ms overhead), designed exactly for your use case ("run this program in a constrained filesystem environment").
Cons: external dependency, not Rust-native, Linux-only.

When to pick it: when you want the Rung 2 benefits without writing the kernel-call Rust. Shell out to `bwrap` from your Rust engine; let it do the namespace setup; your engine's job becomes "generate the bwrap invocation and supervise the process." This is probably the fastest path to a working fancy-chroot for your project.

The specific reason bubblewrap is compelling for you: the whole agent-zone spec becomes a config file that generates a bwrap command line, which means your zone policy is a declarative thing you can inspect, edit, diff, and version-control as text. That's a nice property.

systemd's built-in lightweight container runner. Not a daemon, just a command. Sets up all the namespaces, populates `/dev`, manages cgroups via systemd, handles the chroot/pivot_root, gives you a shell or runs a process in the container.

Also ~one line. More "complete" than bubblewrap in that it sets up a fuller Linux userland (getty, init-like behavior if desired) but also heavier. Integrates with systemd's cgroup management for free.

Pros: no extra install on systemd distros, integrates with `systemctl` for lifecycle, excellent cgroup integration.
Cons: systemd-dependent, overkill if you just want process isolation (it thinks in terms of "containers as tiny machines"), logs via journald which you may or may not want.

When to pick it: if your host is a systemd distro and you want the containers to feel like proper managed services. Less idiomatic for "launch-a-process-in-a-sandbox" than bubblewrap.

Full container runtimes. You know these. For your use case they're almost certainly overkill:

When to pick it: if you specifically want OCI images (so you can distribute pre-built agent environments), or if you want to share infrastructure with an existing containerized platform. Podman's rootless mode is genuinely nice. But for "launch an agent with filesystem and network isolation," you're paying a lot for capabilities you don't need.

Podman is the less-bad option here because it's daemonless and works well rootless. Docker's daemon model is more friction than it's worth for this project.

The "real" sandbox tier. These provide actual security boundaries against malicious code:

When to pick it: only if you're running adversarial agents — third-party agents, red-team agents, agents you don't control. For agents you write and run yourself, this is paranoia.

Your instinct is right, and it's important to pin down why it's right, because it tells you which rung to pick:

The friendly coding agents you're integrating (Claude Code, OpenCode, OpenClaw) are not trying to escape. They're trying to do their job. You're not defending against malicious code; you're imposing a structural model so that zone semantics are real.

This means:

So: you want Rung 2 or Rung 3. Namespaces, not containers, not VMs.

Here's why bubblewrap specifically:

which compiles to a `bwrap ...` command. Zone policy becomes text, which means it's diffable, version-controllable, and introspectable.

The path looks like:

Minimal changes. Everything I said about chroot still applies:

The delta is:

Concrete things that work better with namespaces:

Each of these is a real quality-of-life improvement over chroot, and they come essentially free with bubblewrap.

Network namespace setup is where complexity lives. If you want the agent to reach the internet (to call Anthropic/OpenAI APIs), you need to either:

For v1, I'd actually share the host network namespace (accept weaker network isolation) and apply policy via an HTTP proxy the agent's env points to. That's the pragmatic path. Later you can upgrade to slirp4netns for proper per-agent net namespaces.

User namespaces can surprise you. If the agent needs to write files that end up readable by the host user (and it does — those files are in `workspace/` which the operator and other tools access), UID mapping matters. The default mapping (agent's UID 0 = host's UID X) usually does what you want, but edge cases exist. Test early.

Bubblewrap setuid requirement. On some distros, bubblewrap needs to be setuid-root to unshare user namespaces. On others, unprivileged user namespaces are enabled by default. Check your target distro. Not a blocker but worth knowing.

Overlayfs as the filesystem is a natural fit. Lower layer = the base rootfs (shared, read-only, built once). Middle layer(s) = shared substrate between agents. Upper layer = per-agent writable workspace. Bubblewrap supports overlayfs via `--overlay-src` and `--overlay` flags. This gets you copy-on-write semantics, snapshots for free (the upper layer is the snapshot), and storage efficiency.

Chroot was Rung 0 (ish). Plain chroot worked but with escape risk and no process/network/resource isolation.

Bubblewrap is Rung 3. Full namespace isolation, minimal setup effort, declarative zone specs, correct semantics for your use case.

You skip Rungs 4+ (container runtimes, VMs) because you don't need image management, you don't need hardware virtualization, and you don't have adversarial agents.

You could go to Rung 2 (direct namespace syscalls in Rust) for purity and zero external dependency. I'd argue this is premature optimization — bubblewrap is small, fast, and the dependency is trivial. But if you want to drop it eventually, the port is straightforward.

That's essentially the whole engine. A few hundred lines to polish, but the shape is this.

Keep:

Skip:

"Fancier chroot" has a good answer, and that answer is "Linux namespaces, accessed through bubblewrap." This is a well-trodden path — Flatpak, the Steam Runtime, a bunch of CI sandboxes, and a lot of single-purpose sandbox tools all use bubblewrap for exactly this kind of "run this process in an isolated filesystem + some namespace isolation" use case. You'd be building on well-validated infrastructure.

The key insight: you don't need containers, and you don't need to roll your own namespace code. You need bubblewrap, and your engine wraps it. That's the Goldilocks zone for this project.

Decisions worth locking:

Want to dig into the bubblewrap invocation details for Claude Code / OpenCode specifically, the overlayfs layout, the event-log-through-control-files protocol, or the network proxy design?

```javascript
bwrap \
  --ro-bind /usr /usr \
  --ro-bind /lib /lib \
  --bind ./agent-alpha-workspace /workspace \
  --bind ./shared-substrate /shared \
  --proc /proc \
  --dev /dev \
  --tmpfs /tmp \
  --unshare-pid --unshare-net \
  --chdir /workspace \
  claude
```

```javascript
systemd-nspawn -D /rts/zones/alpha --bind=/shared --private-network claude
```

```rust
struct ZoneSpec {
       workspace: PathBuf,
       shared: Vec<PathBuf>,
       ro_host_paths: Vec<PathBuf>,  // /usr, /lib, etc.
       network: NetworkPolicy,
       env: HashMap<String, String>,
       command: Vec<String>,
   }
```

```javascript
Zone policy (text config)
   │
   ▼
ZoneSpec (Rust struct)
   │
   ▼
bubblewrap invocation (CLI args)
   │
   ▼
bwrap fork/execs agent inside namespaces
   │
   ▼
Agent sees: /workspace, /shared, /control, minimal / otherwise
```

```rust
struct ChrootEngine {
    zones_root: PathBuf,
    shared_root: PathBuf,
    base_rootfs: BaseRootFs,  // bind-mounts or overlay lower
}

struct Zone {
    id: ZoneId,
    workspace: PathBuf,
    shared_mounts: Vec<PathBuf>,
    policy: PolicyRef,
}

impl ChrootEngine {
    fn create_zone(&self, spec: ZoneSpec) -> Zone { ... }
    
    fn launch(&self, zone: &Zone, command: &[&str]) -> LaunchedAgent {
        let args = self.build_bwrap_args(zone, command);
        let child = Command::new("bwrap").args(&args).spawn()?;
        LaunchedAgent { child, zone_id: zone.id }
    }
    
    fn transfer(&self, from: &Zone, to: &Zone, path: &Path) { ... }  // mv
    fn share(&self, zones: &[&Zone], path: &Path) { ... }  // ln
    fn snapshot(&self, zone: &Zone) -> Snapshot { ... }
    fn teardown(&self, zone: Zone) { ... }
}

fn build_bwrap_args(zone: &Zone, command: &[&str]) -> Vec<String> {
    let mut args = vec![
        "--ro-bind", "/usr", "/usr",
        "--ro-bind", "/lib", "/lib",
        "--ro-bind", "/lib64", "/lib64",
        "--ro-bind", "/etc/ssl", "/etc/ssl",
        "--ro-bind", "/etc/resolv.conf", "/etc/resolv.conf",
        "--proc", "/proc",
        "--dev", "/dev",
        "--tmpfs", "/tmp",
        "--bind", zone.workspace.display(), "/workspace",
        "--unshare-pid",
        "--unshare-uts",
        "--unshare-ipc",
        "--chdir", "/workspace",
        "--die-with-parent",
    ];
    for shared in &zone.shared_mounts {
        args.extend(["--bind", shared.display(), "/shared"]);
    }
    args.extend(command);
    args
}
```

- Works inside a mount namespace, so the old root can be unmounted and fully detached. With chroot, the old root is still there, just not reachable by path — which is the basis of most chroot escapes.
- Designed for container-like scenarios.
- Used by essentially every container runtime as the actual "chroot step."

- Mount: each process sees its own filesystem tree, with independent bind mounts. This is the big one — lets you bind-mount things into the agent's view without affecting the host, and cleanly tear down on exit.
- PID: agent sees only its own process subtree. Its ps aux shows only its children. PID 1 inside the namespace is the agent itself.
- Network: agent gets its own network stack — separate interfaces, routing tables, firewall rules. You can whitelist outbound destinations at the kernel level.
- UTS: agent has its own hostname.
- IPC: System V IPC / POSIX message queues are separate.
- User: UIDs can be remapped — agent can be "root" inside its namespace while being a low-privilege user outside. Enables rootless setup.
- Cgroup: CPU, memory, I/O accounting and limits per agent.

- You get image management, registries, networking stacks, volume management, orchestration hooks, etc.
- But also: a daemon (Docker) or more machinery (Podman, containerd), image layer abstractions you don't need (you're bind-mounting the host), OCI-spec overhead, and the cultural weight of "I'm running containers" which pulls people toward Kubernetes.

- gVisor: intercepts syscalls in userspace, reimplementing the kernel API. Agent can't directly reach the host kernel.
- Kata Containers: runs each container in a lightweight VM.
- Firecracker: microVMs, originally for AWS Lambda. Very fast boot (hundreds of ms), proper hardware virtualization.

- You don't need gVisor/Firecracker tier. Security isolation isn't the goal.
- You don't need container-runtime tier (Docker/Podman). Image management and orchestration aren't the goal.
- You do benefit from namespace-tier isolation, because:

Network namespace lets you apply per-agent network policy properly (each agent has its own iptables-equivalent and can't cheat).
PID namespace makes the agent's process tree clean and attributable.
Mount namespace gives you clean bind-mount teardown and overlayfs per agent.
User namespace lets you run unprivileged but have the agent think it's root (some tools need this).
Cgroup namespace lets you account for and limit agent resource use.
- Network namespace lets you apply per-agent network policy properly (each agent has its own iptables-equivalent and can't cheat).
- PID namespace makes the agent's process tree clean and attributable.
- Mount namespace gives you clean bind-mount teardown and overlayfs per agent.
- User namespace lets you run unprivileged but have the agent think it's root (some tools need this).
- Cgroup namespace lets you account for and limit agent resource use.

- Network namespace lets you apply per-agent network policy properly (each agent has its own iptables-equivalent and can't cheat).
- PID namespace makes the agent's process tree clean and attributable.
- Mount namespace gives you clean bind-mount teardown and overlayfs per agent.
- User namespace lets you run unprivileged but have the agent think it's root (some tools need this).
- Cgroup namespace lets you account for and limit agent resource use.

1. Your engine shrinks. Instead of writing the namespace-setup logic in Rust with nix crate calls, you generate a bubblewrap command line from your zone spec. Your Rust engine's job becomes "policy → bwrap args → fork/exec bwrap → supervise." A few hundred lines at most.
2. Zone spec is declarative. A zone becomes a struct like:

1. Zone operations stay trivially filesystem-based. Transfer, share, fence are still mv, ln, rm on the host-visible paths. Bubblewrap just bind-mounts them into the agent's view.
2. You can upgrade to Rung 2 later without architectural change. If you decide you want the namespace-setup logic in-process (to avoid shelling out, or for per-agent programmatic customization), you port the bubblewrap invocation logic to nix::sched::unshare calls. The zone spec doesn't change; only the executor does.
3. It's actively maintained by serious people. The containers/Flatpak folks stress-test it. You benefit.

- Zones are directories (host-visible) that get bind-mounted into agents
- Zone operations are filesystem operations
- Policy is a file
- Control surface is files in /control/
- Event log, map view, etc. all unchanged

- Launch path uses bwrap instead of chroot
- Agents get PID/network/mount/IPC/user namespaces in addition to filesystem root isolation
- Network policy becomes real (per-agent net namespace, with explicit bridge/veth or a proxy forced via iptables rules in the namespace)
- Resource limits become real (cgroups per agent, configurable from your engine)

- ps inside the agent shows only its processes. This is clarifying for the agent's self-model and closes a surprising information leak.
- Network policy is enforceable. You can run iptables rules inside the agent's net namespace, or give it a veth pair routed through a host-side proxy. Agent can't bypass.
- Per-agent /tmp. A fresh tmpfs per zone, gone on teardown. No leak between agents.
- Resource limits. CPU/memory caps via cgroups mean a runaway agent doesn't take down the host.
- Clean teardown. Unmount the namespace, everything inside goes away atomically. No leftover processes, no leftover bind mounts, no leftover anything.
- User namespace support. Agent can run as UID 0 inside its namespace (some tools really want this) while actually being an unprivileged UID outside. Rootless-capable.

- Share the host network namespace (--share-net in bubblewrap) — simplest, but loses the isolation benefit for network.
- Set up a veth pair, give the agent one end, configure routing on the host — full isolation, more setup.
- Use a slirp-style userspace networking (slirp4netns) — no root needed on host, agent gets its own netns with NAT'd access. Simpler than veth.

- Everything from the previous turns: Arrow event log, VegaFusion dataflow, map rendering, SIDC symbols, zone operations as filesystem operations, ACP adapter, Claude Code integration, OpenCode integration.
- The "control files" concept: /control/prompt, /control/policy, /control/events. These work the same inside a bwrap sandbox as they did inside a chroot.

- Writing Plan 9 mount-namespace logic by hand in Rust (bubblewrap does it).
- FUSE for synthesizing interfaces (real files still work).
- 9P server (not needed).
- Container runtime integration (bubblewrap is the runtime).
- Custom image management (host bind-mounts + overlayfs handle it).

1. Bubblewrap shell-out or Rust nix::sched::unshare? I'd argue bubblewrap for v1, port to native syscalls only if you have a specific reason.
2. Network policy: share host net for v1 + proxy, or full net-namespace from day one?
3. Overlayfs or bind-mount-based rootfs: overlayfs is cleaner but adds a wrinkle; bind-mounts are trivial.
4. Rootless or root launcher: bubblewrap supports both; rootless is slightly more fiddly but much better security posture.
5. Cgroup management: systemd user slices, manual cgroup v2, or skip resource limits for v1?

---

## User

Can we get macos and wsl2?

---

## Assistant

Good question to front-load, because the answer reshapes the architecture. Short version: Linux (including WSL2) is straightforward and gets you everything we've been designing. macOS is fundamentally different and you'll end up with a separate implementation path for it, not a port.

Let me work through both honestly.

WSL2 is a real Linux kernel running in a lightweight VM. From the perspective of your chroot/bubblewrap engine, it is Linux. Namespaces, cgroups, overlayfs, bubblewrap all work. You install `bubblewrap` via `apt` and your Rust engine works unmodified.

Caveats worth knowing but not worrying much about:

For your project, treat WSL2 as Linux. It is Linux. One codepath, one binary, same tests. The operator happens to be running it on Windows; that's a deployment detail, not an architecture concern.

The only thing worth doing for Windows users specifically is a small onboarding shim: a PowerShell script that checks for WSL2, installs the right distro if needed, and drops them into a working shell inside WSL where your engine runs. Everything else is identical.

macOS does not have:

macOS does have:

This is a different operating system with different primitives. You can't "port" a bubblewrap-based engine to macOS; you have to implement the same semantics using different mechanisms. Let me walk the real options.

Plain `chroot` still works on macOS. Your engine creates a directory per agent, copies or symlinks in what's needed, `chroot`s, drops privileges (maybe), execs the agent. You lose:

You keep:

This is defensible as a v1 macOS story if you're honest about what's weaker. For friendly agents in a dev environment, filesystem isolation + SDK-layer permission hooks gets you most of the value.

macOS's `sandbox_init` API reads a Scheme-based policy language (SBPL) that can restrict file access, network, IPC, process creation, etc. at the kernel level. It's what Apple's own sandboxing uses.

You load this policy before exec'ing the agent. It applies to the process tree.

Pros: real kernel-enforced restrictions on filesystem, network, and IPC. More fine-grained than chroot.
Cons: Apple has marked the API as deprecated for years and still uses it internally, which is classic Apple — it works fine, there's no replacement, but the headers warn you. The documentation is sparse; most real knowledge comes from reverse-engineering and blog posts. Policy language is finicky. No official spec.

The honest state: `sandbox_init` is usable, Chrome and Firefox use it for their renderer sandboxes on macOS, but you're operating on semi-undocumented infrastructure. The deprecation status is "will be there for years but we don't promise forever."

If you go this route, you end up maintaining SBPL policies alongside your Linux/bubblewrap configs, which is a real translation burden.

Apple's Virtualization.framework (macOS 11+, much better on Apple Silicon) lets you run a lightweight Linux VM natively. Tools like Lima, OrbStack, UTM, and Tart all build on it. The design:

This is essentially "Linux on macOS via a sanctioned VM path," and it's become the de facto answer for any serious dev tool that needs Linux primitives. Docker Desktop does this. OrbStack does this. The overhead on Apple Silicon is genuinely small.

Pros: you have one codepath (Linux/bubblewrap). macOS becomes a launcher that starts the VM and forwards to it. Identical semantics everywhere.
Cons: the user installs a VM runtime. You depend on the VM being healthy. Filesystem I/O across the host/guest boundary (if you want the operator to edit files in macOS-native tools) needs a shared-folder mechanism (VirtioFS, SMB, SSHFS) which has its own performance and reliability characteristics.

This is probably the right answer.

Specialization of Option 3: don't implement VM management yourself, require the user to install one of these and run your engine inside it (as a container, or via `lima shell`, or similar).

Pros: zero VM management code in your engine.
Cons: the user installs Docker Desktop (licensing issues for commercial use) or OrbStack (excellent but paid for pro use). You're depending on their product.

Useful as a stopgap ("for macOS users, run our Linux image in Docker Desktop for now") but not a long-term solution.

Apple shipped a native container runtime and `container` CLI in macOS 15+ for Apple Silicon. It uses Virtualization.framework, runs OCI images, native tool, no Docker Desktop needed. If you're on recent Apple Silicon Macs, this is becoming the sanctioned path.

State of this as of 2026: still early, smaller community than Docker/Podman, but Apple-native so it'll mature. Worth watching; probably not what you build on today unless you're targeting newer Macs exclusively.

Three honest paths, pick one:

Path A, strongly. Here's why:

Path A is what Docker did (Docker Desktop is a VM). It's what Podman Desktop does. It's what essentially every serious Linux-primitives-based developer tool does on macOS. Your project is well-suited to join that pattern.

Your codebase:

The Mac launcher is ~500 lines if you write it. If you skip it, Mac users `ssh` into their VM and operate from there, which is also fine for a technical audience.

This is the one genuinely annoying thing about Path A. Agents are inside the Linux VM and writing to `/rts/zones/alpha/workspace/foo.py`. The operator on the Mac wants to click a file and have it open in Cursor/VS Code/whatever Mac-native editor they use.

Solutions:

OrbStack in particular has made this mostly transparent — their shared-folder story Just Works for most cases. VS Code Remote is the complementary piece: the editor knows how to reach into the VM and open files in-place.

None of these are your problem to build. They're platform integrations you document.

Since you asked about both together, a few more WSL2 notes:

systemd is opt-in. Add `[boot]\nsystemd=true` to `/etc/wsl.conf` if you want systemd-based services inside WSL2. For your engine this matters only if you choose systemd-nspawn (which I'd argue against) or if you want to run your engine as a systemd unit. Neither is required.

Filesystem location matters for performance. Anything under the WSL2 root filesystem (`/home/user/rts/...`) is fast. Anything under `/mnt/c/` (mapped Windows filesystem) is slow for lots of small file operations, which is exactly what agents generate. Keep zones inside WSL2's native filesystem. Operators can still access them from Windows via `\\wsl$\Ubuntu\home\user\rts\...` or more conveniently through VS Code's WSL integration.

Networking is straightforward in WSL2. The VM has its own IP on the host, and localhost is forwarded through. Per-agent network namespaces work normally inside WSL2.

Multi-distro isolation as a bonus. A power user could run your engine in its own WSL2 distro (separate from their main dev distro) for extra isolation. Probably overkill but worth noting.

File watching can be flaky across the boundary. If you use `inotify` on files in `/mnt/c/`, it doesn't reliably fire. Irrelevant if zones stay WSL-native; relevant if you try to let agents work on Windows-side files.

WSL2: zero additional work. Treat as Linux. Document "install a Linux distro via WSL2, install bubblewrap, run the engine" and you're done. Windows users get the full Linux experience.

macOS: requires a decision. Path A (Linux VM) is the right one for almost every reason that matters. Path B (native macOS implementation) is technically possible via `sandbox_init` but costs you forever and gives you a weaker version. Path C (no Mac support) is too narrow.

The practical plan:

The architecture stays pristine: one engine, one set of semantics, one test matrix, one set of behaviors to reason about. The Mac story is operational (install a VM) rather than architectural (fork the engine).

Decisions worth locking:

Want to dig into the Mac launcher design, the OrbStack-specific integration details, the VS Code Remote file-handoff story, or the WSL2 onboarding polish?

```scheme
(version 1)
(deny default)
(allow file-read* (subpath "/rts/zones/alpha"))
(allow file-write* (subpath "/rts/zones/alpha/workspace"))
(allow network-outbound (remote ip))
(allow process-fork)
...
```

- User namespaces: enabled by default on recent WSL2 kernels. If you hit issues on older versions, sudo sysctl kernel.unprivileged_userns_clone=1 fixes it.
- systemd: off by default historically, now opt-in via /etc/wsl.conf. Matters only if you choose the systemd-nspawn path; irrelevant for bubblewrap.
- Filesystem performance across the host/guest boundary: reading/writing files on the Windows side via /mnt/c/ is slow. Keep all zone contents inside the WSL2 filesystem (/home/..., /rts/...). This is a performance gotcha, not an architectural issue.
- Cgroup v2: WSL2 supports it, though it took some kernel-version flux. Modern WSL2 (2023+) is fine.
- Networking: WSL2 has its own network stack visible inside the VM. Your per-agent network namespaces behave normally. Getting traffic to Windows-host services requires the host.docker.internal-style routing, but that's only relevant if you care about Windows-side services, which you probably don't for a dev tool running inside WSL.

- Linux namespaces (mount, PID, network, user, etc.)
- cgroups
- overlayfs
- bubblewrap (no port exists and the primitives aren't there to port it to)
- bind mounts in the Linux sense (macOS has mount_nullfs on old versions, removed in newer)

- chroot (works, same limitations as Linux chroot, same escape vulnerabilities)
- sandbox_init / Seatbelt (Apple's sandbox framework, somewhat deprecated, undocumented, quirky)
- App Sandbox (for signed apps, too restrictive for your use case)
- Endpoint Security framework (for observation, not restriction; requires signed entitlement)
- DYLD interposition (library-level tricks, fragile)
- Full VMs (via Virtualization.framework on Apple Silicon, Hypervisor.framework, or third-party runtimes)

- Network isolation
- PID isolation
- Resource limits
- Clean teardown of sub-mounts (macOS chroot doesn't do namespace cleanup, because there's no namespace)

- Filesystem path isolation (agent can't navigate out by path)
- The zone-as-directory mental model
- The control-file protocol
- Everything at the application/event layer (event log, policy, map UI)

- Operator runs a small Linux VM on their Mac.
- Your engine runs inside that Linux VM.
- Agents run inside bubblewrap sandboxes inside the VM.
- The Mac is just the host; the actual work happens in Linux.

- Engine is Linux-only.
- Linux users (including WSL2) run it directly.
- Mac users run it inside a Linux VM (you provide a Lima/OrbStack recipe, or bundle a Virtualization.framework wrapper).
- One codepath, strongest semantics, lowest maintenance.
- Cost: Mac users need a VM. On Apple Silicon with OrbStack or Apple's container, this is ~30 seconds of setup and near-native performance.

- Engine abstracts over backends: bubblewrap on Linux, sandbox_init + chroot on macOS, maybe something else on BSD.
- Each backend implements the same interface: create zone, launch agent in zone, enforce policy.
- Tests run on all platforms.
- Semantics kept consistent but weaker on macOS than Linux.
- Cost: three codepaths, divergent capabilities, permanent maintenance burden on the weakest platform.

- Don't pretend to support Mac.
- Users on Mac either use WSL2 equivalent (a Linux VM they manage) or don't use the tool.
- Simplest path; narrowest audience.

1. Your engine's value is in the namespace semantics, not the platform. If you fork the engine to "do something sort of like namespaces" on macOS, you have a lesser product on a platform that your users can already run real Linux on via a VM.
2. The VM overhead on modern Macs is genuinely low. Apple Silicon + Virtualization.framework is fast enough that the VM layer is not perceptibly slow for dev workloads. OrbStack in particular boots in ~2 seconds and adds negligible overhead to filesystem and network operations.
3. Most of your users will be on Linux anyway. The developer audience for an agent-fleet orchestration tool skews hard to Linux and WSL2. Mac users are a minority, and they're used to running Linux via a VM for serious tooling.
4. Path A's Mac story is operationally clean. The Mac experience is:

Install OrbStack (or Apple container, or Lima).
container run rts-engine ... or equivalent.
Mount the operator's code directory into the VM with VirtioFS.
Everything works exactly as on Linux.

That's a tractable onboarding doc and a maintainable architecture.
5. Install OrbStack (or Apple container, or Lima).
6. container run rts-engine ... or equivalent.
7. Mount the operator's code directory into the VM with VirtioFS.
8. Everything works exactly as on Linux.
9. Path B's cost is permanent and hidden. The macOS codepath will always be weaker, always have subtle behavior differences, always need separate testing, and will be the source of bug reports that only reproduce on Macs. This is a tax you pay forever. The Mac users who care about the weaker semantics will complain; the ones who don't care would have been happy with Path A's VM.
10. Path C is too narrow. WSL2 is a huge unlock for Windows, and Linux is the natural home for this tool; but saying "sorry, no Macs" for a developer tool in 2026 is a real hit to reach. Path A gets you Mac support with ~0 extra engineering.

- Install OrbStack (or Apple container, or Lima).
- container run rts-engine ... or equivalent.
- Mount the operator's code directory into the VM with VirtioFS.
- Everything works exactly as on Linux.

- Engine (Rust): Linux-only. Uses bubblewrap, namespaces, cgroups, overlayfs. This is "the real product."
- Web UI: browser-based, platform-independent. Served by the engine.
- CLI: platform-independent thin client that talks to the engine over a Unix socket or HTTP. Runs wherever the user is, including on the Mac host (to open a browser, show desktop notifications, hand off to open for files, etc.).
- Mac launcher (optional, small): a Swift or Rust tool that:

Detects OrbStack/Lima/Docker Desktop/Apple container
Launches the engine VM if not running
Exposes the engine's socket to the host
Registers URI handlers (for rts://... URLs to open in the browser)
Handles the "click a file → open in Mac-native editor" handoff by sharing paths between VM and host via VirtioFS
- Detects OrbStack/Lima/Docker Desktop/Apple container
- Launches the engine VM if not running
- Exposes the engine's socket to the host
- Registers URI handlers (for rts://... URLs to open in the browser)
- Handles the "click a file → open in Mac-native editor" handoff by sharing paths between VM and host via VirtioFS

- Detects OrbStack/Lima/Docker Desktop/Apple container
- Launches the engine VM if not running
- Exposes the engine's socket to the host
- Registers URI handlers (for rts://... URLs to open in the browser)
- Handles the "click a file → open in Mac-native editor" handoff by sharing paths between VM and host via VirtioFS

- VirtioFS shared folder (OrbStack, Lima, Apple container all support it). The operator's ~/rts-workspace on the Mac is mounted into the VM. Files are visible and editable from both sides. Performance is good on Apple Silicon, acceptable elsewhere.
- VS Code Remote: VS Code's "Remote - WSL/SSH/Containers" extensions transparently connect to the VM. File editing happens "on the Mac" from the UX perspective, while the files live in the VM.
- SSHFS: classic. Works. Slower than VirtioFS.

1. Build engine on Linux. This covers native Linux and WSL2.
2. Recommend OrbStack or Apple's container CLI for Mac users. Document the setup.
3. Optionally, a small Mac-native companion app that automates VM setup and does file/URI handoff.
4. Accept that Mac users have one extra step. This is normal for tools in this category.

1. Mac via OrbStack/Apple-container/Lima/Docker Desktop: I'd build for OrbStack first (best UX) and Apple container second (Apple's sanctioned path), document others as "should work."
2. Mac launcher scope: nothing (users SSH into VM), lightweight (auto-starts VM + URI handler), or full (native tray app with controls)?
3. File-handoff mechanism: VirtioFS by default, VS Code Remote as the recommended editor integration?
4. WSL2 onboarding script: yes or no? I'd argue yes, it makes first-run much smoother.

## User

Ok show me the full architecture as an artifact/ mermaid diagram

---

## Assistant

I'll build a mermaid diagram showing the full architecture we've designed across this conversation. Let me load the diagram module for proper styling and then render it.

Now I'll build the architecture diagram. The system has a natural four-tier structure: operator interfaces at the top, the RTS core in the middle, the chroot/bubblewrap isolation layer, and the agent processes at the bottom.

V

Reading top-to-bottom, the architecture has six tiers, each with a single clear job:

Operator interfaces — four coexisting front-ends, all reading the same state. The 2D map is the authoring surface. The 3D monitor is the projection-from-2D for comfortable long-session watching. OS handoff drops you into your real editor via `xdg-open`. Agent TUIs (OpenCode's TUI, Claude Code's interactive mode) run alongside as peer clients — neither the RTS nor the TUI is privileged.

RTS core — the Rust service that owns the world. VegaFusion + Arrow for dataflow, an Arrow-native world state with zones/objects/edges tables, an event log as Urbit-style source of truth, live policy objects as editable files, the command dispatcher for zone operations, and the symbol renderer using milsymbol / NATO APP-6 glyphs.

Agent integration adapters — three normalizers feeding into the common schema. ACP is the emerging LSP-for-agents standard (OpenCode, Kiro, Codex all speak it). Claude Agent SDK is special-cased because its `--output-format stream-json` and permission hooks give you the richest observation and control surface. HTTP API is for agents that expose their own server (OpenCode, OpenClaw).

Isolation layer — bubblewrap as "fancier chroot," giving each agent its own mount/PID/network/IPC/user/cgroup namespaces. Zones are real directories with bind-mounted host runtime and per-agent workspaces. The commons region holds shared substrate; transfer is `mv`, share is `ln`, snapshot is overlayfs.

Agent processes — the actual coding agents, each seeing `/` as its zone. Claude Code with permission hooks routed back to the RTS, OpenCode via ACP or HTTP (optionally with its TUI coexisting), any other ACP-speaking agent.

Host platform and substrate — Linux/WSL2 natively, macOS via Linux VM (OrbStack or Apple's `container` CLI) with VirtioFS for file-handoff back to the Mac host. Below that, the real filesystem and external services the agents actually operate on.

Dashed lines mark the isolation boundaries — agents don't see the host directly; they see what bubblewrap mounts into their zone.

A few things worth noting about what's not explicit in the diagram but is implied by it:

This is the full picture we've built across the conversation. Each block has a design rationale rooted in earlier turns — the NATO symbology choice enables the low-mark-count assumption that makes VegaFusion viable; the no-fog-of-war choice justifies full observability across every layer; the zones-as-filesystem choice collapses the permission model into kernel-enforced isolation; the Plan 9 influence shows up in the `/control/` file protocol; the Urbit influence shows up in the event log as source of truth.

Want me to produce a second diagram zoomed into any one layer (the adapter internals, the chroot zone structure, the event flow for a single tool call), or a sequence diagram showing what happens when an operator issues a zone-transfer command end-to-end?

1. Data flow is mostly downward for commands, upward for events. Operator issues a zone op → dispatcher → filesystem operation in the isolation layer → agent sees the change on its next tool call. Agent emits a tool call → adapter normalizes it → event log → VegaFusion → rendered as an edge on the map.
2. The adapter layer is the narrow waist. Every agent framework produces slightly different events; they all normalize to (agent_id, verb, object, timestamp, metadata). Adding a new agent type is writing an adapter, not changing anything else.
3. The event log is the source of truth. World state is a cache derived from it. Crash recovery is replay. Time-scrubbing is reading earlier log positions. Arrow tables are an index, not a database.
4. Policy lives as files inside each zone. The RTS reads and writes /control/policy in each chroot. Agents read their own policy to know their scope. Changes propagate live without restart.

## User

If we add a prefix command to a standard shell for example "magic" can we control the subsequent commands wrt to the os?

---

## Assistant

Yes, and this is a genuinely useful thing to do — it's the right control-plane primitive for several of the problems we've been circling, particularly "how does the operator issue zone-scoped commands without leaving their shell." Let me work through what's possible, because the answer depends on what you mean by "control," and the options form a ladder similar to the chroot/bubblewrap one.

When you type `magic do-something foo bar` at a shell, `magic` is just an executable on `$PATH` that receives `do-something foo bar` as its argv. Whether `magic` can "control" what follows depends on whether "what follows" is:

Case 2 is where most of the value lives and where your intuition probably is. Case 3 is possible but requires different mechanisms. Let me walk all three because the design choice between them matters.

`magic create-zone alpha`, `magic list-zones`, `magic transfer foo.py alpha beta` — these are just subcommands. You write `magic` in Rust, it parses argv, does the thing. Standard CLI pattern, same as `git`, `docker`, `kubectl`. No OS magic required.

This is table stakes and you'd want it regardless.

Pattern: `magic <zone> <any command>` runs that command inside the zone. Examples:

This is structurally identical to how `sudo`, `nice`, `time`, `taskset`, `chroot`, `docker run`, `ssh`, and `nsenter` work. You write a command that sets up an environment and then execs the target. Everything you've built into the chroot/bubblewrap engine becomes available from the shell with one-word ergonomics.

Implementation in Rust:

Using `exec` (not `spawn`) means `magic` becomes `bwrap`, which becomes the command. Your shell sees it as a single process that replaces itself twice; exit codes, signals, stdin/stdout, terminal control — everything flows correctly. Ctrl-C works. Pipes work. Redirect works. It's transparent.

This is the most useful single thing `magic` can do. Every other shell-level feature is nice-to-have; this one turns the zone model from an RTS-internal concept into a first-class shell primitive.

Concrete properties:

This is the single most useful thing to build. The whole RTS becomes shell-scriptable.

What if you want `magic` to set state that persists in your shell after it returns? E.g., "once I've run `magic activate alpha`, every subsequent command in this shell runs inside zone alpha until I type `magic deactivate`"?

This is fundamentally harder because a child process cannot modify its parent's environment. When `magic` exits, any cwd change, env var set, namespace unshare, or alias defined inside `magic` vanishes. The shell is the parent; `magic` is the child. Unix process model is explicit: children can't reach up.

The mechanisms that exist to work around this, ranked by how much they demand from the user:

`magic` is defined as a shell function rather than a standalone binary, so it executes in the shell's own process and can modify shell state.

Combine with a `preexec` hook (bash `trap DEBUG`, zsh `preexec`, fish `fish_preexec`) that wraps every subsequent command in the zone if `$MAGIC_ZONE` is set:

Effectively this gives you: "I've activated zone alpha; now every command I type gets routed through `magic alpha ...` transparently."

Pros: real session-level control. Persists across commands. Standard shell primitives.
Cons: shell-specific (bash vs zsh vs fish each need their own); users have to source a setup file; debugging is fiddly; `preexec` has edge cases (pipelines, subshells).

Prior art: `conda activate`, `nvm use`, `direnv`, `pyenv` — all of these are shell functions that manipulate shell state because they can't be done from a child process alone. They all ship a "one-liner" the user adds to their shellrc. This is the standard pattern and users are accustomed to it.

`magic activate alpha` spawns a new shell as a child with `MAGIC_ZONE=alpha` set and a customized prompt, and you work in that subshell. `exit` drops back to your original shell with the variable unset.

This is what `nix-shell` does. Simpler than preexec hooks, but you pay a subshell per activation.

Pros: simpler than A, no preexec hook needed, works identically across shells if you spawn a known shell.
Cons: nested shells can confuse users, history can get complicated, each activation is a new process.

`magic alpha` launches a new shell inside zone alpha (via bubblewrap), and you just use that shell as your working environment. This is the thing I already described as "`magic alpha bash`" — it's Mechanism C, but framed as "this is your primary workflow."

The user opens a terminal, types `magic alpha` (or their terminal launches it automatically), and is now inside zone alpha for the lifetime of that terminal. Multiple zones = multiple terminals. tmux/screen with per-zone panes.

Pros: simplest mental model — a terminal is a zone. No session-tracking magic.
Cons: requires the operator to think about which terminal is which zone, forces multi-terminal or multi-pane workflow.

You modify the `$PATH` so that common commands (`bash`, `node`, `python`, `git`) resolve first to wrapper scripts that re-exec through `magic`. Extreme; fragile; usually the wrong idea. Mentioned for completeness because people sometimes reach for it — don't.

Build Case 2 first and call it done for 80% of what you want. magic <zone> <cmd> is the primary shell interface. Everything composable, no session state, no shell hooks.

Add Mechanism A (shell function with preexec hook) as an optional opt-in for users who want the `conda activate`-style experience. Ship a `magic.bash`, `magic.zsh`, `magic.fish` that users source. Keep the core tool standalone.

Don't build C (replace shell) as the primary interface because it forces a workflow. Do offer `magic <zone>` with no trailing command as a shortcut for "drop me into a shell in this zone" — users who want C can use that.

Don't build D. Users who want transparent interception should use A.

You asked specifically about controlling "subsequent commands wrt the OS." Given Case 2 as the vehicle, here's what controls you can realistically apply to any command run via `magic <zone> <cmd>`:

Filesystem namespace (via bubblewrap/chroot): the command sees only the zone's filesystem. This is the core thing.

Process namespace: the command is PID 1 inside the zone, sees no host processes.

Network namespace: the command has its own network stack. You can route it through a proxy, firewall it, give it no network at all, or share the host's network.

User/UID: the command runs as a non-root user on the host even if the wrapper was launched with privileges, or runs as "root" inside a user namespace while being unprivileged outside.

Cgroup limits: CPU, memory, I/O ceilings per invocation.

Environment variables: you can whitelist/blacklist, inject zone-specific secrets, strip potentially-leaky env (`SSH_AUTH_SOCK`, `AWS_*`).

Working directory: auto-chdir to the zone's workspace.

File descriptors: close everything except stdin/stdout/stderr, or specifically pass others (useful for the `/control/` files protocol).

Signal handling: wrap the command so you can interrupt cleanly (SIGTERM before SIGKILL, etc.).

Seccomp filters: deny specific syscalls. Overkill unless you have a specific threat.

Auditing: log every `magic` invocation to the event log. The RTS now sees operator activity as events on the map too — the operator becomes a pseudo-agent, which is the "operator as peer in the namespace" property from the Plan 9 turn.

All of this can be configured per-zone via the zone's policy file. `magic alpha grep foo .` reads alpha's policy, sets up the environment accordingly, execs.

A non-exhaustive list of subcommands that would be natural:

This is essentially a complete operator CLI. For any given user session, the web UI and the CLI should be interchangeable — whatever you can click in the map, you can type via `magic`. This matters because power users live in the shell and you want to reward that.

magic is an RTS client. It connects to the RTS daemon (over a Unix socket, typically `/run/rts/sock` or `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/rts/sock`) and issues RPCs. The RTS handles the actual zone setup, policy lookup, event logging. `magic` is thin — it translates shell ergonomics into RTS API calls.

Every invocation emits events. `magic alpha grep foo .` logs an event: "operator ran `grep foo .` in zone alpha at T." The RTS sees it. The map shows an edge from the "operator" pseudo-agent to whatever files grep touched (detectable via the zone's file-event stream or via `grep`'s exit and the file-access timestamps). The operator's work becomes data alongside the agents' work.

Shell completion. Zone names, agent IDs, common subcommands all completable. `magic alpha <tab>` completes files inside alpha's workspace. This is quality-of-life but significantly raises the tool's usability.

magic respects zone policy for itself too. If the operator is fenced out of a zone (rare, but you might want a "read-only observation" mode for paired operators), `magic` enforces it. This is where your "operator as pseudo-agent" model pays off — policy applies uniformly.

One thing worth being explicit about: in Case 2, `magic` controls each invocation of a command, not the subsequent commands as session state. The preexec hook in Mechanism A bridges this gap — once activated, every subsequent command you type in that shell runs through `magic` automatically.

The operator-facing experience with A + preexec:

The subtle case is shell builtins (`cd`, `export`, `alias`). They can't be wrapped because they execute in the shell itself, not as subprocesses. This is usually fine because (a) `cd` inside a zone still makes sense (changes your current zone-relative directory), and (b) `export` setting an env var affects your host shell, which is usually what you want. But it's an asymmetry worth knowing about.

If you want complete interception including builtins, you'd have to replace the shell — back to Mechanism C. A/preexec is 95%; C is 100% with more friction.

A few prior-art tools worth knowing about because they solve related problems:

Mental model: magic is to your RTS as kubectl is to Kubernetes. It's the shell-side control plane. The web UI and the CLI are sibling clients of the same daemon.

A few things to decide early because they shape the UX:

Transport to the RTS daemon: Unix socket is correct. HTTP is fine but socket is more Unix-native and gets you `SO_PEERCRED` for authentication (the RTS knows which Unix user invoked `magic`).

Authentication/authorization: for single-operator personal tools, `SO_PEERCRED` is enough — only the owning user can connect. For multi-operator, you'd need real auth; that's a later concern.

Command argument escaping: `magic alpha bash -c 'echo $HOME'` needs to preserve the quoting correctly. Shell quoting is notoriously easy to mess up. Use `exec` (not shell interpolation) and pass argv as an array, not a string. This is a class of bug to actively avoid.

Signal forwarding: Ctrl-C in the terminal should interrupt the inner command, not just `magic`. `exec`ing bubblewrap handles this correctly; don't insert Rust-level signal handlers between bubblewrap and the command.

Exit codes: the inner command's exit code should propagate. `exec` handles this automatically.

stdin/stdout/stderr/TTY: interactive commands need a TTY. bubblewrap passes the parent's TTY through by default. Non-interactive commands (piped input/output) should also work. Test both early — TTY bugs are insidious.

Completion: generate shell completion (bash, zsh, fish) at install time via something like `clap_complete`. Users expect tab-completion in 2026.

Updating the diagram from last turn mentally: `magic` is a new block in the "Operator interfaces" tier, sibling to 2D map view, 3D monitor, and agent TUIs. It talks to the RTS core via the same API the map talks to. It reads zone specs from the world state, emits events to the event log, and executes commands through the isolation layer.

The full set of operator interfaces becomes:

Five peers, all clients of the same daemon, all doing the same operations through different surfaces. That's architecturally clean and it matches how mature tools (Docker, Kubernetes, Nix) actually end up structured.

Week 1 of CLI work, on top of the engine that already exists:

That's a week of focused work and gives you full CLI coverage. The engine from previous turns powers all of it; `magic` is just a well-shaped client.

Decisions worth locking:

Want to dig into the preexec hook design specifically, the socket protocol between `magic` and the daemon, the completion-generation story, or the event schema for operator-as-pseudo-agent?

```javascript
magic alpha grep -r TODO src/
magic alpha vim src/foo.py
magic beta npm install
magic shared ls
magic alpha bash    # drop into an interactive shell inside the zone
```

```rust
fn main() {
    let args: Vec<String> = std::env::args().collect();
    let zone = &args[1];
    let command = &args[2..];
    
    let zone_spec = load_zone(zone)?;
    let bwrap_args = build_bwrap_args(&zone_spec, command);
    
    // Replace this process with bwrap
    Command::new("bwrap").args(&bwrap_args).exec();
}
```

```bash
magic() {
    case "$1" in
        activate)
            export MAGIC_ZONE="$2"
            export PS1="(magic:$2) $PS1"
            ;;
        deactivate)
            unset MAGIC_ZONE
            ;;
        *)
            command magic "$@"  # fall through to real binary
            ;;
    esac
}
```

```bash
preexec() {
    [[ -n "$MAGIC_ZONE" && "$1" != magic* ]] && exec magic "$MAGIC_ZONE" "$@"
}
```

```bash
magic() {
    if [[ "$1" == activate ]]; then
        MAGIC_ZONE="$2" bash --rcfile <(echo "PS1='(magic:$2) $PS1'; source ~/.bashrc")
    fi
}
```

```javascript
$ magic activate alpha
(magic:alpha) $ grep -r TODO .     # runs inside zone alpha
(magic:alpha) $ vim src/foo.py      # edits inside zone alpha
(magic:alpha) $ npm test            # runs inside zone alpha
(magic:alpha) $ cd ..               # this is a shell builtin, doesn't go through magic
(magic:alpha) $ ls                  # this still runs in zone alpha
(magic:alpha) $ magic deactivate
$ ls                                # back to host
```

1. Arguments to magic itself (trivial — magic reads them and does whatever)
2. A subsequent command that magic executes (magic alpha grep foo . → magic runs grep foo . in a controlled environment)
3. Commands you'll type after magic in the same shell session (this is the hard and interesting case)

- Pipes cross zones cleanly: magic alpha cat foo.py | magic beta sh -c 'cat > imported.py' transfers content by piping through the zone boundary. The zones don't share a filesystem, but they share stdin/stdout because that's how Unix pipes work. This is a nice property — cross-zone communication through the shell falls out for free.
- Environment passes through: your shell's env vars reach the inner command (you can filter if you want).
- Interactive shells work: magic alpha bash drops you into a bash inside the zone. From the operator's perspective, it's like SSH'ing into a container, but without SSH. Exit to return to the host shell.
- Scripts compose: magic alpha make test && magic alpha git commit is natural.

- magic zone create <name> / zone destroy <name> / zone list
- magic transfer <path> <from-zone> <to-zone>
- magic share <path> <zone-a> <zone-b> (hard-link)
- magic snapshot <zone> / magic rollback <zone> <snap>
- magic agent spawn <kind> <zone> (launches Claude Code / OpenCode in a zone)
- magic agent kill <id> / magic agent pause <id> / magic agent resume <id>
- magic agent tail <id> (follow event stream)
- magic agent send <id> <message> (inject prompt)
- magic policy edit <zone> (opens the policy file in $EDITOR)
- magic replay <zone> --from <time> (time-scrubbing from CLI)
- magic diff <zone> (git-style diff of what's changed in a zone)
- magic watch (tail the event log, human-readable)
- magic map (open the web UI in a browser)
- magic <zone> <cmd> (the prefix-launcher from Case 2)
- magic <zone> (no command → drop into shell in that zone)

- nsenter: enters existing Linux namespaces. Not a launcher; operates on running processes. Useful primitive but not the user-facing shape you want.
- firejail: "bubblewrap but older and with a security focus." Could be an alternative to bubblewrap; I still prefer bwrap.
- systemd-run: spawn a command in a transient systemd unit with cgroup limits, name it, supervise it. Complementary to magic; magic could use systemd-run under the hood for resource management.
- toolbx / distrobox: Fedora/Ubuntu tools for running commands in containers with tight host integration. Similar philosophical shape ("run this command in an isolated env") — worth reading their code for UX decisions.
- direnv: perfect example of Mechanism A done well. Activates env per-directory via shell hook. Users trust and love it.
- nix-shell / nix develop: Mechanism B done well. Spawns a subshell with specific deps available. Users are very comfortable with this pattern.
- kubectl exec: prefix-launcher for Kubernetes pods. Ergonomically the closest match to what magic <zone> <cmd> is doing.

- Map view: visual, strategic, zone-oriented
- 3D monitor: ambient, embodied, long-session
- OS handoff: click-through to existing editors
- Agent TUIs: direct agent interaction
- magic CLI: shell-native, scriptable, keyboard-first

1. Shell-builtin opacity: as noted, cd, export, etc. can't be intercepted without shell-level integration. This is a known limitation of any command-wrapper tool; users generally understand it.
2. Non-shell invocations bypass magic: if a script spawns a command directly via exec rather than calling magic, it runs on the host, not in a zone. For scripts to respect zones, they need to use magic explicitly. You can partially mitigate with LD_PRELOAD tricks but don't — that path leads to pain.
3. Performance overhead per invocation: bubblewrap setup is ~5-30ms. For most commands this is negligible; for very fast commands in tight loops (find ... -exec), it adds up. An operator running a big find loop might notice. Rarely a practical issue but worth knowing.
4. The preexec hook is opinionated: some users have their own preexec logic (time tracking, command logging, custom prompts). magic's hook needs to play nicely. The pattern is to have magic activate install the hook and magic deactivate remove it, rather than always-on.
5. Windows/WSL story: magic runs in WSL natively. From PowerShell/cmd on the Windows side, it's "run wsl magic <...>". Not as clean as native Linux but acceptable. Windows-native operators can use the web UI primarily.

1. Core binary magic with Case 2 as primary: magic <zone> <command> execs via bubblewrap. magic <zone> with no command drops into $SHELL.
2. Socket client talks to RTS daemon. Zone resolution and policy lookup happen server-side.
3. Subcommands for the common operations: zone create/list/destroy, agent spawn/kill/tail, policy edit, transfer, share, snapshot.
4. Completion scripts for bash/zsh/fish.
5. Optional shell integration (Mechanism A) as a sourceable file, documented as opt-in.

1. magic or another name? "magic" is cute but generic — is there something more specific that fits the project's identity? rts, zone, something invented?
2. Prefix-launcher vs. activate-based as the primary interface — I'd argue prefix-launcher, with activate as an optional convenience.
3. Socket location: $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/rts/sock is the standard choice.
4. Shell integration scope: bash+zsh only, or fish included? (Fish has a fairly different preexec model.)
5. How much of the operator's shell activity gets logged to the event log? Every magic invocation, or just ones that modify state? Privacy vs. observability trade.

---

## User

Can we use SSH to handle cross domain stuff?

---

## Assistant

Yes — SSH is actually a very good fit here, and it's underused as a namespace bridge even though that's exactly what it was built to be. The reason it works for cross-domain stuff in your architecture is that SSH already solves the same problems you'd otherwise solve from scratch: authenticated identity, multiplexed channels, port and stream forwarding, agent forwarding, cross-machine process control. You're getting a battle-tested control plane for free if you use it as the substrate rather than something you bolt on later.

Let me work through where SSH fits, what it does well, where it's not the right answer, and what the architecture looks like with it as a first-class citizen.

Worth being precise because "cross-domain" can mean several things and SSH addresses them differently:

Each is a different problem; SSH solves 2, 3, and 5 cleanly, helps with 4, and is wrong for 1. Let me take them in order.

This is the strongest case. If your RTS engine and the agents it controls are on a different machine than the operator (which they often will be — beefier hardware, persistent uptime, separation of dev laptop from work environment), SSH is the right transport for everything that needs to cross machines.

Specifically:

Operator's magic CLI talks to a remote daemon. Instead of a local Unix socket, the CLI's transport is SSH:

becomes, transparently:
