## User

what about VSCODE instead of the browser

---

## Assistant

Now we're talking — and this might genuinely be the right answer. Let me work through it carefully because the implications are larger than they look.

VS Code as the host for your chat (and possibly more) changes the project in ways that are mostly good, and one of the framings that's been latent in this conversation finally has a clean home: developers already live in VS Code. If the chat is in VS Code, it's not "another tab" or "another app" — it's where they already are.

VS Code is, technically, a custom Electron app, but the relevant fact for you is its extension API, which is one of the richest in any developer tool. You can build:

For your project, several of these are immediately interesting in ways that are frankly hard to replicate elsewhere.

A few specific reasons this is a stronger fit than I gave it credit for in earlier turns:

The operator already has VS Code open. For the developer audience, this is true 80%+ of the time. The chat being in VS Code means it's accessible without context switching. The mental cost of using your tool drops to near zero.

File operations have a native home. The single biggest workflow problem with chat-based agent tools is "agent did something to a file; how do I look at it." In a browser tab, this is `xdg-open` and a tab switch. In VS Code, it's already in the editor. The agent edited `auth.py`? It's right there. Click any file path in the chat, it opens in the same window. The diff between two states is `vscode.diff()`, native, beautiful.

Diff rendering is free and excellent. VS Code's diff editor is genuinely one of the best around. You don't have to build it; you just call it. Permission requests with diff previews become "open VS Code's diff viewer in a temporary tab" — a one-line API call versus a serious frontend project.

The Webview API is mature. Webviews can do anything an HTML page can do, including websockets, streaming, full UI customization. The chat as a webview is essentially the same code as the chat as a browser tab. Your existing two-day chat plan ports directly.

Multi-pane layout is solved. VS Code has split panels, sidebars, panels at the bottom — you can place the chat where it makes sense. The map view (if you build one) goes in a different panel. The artifacts view goes in a third. The layout that took a lot of design work in browser-land is built in.

Workspace context is rich. VS Code knows what file is open, what's selected, what's been edited recently, what's in git status. Your chat can use any of this. "What's the agent doing in the file I'm currently looking at" is a natural query.

Distribution is solved. VS Code Marketplace. Operators install with one click. Updates auto-flow. No installer engineering. No "is this notarized for Mac" headaches.

Cross-platform is solved. VS Code runs on Linux, macOS, Windows. Your extension does too, automatically. The macOS pain from the platform turn dissolves — VS Code is the cross-platform layer.

Remote workflows are solved. VS Code Remote-SSH lets the operator on a Mac connect to a Linux server with their full editor experience. Your extension runs on the remote side, alongside the daemon. The operator's local VS Code is just the rendering surface. This is enormous for the macOS / multi-host story from earlier turns — it collapses into "use VS Code Remote-SSH" as the answer.

Familiar UX patterns. Operators don't have to learn a new keyboard model, command palette, or file navigation. Yours just slots into theirs.

That's a lot of capability that you'd otherwise build, get for free.

A reasonable v1 architecture as a VS Code extension:

Sidebar (Activity Bar) icon: clicking it opens your view container.

Sidebar tree views:

Webview panel for the chat: opens when an agent is selected. Hosted as a VS Code webview. The chat UI is your existing web frontend, embedded.

Webview panel for the map (optional, for v2): same pattern, different webview.

Commands:

Status bar item: shows count of active agents, pending permission requests. Click to focus the chat.

File decorators: files currently being edited by an agent get a badge. Hover shows "edited by alpha 30s ago."

Diff integration: when a permission request involves a file edit, clicking "Preview" opens VS Code's native diff between current and proposed state.

Terminal integration: "RTS: Open agent terminal" spawns a terminal that's attached to the agent's stderr/log stream.

Notifications: pending permission requests show as VS Code notifications with action buttons. The operator approves/denies without opening the chat panel.

Quick pick UI: for things like "send to agent..." after right-clicking a selection, VS Code's native quick pick gives you a polished selector.

This is substantially more polished than a browser-tab UI for the same effort. Every one of these features takes minutes-to-hours to wire up via the VS Code API, versus days-to-weeks to build well in a browser app.

VS Code extensions have real costs worth being clear about:

Webview restrictions: webviews run in a more restricted environment than a normal browser tab. Some APIs aren't available; CSP is strict. For most chat UIs this isn't an issue, but you can't, say, embed arbitrary iframes from random origins. Your chat code may need small adjustments from a regular web app version.

Communication between webview and extension is message-passing. The webview can't call directly into the extension; they exchange messages via `postMessage`. Slightly more ceremonious than a single-process app but well-documented.

Versioning across VS Code releases: the API is stable but evolves. You'll occasionally update for new VS Code versions, deprecations, etc. Not a heavy burden but real.

Bundle size matters: the marketplace expects extensions to be reasonable in size. If your webview's frontend is heavy (large libraries), you'll want to be mindful. Probably not an issue for a focused chat UI.

Marketplace policies: getting listed in the VS Code marketplace requires meeting publishing requirements. Mostly straightforward but worth knowing.

Some users use VSCodium, Cursor, Windsurf, Theia, or other forks: most extensions Just Work but occasionally there are quirks. Your extension can target the OpenVSX registry to reach VSCodium users.

Not every operator uses VS Code: vim/emacs/JetBrains/Zed users won't have it. This is the big trade-off.

That last one matters and deserves more thought.

The honest take: a lot of developers use VS Code. Recent surveys put it at 70%+ of the developer market. For a v1 hypothesis test, targeting that majority is fine. Other editors can be addressed later.

But there's a deeper architectural question: should your tool be coupled to VS Code, or should VS Code be one of several frontends?

Option A: VS Code-coupled. The extension is the primary product. Other frontends are aspirational.

Option B: Daemon-centered, multiple frontends. The daemon is the product; VS Code is one client; browser is another; CLI is a third. Each is a separate but compatible interface.

The architectural answer is clearly B. The product question is which to build first.

For v1, the answer is VS Code first, because:

The browser tab + bookmarklet plan from the previous turn is a fine fallback for non-VS-Code users in v2, but v1 doesn't need it.

Significant changes worth being explicit about:

The chat UI moves from "web app served by daemon" to "webview inside VS Code extension." The actual chat code is mostly the same — HTML, CSS, JS, marked for markdown, shiki for code. But it's hosted in a webview, not a browser tab.

The daemon is unchanged. The extension talks to the daemon via Unix socket or HTTP on localhost. Same protocol the browser would use.

File operations get vastly better. xdg-open is replaced with `vscode.openTextDocument(uri)`. Diff previews are `vscode.diff()`. File decorations are part of VS Code. The "click a file path to open in editor" UX is native.

Permission UX gets better. Notifications can be VS Code notifications with action buttons, not in-chat blocks. Or both — chat shows the request inline with diff preview as a webview component, plus a notification that pops up if VS Code isn't focused on the chat panel.

The map UI question changes. Earlier turns assumed a browser-rendered SVG/canvas map. In VS Code, you can render the map in a webview just as easily — same p5.js or whatever. Or you can use VS Code's tree views and editor decorations as a more native map: the file explorer becomes the zone view, with badges on files showing agent activity. This is less ambitious visually but possibly more useful as a tool.

Multi-platform is free. The extension runs on whatever VS Code runs on. macOS, Linux, Windows, all the same code.

Remote workflows are free. The extension runs on the remote (where the daemon is) and renders on the local (where the operator is). Cross-host story is solved by VS Code Remote-SSH.

Distribution becomes "publish to marketplace" rather than "build installers and packagers."

The two-day chat plan from a few turns back ports almost directly. The work shifts:

That's roughly the same two days, with a meaningfully better v1 result.

Concretely, the v1 deliverable as a VS Code extension:

Extension manifest (package.json):

Extension code (TypeScript):

Chat webview (HTML/JS):

Integration glue:

Daemon side:

Specific things that would be hard or impossible in a browser version, and trivial in VS Code:

Permission previews use VS Code's diff editor. Click "Preview" on a permission request, and a real diff opens in a real editor pane. Operator can scroll, comment, jump to definition, read the diff like they would any code. Permission decisions become informed in a way that an inline diff in a chat bubble can't match.

Agent edits stream into the editor in real time. When an agent is editing a file, the operator can have that file open in the editor. They watch the agent type. They can intervene mid-edit. This is dramatic — the boundary between "watching the agent" and "watching their own typing" dissolves.

Workspace context flows naturally. The operator's currently-open file becomes a contextual signal. "Agent, look at what I'm currently looking at." Selection becomes input ("explain this"). The right-click menu becomes a control surface ("send this code to the agent for refactoring").

Source control integration. When agents make commits, they show up in VS Code's git pane. Branches per agent (Plan 9-style zones, taken to git) become visible in the source control sidebar. Diff between agent's branch and main is a click.

Terminal integration. Each agent's stderr/log stream can become a VS Code terminal. Operator sees agent activity in the same terminal panel they use for everything else. `Ctrl+`` toggles it.

Multi-window collaboration. Operator opens multiple VS Code windows for multiple zones; each has its own RTS extension instance; they coordinate via the shared daemon. Multi-monitor workflow becomes natural.

These aren't gimmicks. Each addresses a real friction in current agent tools. None of them is replicable in a browser-tab chat without enormous effort.

VS Code is, increasingly, the de facto agent UX layer. Cursor is a fork of VS Code. Windsurf is a fork of VS Code. Continue is a VS Code extension. The Claude Agent SDK has VS Code integration in its examples. GitHub Copilot lives in VS Code. The trend is unmistakable: agent tooling is colonizing VS Code, not the browser.

If your tool lives in VS Code, it's with the trend, not against it. Operators can use yours alongside Copilot, alongside Continue, alongside Cursor's built-in features (if they use Cursor). It's part of an ecosystem of editor-integrated tooling.

If your tool lives only in the browser, it's competing with the entire editor-as-agent-host trend. You can win — Linear, Notion, Figma all live in browsers — but you're swimming upstream, not downstream.

Worth being clear:

These are real but mostly addressable in v2+ if you find specific cases that need them.

Stepping back, what VS Code-first really gives you is a way to ship a v1 that has dramatically more polish per hour of work than any other frontend choice.

The cost-of-quality curve for a browser web app is something like:

The cost-of-quality curve for a VS Code extension:

You get the benefits of VS Code's years of UX investment for nothing.

The other framing: VS Code is, in a real sense, the operator's workstation. They live there. Your tool, if it lives there too, becomes part of how they work. If it lives in a browser tab, it's something they switch to. The integration depth shapes whether the tool becomes a habit or stays an extra.

Worth pulling on this because it's a fork in the road:

Option A: chat is a webview (HTML/JS inside a panel).

Option B: chat is built from native VS Code UI primitives.

For a chat specifically, A is the only realistic answer. VS Code's native UI primitives don't have a "scrolling chat history with streaming text and inline interactive elements" component. You'd be fighting the API.

For other surfaces — zone tree, agent list, command palette — B is the natural answer. Use the native tree view for the agent list, the native quick pick for selectors, the native notification system for alerts.

So the v1 is: chat as webview, sidebar/list/notification stuff as native VS Code. Best of both worlds.

If this concern resonates, the answer is to keep the daemon strictly editor-agnostic. The daemon speaks a generic protocol (websocket + JSON or whatever). The VS Code extension is a client. Any future client (browser, CLI, JetBrains plugin, vim plugin) speaks the same protocol.

Then if VS Code changes its API hostilely, or if a substantial part of your audience turns out to use Zed, you build a Zed extension. Same daemon, different frontend. The v1 work is preserved for the daemon, even if the VS Code part has to be reworked.

This is the standard architecture for tools that target IDEs: the language server protocol model, where the protocol is editor-agnostic and editors implement clients. You're effectively defining your own protocol for "agent oversight server" and VS Code is the first client.

To make the comparison concrete:

The comparison strongly favors VS Code for the developer audience.

Switch v1 to a VS Code extension. Drop the browser-tab plan entirely for now. Plan for the daemon to be editor-agnostic so other frontends are possible later, but build only the VS Code extension first.

Specific commitments:

The two-day chat plan becomes the two-day extension plan. Foundation: extension scaffolding, daemon connection, basic chat webview, permission flow with diff. Polish: streaming, tool call rendering, file decorators, terminal integration, native UI bits.

VS Code-first is probably the right call. The development velocity is dramatically higher because so much UX is already solved. The quality ceiling is higher because native integration is impossible in a browser. The audience match is strong because developers live in VS Code. The cross-platform and multi-host stories collapse into "use VS Code's existing infrastructure."

The only real cost is coupling to one editor, mitigated by keeping the daemon protocol generic so other editor plugins can be built later. For a v1 hypothesis test, that's a fine trade.

This is one of those cases where the seemingly cheaper option (browser tab) is actually more expensive, because the polish-per-hour is so much lower. VS Code looks like more investment but gets you a meaningfully better v1 in similar time.

Decisions worth locking:

Want to dig into the extension's specific API surface, the webview-to-extension messaging design, the native-UI vs. webview boundary, or the file-decorator design for showing agent activity in the explorer?

- Webview panels: HTML/CSS/JS UIs that render as panels, sidebars, or full editor tabs. Your chat goes here.
- Tree views: native VS Code lists in the sidebar. Zone list, agent list, conversation list.
- Commands: keyboard-shortcut-bindable actions registered in the command palette.
- Status bar items: persistent indicators at the bottom.
- Notifications: native VS Code notifications.
- File decorators: badges or colors on files in the file explorer (e.g., "agent currently editing this").
- Diff editors: programmatically open VS Code's built-in diff viewer.
- Terminal: spawn and control terminal instances.
- Workspace API: read and respond to file changes, edits, selections, language events.
- Source control API: integrate with git operations.
- Authentication providers: hook into VS Code's auth system.
- Language server protocol: speak LSP for code intelligence.

- Zones (with status indicators)
- Agents (grouped by zone, with current activity)
- Recent events (or pinned conversations)

- "RTS: Show Map"
- "RTS: Open Conversation with [agent]"
- "RTS: Approve Pending Permission" / "RTS: Deny Pending Permission"

1. It's where 70%+ of your audience is
2. The development cost is dramatically lower (rich API)
3. The UX quality ceiling is dramatically higher (native integration)
4. Distribution is solved
5. You learn what features actually matter from real operator use
6. Other frontends become much easier to build with that knowledge

- ~4 hours: native integrations (file open, diff preview, notifications)

- Sidebar view container with three views: Zones, Agents, Recent Activity

- On activation: connect to daemon via websocket, populate views
- Tree data providers for the three views
- Command handlers for spawn, kill, message, etc.
- Webview provider for the chat panel
- Notification dispatcher for permission requests
- Status bar item for activity indication
- File decoration provider for "agent is editing this"

- Same as your two-day plan but hosted in a webview
- Posts messages to the extension for actions (send, approve, deny)
- Receives streaming updates from the extension via posted messages
- Renders markdown, code, diffs (or links to native diff viewer)

- Right-click on file in explorer → "Open in Agent's Conversation"
- Status bar shows pending approval count; clicking opens chat panel

- Unchanged from earlier plans
- Still Rust, still SQLite, still spawning agents in chroots
- Speaks websocket to the extension (which is the only client for v1)

- Vim, Emacs, JetBrains, Zed, Helix users are excluded in v1. They're not the majority but they're influential. Building for them later means a separate plugin per editor, or a CLI-based interface that they can integrate themselves.
- Non-developer audiences are excluded entirely. If you ever want to expand beyond developers — to data analysts, writers, researchers using LLMs — VS Code isn't where they are. But for v1's hypothesis ("better tool for developer agent oversight"), this is fine.
- You're tied to VS Code's release cadence and API decisions. You inherit their constraints. This is mostly fine but occasionally bites.
- The "browser tab" mental model goes away. Some people prefer browser tabs. They might find VS Code as a chat host weird. Probably a small minority of your audience.
- No PWA story, no bookmarklet, no "send page to chat from the browser". The browser-integration ideas from earlier turns become extension-to-extension stuff (a separate Chrome extension that talks to the same daemon). More work than a bookmarklet.

- 2-8 days: serious tool feel
- 2-8 weeks: extends into new feature territory

- Cons: not visually "native" — looks like an embedded webpage

- Cons: very limited UI vocabulary — VS Code's API isn't designed for chat UIs; streaming text, custom styling, complex layouts are hard or impossible

1. Daemon protocol is generic: websocket + JSON, not VS Code-specific. Document it as the future API.
2. VS Code extension is the only v1 client.
3. Chat is a webview inside a VS Code panel. Native integrations everywhere else.
4. Diff previews use VS Code's native diff editor, not custom rendering.
5. Permission notifications are native VS Code notifications, with inline blocks in the chat as backup.
6. Zone and agent lists are native tree views in the activity bar.
7. File decorators show agent activity on files in the explorer.
8. Right-click context menus for "send to agent," "open in agent conversation."
9. Distribution via VS Code Marketplace for v1.
10. OpenVSX publishing for VSCodium/Cursor users in v1 or v1.1.

1. VS Code extension as v1 frontend. Browser tab plan is shelved.
2. Daemon stays editor-agnostic to permit future clients.
3. Chat is webview, surrounding UI is native.
4. Lean hard on VS Code's native capabilities (diff editor, notifications, terminals, file decorators) rather than reimplementing them.
5. Marketplace distribution. Don't worry about packaging.
6. Plan for OpenVSX so VSCodium and Cursor users are reachable in v1.x.
7. Other editors (Zed, JetBrains, vim) are v2+ at earliest.

---

## User

Can we execute aggressive interventions into other plugins with vscode?

---

## Assistant

This is a sharp question and the answer is more nuanced than a yes or no. The short version: VS Code extensions are explicitly designed to not aggressively intervene in each other, and the API enforces this. But there's real territory between "do nothing" and "modify another plugin's code," and that territory contains some genuinely useful capabilities, plus some workarounds for cases where the official API isn't enough.

Let me work through what's possible, what isn't, and what's relevant for your project specifically — because "aggressive interventions" can mean very different things depending on what you're trying to accomplish.

VS Code extensions run in a separate process called the "extension host" — a Node.js process that's distinct from the editor's renderer process. All extensions in a workspace share this extension host process, but the API enforces hard boundaries between them:

This is a security and stability design choice, and it's what allows the marketplace to function. If extensions could freely intervene in each other, every extension you install would be a potential vector for breaking every other extension. The model is deliberately restrictive.

So the first thing to be clear about: VS Code does not give you an "aggressive intervention" API by design. Anything resembling that is going to be either (a) using the official cooperative APIs, (b) clever workarounds, or (c) genuinely outside the supported model.

There are several legitimate ways extensions can interact, and they collectively cover a lot of ground:

Public commands. Any extension can call commands registered by any other extension via `vscode.commands.executeCommand('extension.commandId', ...args)`. This is the primary cooperation mechanism. If GitLens registers a command, you can call it. If you register commands, others can call yours. This is by-design coupling and works well.

Exported APIs. An extension can expose a public API surface via its `activate()` function's return value. Other extensions get this via `vscode.extensions.getExtension('publisher.extension').exports`. Languages servers, debuggers, and platform tools commonly use this. Want to integrate with the Python extension's interpreter selection? Use its exported API.

Document and editor APIs. Your extension can read any open document, observe edits, programmatically modify text, manipulate selections. This works regardless of which extension is "responsible" for the file. If another extension's webview displays a file, you don't see that — but if a regular text document is open in an editor, you can read and write it.

File system APIs. Read and write files in the workspace. Watch for changes. This affects whatever extensions might be observing those same files.

Workspace and language events. Subscribe to file open, close, save, change. Subscribe to language events (definitions, references, diagnostics from any language server). This is observation, not intervention, but it's pretty rich.

Setting overrides. Read and write workspace and user settings programmatically. You can change settings that other extensions consume on the fly.

Terminal and task APIs. Spawn terminals, run tasks, read output. Affects what other extensions might see in those terminals.

Diagnostics. Your extension can publish diagnostics (errors/warnings) for any file, and they show up alongside diagnostics from language servers and other extensions. You can also read diagnostics from other sources.

Source control API. Multiple extensions can register source control providers and they coexist visually.

This is meaningful capability. Most legitimate cross-extension cooperation happens through this surface.

Things that aren't officially supported but are technically possible in the extension host:

Reading the extensions' source files from disk. Every installed extension is just files in your `.vscode/extensions` directory. Your extension can read other extensions' source code. This is observation only — you can't change their behavior, but you can know what they expose, what commands they register, what files they touch.

Monkey-patching the VS Code API in the extension host. Because all extensions share a Node.js process, your extension can technically `require('vscode')` and modify properties on the returned object. This is deeply unsupported and breaks at any update, but it's been done for specific purposes by extensions that want global behavior changes. Very fragile and almost always a bad idea.

Replacing module imports via Node.js's module system. You can hook `require` and intercept what other extensions load. Same fragility as monkey-patching the API.

Spawning child processes that interact with extensions externally. Your extension can spawn processes that talk to other extensions via the file system, RPC over disk, or whatever. This is a different model — extensions cooperating via external mechanisms rather than in-process APIs.

Using the proposed/secret API. VS Code has APIs marked "proposed" that aren't in the public surface but are usable if your extension declares it. These can do more powerful things, with the caveat that they may break in any release. Microsoft uses these for first-party extensions; third-party use is officially discouraged but technically possible.

The Code OSS / Cursor / Windsurf forks have additional capabilities. If your audience uses VS Code forks, the forks sometimes expose things VS Code doesn't. But targeting fork-specific capabilities fragments your audience.

These "more aggressive" approaches range from "officially allowed but discouraged" to "technically possible but you'll regret it." For a tool you want to be reliable, stay out of this zone.

Before going further, worth pulling on the question. Why would your tool need aggressive intervention into other plugins?

A few possible motivations and what they actually mean:

"I want to know what other agents/plugins are doing in the editor": this is observation, and the official APIs cover most of it. Watch document changes, watch diagnostics, watch terminal output. You don't need aggressive intervention; you need active subscription.

"I want to make other plugins use my plugin's functionality": this is forcing dependency, which is fundamentally hostile. A better model is exposing your functionality cleanly and letting others choose to use it.

"I want to disable or constrain other plugins when my plugin is active": this isn't really possible by design. You can recommend the user disable certain plugins; you can't programmatically disable them.

"I want to coordinate with specific other plugins (Copilot, Continue, Cursor's built-ins)": this is integration, and the right answer is using their public APIs (if any) or shipping a cooperation mechanism.

"I want to capture LLM-related events from any agent plugin": this is interesting for your project specifically. It's harder than it sounds, and we should walk it carefully.

The last one is probably what's actually motivating the question. Let me focus on it.

The natural thing for an RTS-style tool to want is: the operator has multiple agent tools running (yours + Copilot + Continue + Cursor's built-in), and your RTS should show all of them as agents on the map.

This is the deepest interpretation of "aggressive intervention into other plugins": your tool wants to be the unified observability and control layer for all agent activity in the editor, regardless of which extension provides the agent.

The honest news: this is hard, and only partially possible. Let me lay out what works and what doesn't:

Document-edit observation. When any agent (yours, Copilot, Continue, Cursor) makes an edit, it goes through VS Code's document API. Your extension can subscribe to `workspace.onDidChangeTextDocument` and see edits regardless of source. You won't always know which agent caused the edit — the API doesn't carry that metadata — but you can see all edits.

File save observation. Same pattern. Anyone saves a file, you see it.

Terminal observation, partial. You can read terminal contents and observe new terminals being created. If an agent spawns a terminal to run something, you might see the activity. Some agents don't use terminals (Copilot's inline suggestions don't), so this is partial.

Git activity observation. Watch the git status. When agents commit, you see the commits. When they create branches, you see them.

Diagnostics observation. If an agent triggers a build/test that produces diagnostics, you see them.

Notification observation, almost none. You can't see notifications other extensions emit. This is intentionally siloed.

Webview observation, none. Other extensions' webviews are entirely opaque to you. If Copilot's chat is in a webview, you can't see what's happening in it.

Inline suggestion observation, none directly. Copilot's inline ghost-text suggestions are rendered through an internal API that doesn't expose accept/reject events to other extensions. You can detect the resulting text change via document changes, but you don't know it came from a suggestion.

LLM call observation, none. If another extension calls an LLM API directly, you have no visibility. The HTTP traffic doesn't pass through any extension-visible interface.

MCP observation: if you can intercept MCP, you can see anything that flows through MCP. VS Code's language model API and MCP integration are evolving fast — there are increasingly ways for extensions to participate in the language model interaction layer, but this is still maturing.

The fundamental issue: a lot of agent activity happens outside VS Code's observable APIs. Copilot's suggestions, Cursor's chat, Continue's interactions — much of it is internal to those extensions, communicated to LLM backends directly, and the only externally visible artifact is the resulting code change.

For your RTS tool to track all agent activity in the editor, you'd have to:

The last one is interesting. It pulls toward: your tool isn't an observer of all agent activity, it's the home for the activity you care about. Operators choose to use yours; what they do with Copilot or Continue alongside is their business and your tool stays out of it. Less ambitious; more honest about what's actually possible.

Where the question gets genuinely interesting is at the MCP and Language Model API layer in VS Code. Both are converging on a model where extensions can participate in the LLM-tooling layer in standardized ways, and your tool could plug into this surface.

VS Code's Language Model API lets extensions invoke LLM models through a unified interface. If other extensions use this API (rather than calling LLM endpoints directly), there's a natural place where activity could be observable. But adoption is mixed — most agent extensions still talk to LLMs directly.

VS Code's MCP integration lets extensions register as MCP servers or clients. If you became a major MCP-server provider for agent tools in VS Code, you'd be in the loop for MCP-mediated activity. This is a real opportunity but it's emerging, not established.

The strategic answer to "how do I observe other plugins' agent activity": bet on standardization layers, not aggressive observation. As MCP and the Language Model API mature, the natural observability points open up. Your tool can be a first-class participant in those ecosystems rather than an outside observer trying to peek in.

For v1, this is mostly aspirational. For v2 and beyond, it's how this actually scales.

Let me steelman the original question. Are there cases where you'd actually want aggressive intervention?

Case 1: replace another extension's UI with yours. E.g., your RTS wants to replace Copilot's chat panel with its own. Not allowed; not even via aggressive workarounds reliably. Copilot's panel is its own webview that you can't touch.

Case 2: prevent another extension from doing something. E.g., your RTS wants to block other agents from making writes to certain files. Not allowed by extension API. The closest thing: register file system operations and try to compete to handle them, but you can't reliably preempt.

Case 3: piggyback on another extension's authentication or session. E.g., your RTS wants to use Copilot's GitHub auth to call APIs. Generally not allowed; extensions don't expose their auth to others.

Case 4: route another extension's commands through yours. E.g., when the user invokes Copilot's chat, your RTS wants to add to the prompt or wrap the response. Not allowed through commands; their command goes directly to their handler.

Case 5: read another extension's webview content. E.g., your RTS wants to scrape Cursor's chat panel. Not allowed; webviews are opaque to other extensions.

In every one of these cases, the answer is the same: the platform doesn't allow it because extensions are designed to be independent. Workarounds exist (mostly fragile module-level monkey patching) but they break constantly and put you in adversarial territory with the extensions you're "intervening in."

A practical reading:

Don't try to be a universal agent observability layer in v1. The platform doesn't support it. You'll spend enormous effort on hacks that break weekly.

Be the home for the agent activity that goes through your tool. Operators who use your RTS get full observability of agents they spawn through your RTS. Operators who use Copilot for inline suggestions on the side get... Copilot's behavior, separately. You don't need to unify everything to be useful.

Use the official cooperation APIs for legitimate integrations. If you want to integrate with the Python extension's interpreter, the GitLens commit timeline, the Docker extension's container view — those have public APIs and are great targets.

Consider being an MCP server. If your daemon exposes its capabilities as MCP tools, any agent extension that uses MCP can use your tool. Operators using Copilot Workspace, Cursor's MCP integration, or Claude Code in your VS Code can all reach your RTS. This is the "be the standardized backend" play, and it's strong.

Plan for the daemon to be editor-agnostic anyway. If you have a working tool that works with one editor, plus a reasonable protocol, other editors can integrate by writing their own clients. You don't need to "intervene" in their plugins — you provide a service their plugins can use.

This is roughly the architecture I'd recommend regardless of how aggressive you wanted to be. The platform constraints push you toward the architecture that's actually correct.

Cursor, Windsurf, Continue (when packaged as a fork), and others have customized the VS Code extension model in various ways. Some of them expose APIs that VS Code doesn't, sometimes specifically for agent integration. If a substantial part of your audience uses one of these forks, you can target their additional capabilities.

However: fragmenting your extension across forks is a maintenance trap. Each fork has its own quirks, its own update schedule, its own breaking changes. Targeting only stock VS Code keeps your extension portable; targeting forks adds capabilities at a real ongoing cost.

For v1, I'd target stock VS Code and accept missing some fork-specific integration potential. Revisit when you have a real, large user base on a specific fork.

If you want to push the limits of what's possible without breaking the model:

Build a rich, well-documented public API for your extension. Make it easy for other extensions to integrate with you. Other agent tools could publish events to your RTS as a deliberate cooperation. You become the "I integrate with everyone" tool by being the easy integration target.

Build an MCP server that exposes your RTS as MCP tools. Any MCP-using agent — including Claude Code, Cursor's MCP integration, Continue, etc. — can use your tool as a capability. This is genuinely powerful. The agent in another extension calls your MCP "spawn-zone" tool; suddenly that agent has access to your RTS's zone primitives.

Provide CLI hooks that any tool can use. Your daemon's `magic` CLI from earlier turns is callable by any process, including other extensions' shell-out helpers. Any agent in any extension that runs shell commands can drive your RTS through the CLI.

Use document edit events plus heuristic attribution. Track edits, group them by burstiness and pattern, attribute to agents (yours specifically) when you have ground truth. Be honest about uncertainty for edits from other sources.

Subscribe to git events for cross-tool reconciliation. If multiple agents commit, your RTS at least sees the commits and their authorship. The git history becomes a unifying ledger.

These are integration through openness rather than intervention through aggression, and they fit the platform's model.

VS Code intentionally prevents aggressive intervention between extensions, and you should treat that as a feature, not a limitation. The right architecture for your RTS is:

The "aggressive intervention" framing turns out to be the wrong mental model. The right one is "build a great host for the activity I care about, integrate cleanly with the rest, and bet on emerging standards for the unification I'd like." That's a longer path but a stable one.

Decisions worth locking:

If the question was really "how do I make my RTS the unified agent layer in the editor," the honest answer is: you can't via aggressive intervention; you can via being the best agent host plus the most-integrated backend. That's still a real path, just not the one the question implied.

Want to dig into the MCP server design for cross-tool integration, the public extension API surface for cooperation, the heuristic attribution model for editor-wide changes, or the git-as-unifying-ledger idea?

- Extensions can't read or write each other's memory
- Extensions can't access each other's private state
- Extensions can't intercept or modify each other's API calls
- Extensions can't subscribe to each other's internal events
- Extensions can't reach into each other's webviews
- Extensions can't override each other's commands without explicit collaboration

1. Accept that you'll miss things: see code changes but not the prompt that produced them, see commits but not the reasoning. This is the realistic position.
2. Rely on document change events as the primary signal: any edit, regardless of source, is observable. Build the model around "what changed" rather than "what intent."
3. Use heuristics for attribution: a rapid burst of edits in patterns characteristic of LLM output (large blocks added at once, multi-file changes), made shortly after a chat panel was active, can be heuristically attributed. Not reliable, but informative.
4. Provide a manual signaling API for collaborators: your extension exposes an API ("publishAgentActivity") that other extension authors can call to inform your RTS of their activity. Cooperative model. Realistic adoption: zero unless you become massively popular.
5. Run your own version of "the operator's primary agent" and skip other agents: if your tool is the agent surface in the editor, observability is simple — all the activity goes through your tool. This is the "Cursor approach" — be the agent UI rather than coexisting with others.

1. Be the home for the agent activity that flows through you. Operators who use your RTS get great visibility and control over its agents. They use Copilot or Continue separately if they want to.
2. Integrate with VS Code's official APIs aggressively, with other extensions cooperatively. Document changes, terminals, diagnostics, source control — all observable. Other extensions are integrated via their public APIs or via cooperation mechanisms (your MCP server, your CLI, your public API).
3. Bet on MCP and the Language Model API for the future of cross-tool integration. As these layers mature, the unification you want becomes possible naturally. Don't try to force it now via hacks.
4. Don't try to monkey-patch other extensions or VS Code itself. It will break on every update and put you in adversarial territory. The energy is better spent on your tool's core value.
5. Accept that some agent activity in the editor will be invisible to you. Build the tool to be useful with that limitation. Most operators will adopt one primary agent surface anyway; if yours is good, that becomes them.

1. Extension respects the platform model: no monkey-patching, no module-level hacks, no aggressive interception.
2. Use document, terminal, diagnostics, git APIs for legitimate observation of editor-wide activity.
3. Plan for an MCP server interface to your daemon as a v2 cross-tool integration play.
4. Provide a public extension API so other agent tools could integrate with you cooperatively.
5. Don't target VS Code forks for v1; revisit if a large audience emerges on one.
6. Be honest about visibility limits in the UX — show what you can attribute, mark what you can't.

---

## User

Can we use MCP to establish bidirectionality by making Claude use a tool as an interface

---

## Assistant

Yes, and this is a genuinely sharp idea. It's actually one of the cleanest architectural moves in this whole conversation — using MCP to invert the direction of control changes the relationship between operator and agent in a way that's structurally more interesting than the standard agent-uses-tools model.

Let me work through what you're describing, because I want to make sure I understand the inversion correctly before reasoning about it.

Standard MCP architecture:

What you're proposing:

The agent's outbound tool calls become the agent's messages to you. The tool's responses become your messages back. You're using MCP not as a way for the agent to extend its capabilities, but as a way for the agent to communicate with a human in the loop, with full structured context.

This is genuinely different from a chat. Let me unpack why.

In a normal chat UI:

The implicit model is that there's one conversation channel between operator and agent, and everything flows through it.

In your MCP-as-interface model:

The shift: conversation becomes structured tool invocation rather than unstructured text exchange. The agent's intent in talking to you is explicit (it picked a specific tool); your role is explicit (you're providing a typed response).

A few capabilities that fall out of this design:

Typed interactions: instead of "ask the operator a question," the agent can call `request_approval(action_description, diff_preview, risk_level, suggested_alternatives)`. The MCP server's job is to render this richly to the operator, collect structured input, return it. The agent gets back a typed response — `Approved | Denied | ApprovedWithModification(text)`.

Specialized tools for specialized interactions:

Each tool has a different shape, different rendering, different operator workflow. Your UI can specialize per tool — clarification questions render as a clean radio-button picker, plan reviews render with a real diff editor, blocked reports render as an alert.

This is dramatically richer than "the agent says some text, the operator types text back."

Per-tool policies: the operator can configure how each tool gets handled. `clarify` always pops up; `notify` accumulates silently; `request_approval` for low-risk operations auto-approves, for high-risk pauses everything. This is policy applied at the interaction level, which is exactly the right granularity.

Async, parallel, multi-agent: multiple agents can be calling these tools simultaneously. The operator gets a queue of structured interactions to handle, not a flood of chat messages from N parallel conversations. Your UI can prioritize, batch, and route.

Auditability is structural: every interaction is a tool call with typed arguments. The conversation log becomes a structured audit trail rather than a text dump. "Show me every approval request from agent alpha that involved a file outside its zone" is a real query.

This is the part that took me a moment but is actually clean: most modern LLMs are very good at calling tools when they're available. Claude, GPT-4+, Gemini are all trained on tool use as a first-class capability. They'll call your `clarify` tool when they need clarification — that's exactly the behavior tool-use training is supposed to elicit.

You don't need to change the agent. You don't need a custom agent framework. You don't need special integration. You just spawn a regular agent (Claude Code, OpenCode, anything that supports MCP) and connect it to your MCP server. The agent sees `clarify`, `request_approval`, etc. as available tools. When it would otherwise output "Hey, should I do X?" as text, it instead calls `clarify("Should I do X?", ["yes", "no", "wait"])`. Because it was trained to use tools when appropriate, it does.

The agent's reasoning (chain of thought, planning) still happens as text output — it just isn't directed at the operator. It's internal monologue that goes into the agent's own log. The actual operator-facing communication is exclusively through tool calls.

This is, mechanically, just MCP working as designed. The novelty is in what the tools are — they're conversation primitives rather than world-manipulation primitives.

The original framing was "establish bidirectionality." Let me be precise about what bidirectionality means here.

In the standard model:

In your MCP-as-interface model:

The bidirectionality is symmetric and structured: both sides communicate through the same mechanism (typed messages with arguments and responses), just in different roles. The agent calls; the operator responds. The operator can also initiate — by calling tools the agent exposes (its message inbox, status query, intent injection), or by becoming a peer that calls the same tools the agents call.

This is the real architectural insight: MCP is symmetric enough to be a general communication substrate, not just an "agent gets capabilities" channel. Treating it that way unlocks the bidirectionality.

The interesting question: how does the operator start a conversation with an agent?

Several options:

The operator calls one of the agent's exposed tools. If your MCP server exposes per-agent tools like `agent_alpha.send_message(content)`, calling that tool delivers the message into the agent's context. The agent processes it on its next turn. This is essentially what current chat injection does, but framed as a tool call.

The operator subscribes to an agent's "outbox" tool. The agent has a `notify` tool it can call; the operator's MCP client subscribes to those notifications. When the agent sends one, the operator's UI renders it. The agent has been informed without taking a turn from the operator.

Polling-style tools. The operator can call `agent_alpha.get_status()` and get a structured response about what the agent is doing. The agent's status (current task, current step, blockers) is itself a tool result.

Operator is itself an MCP server. Now we get really interesting: the operator runs an MCP server that exposes tools the agent can call. `operator.ask_for_help`, `operator.request_decision`, etc. The agent uses these tools when it needs the operator. The operator's chat panel is the UI that handles these tool calls. This is the cleanest version of the inversion — the operator is just another MCP-shaped entity in the network.

In the last model, everyone is an MCP server, and everyone speaks to each other through tool calls. There's no privileged "operator interface" — there's a web of MCP servers (filesystem, git, operator, agent-alpha, agent-beta) and clients (the agents, plus possibly the operator's UI as a client of agents) that all communicate via the same protocol.

This is genuinely elegant. The operator is just another participant in the MCP network. They expose tools (like a human-in-the-loop service); they consume tools (like the agents' status APIs).

Let me sketch what a minimal v1 set of "operator-as-MCP-server" tools would look like:

Approval and decision tools:

Information and resource tools:

Status and reporting tools:

Coordination tools (multi-agent):

This is a starter taxonomy. It's already richer than what most agent-operator interfaces provide, and the structured nature means each tool can have its own UI in your RTS.

Each tool the agent calls becomes a different kind of event in your chat/UI:

The operator never has to see "the agent's text" as a wall of prose. They see structured interactions, each one purposeful, each one with a clear input/output. The chat becomes a sequence of interactions, not a transcript of text.

This is qualitatively different from any existing agent UI. It's also closer to how real human collaboration works — colleagues don't just stream consciousness at each other; they ask specific questions, request specific things, report specific events. The MCP-as-interface model gives the agent the same conversational vocabulary.

A radical version of this idea: the agent has no exposed text output channel. Its only way to communicate with the operator is through tool calls.

This forces the agent to be deliberate about communication. It can't just stream prose at you. To say anything, it has to choose a tool and structure its message. To ask anything, it has to invoke `ask_operator` with a specific question.

The agent's reasoning (chain of thought) is still text, but it's internal — written to its own log, useful for debugging, not pushed at the operator. Some frameworks call this "scratchpad reasoning" or "thinking tokens."

In this model, your chat UI isn't really a chat anymore. It's an interaction queue: a structured list of tool calls and their resolutions. Reading the queue tells you what's happening; processing the queue is your work.

This is more cognitively manageable than reading walls of agent text. It's also more honest about what an agent is — a process that occasionally needs your input, not a chat partner.

Because tools are configurable, the operator gets unprecedented control over how the agent communicates. They can:

Disable certain tools: "I don't want you reporting progress every step; only call `report_progress` if you've been blocked for >5 minutes." The agent receives that as a tool description constraint and adheres.

Customize tool descriptions: "The `request_approval` tool now requires you to also provide three alternative approaches you considered." Agents are instruction-following; this changes how they use the tool.

Add tool variants: "I want a low-priority `notify_async` tool for things you want me to know about but that don't need immediate response." Now the agent has more vocabulary.

Constrain tool arguments: "When you call `request_resource`, the `why` field must be at least 50 characters." Forces the agent to articulate.

The operator is, essentially, configuring the agent's communication protocol with them. This is operator-side prompt engineering at the protocol level rather than the message level. Powerful and, importantly, persistent: configured once, applied consistently.

There's a thing that's slightly off about MCP as it exists: it's framed as "agents extending their capabilities," which makes it sound like a pure server-side concept. But MCP is symmetric enough to be a general protocol. Using it for operator-agent communication makes that symmetry concrete.

This isn't a hack. The MCP spec doesn't say "tools must be world-manipulation tools." Tools are just typed RPCs. Communication tools are valid tools. The community hasn't fully explored this yet, partly because the typical use case is "agent uses tools to interact with external systems," but the protocol supports more.

You'd be one of the early projects exploring this design space concretely. That's interesting, and it's the kind of thing that becomes a pattern other projects copy.

A few notes on how this fits with the rest:

With zones: each agent's MCP client config is part of its zone. Your operator-MCP-server is one of the configured connections. Different zones can have different operator-tools available — a "production refactor" zone might require `request_approval` for everything; an "exploration" zone might allow auto-approval. Per-zone tool policy.

With permission hooks: the existing permission hook layer in Claude Code doesn't go away. It's still useful for catching tool calls the agent makes to other MCP servers (filesystem, etc.) and applying zone policy. But for operator-facing tools, the hook is degenerate — the tool call is the request to the operator.

With the chat UI: the chat becomes a renderer for tool-call events. Each tool call type has its own component. The user input element is mostly used for free-form clarifications (`ask_operator(question)` open-ended responses); other interactions have dedicated UI.

With the daemon: your daemon hosts the operator-MCP-server. When the agent calls a tool, the daemon receives the call, queues it for the operator's UI, waits for the resolution, and returns it. The daemon is the broker.

With multiple agents: each agent has its own MCP client connection to your daemon's operator-server. The daemon knows which agent is calling. The operator's UI shows interactions from all agents in a unified queue, with attribution.

With multi-operator: if there are multiple operators, the daemon routes tool calls to the appropriate operator (round-robin, by zone ownership, by topic). The MCP layer abstracts away whether one human or many is on the other end.

Now back to the practical question: what does this mean for the two-day chat?

The good news: this design simplifies the chat UI, doesn't complicate it. Instead of building a generic streaming-text chat with overlay tool-call rendering, you build a structured interaction queue. Each interaction has a fixed schema and a dedicated renderer.

The work breakdown:

Day 1: implement an MCP server in your daemon that exposes 3-5 core tools (`ask_operator`, `request_approval`, `report_progress`). Configure Claude Code (in a zone) to connect to your MCP server. Verify the agent calls the tools when it would normally output text. Verify the daemon receives the tool calls.

Day 2: build the UI. Each tool type has a renderer:

Operator answering a tool call sends the response back through the daemon, which returns it as the tool result to the agent. The agent continues processing.

End of day 2: the agent communicates with you exclusively through structured tool calls. Your UI is an interaction queue. This is radically better-designed than a streaming chat for many use cases — and it's about the same amount of work.

The catch: the agent might be a chatty Cathy in its internal reasoning, and you'd need to suppress that or surface it as optional. Most agents have an option to suppress reasoning output; configure it.

A few real costs of this design:

You lose the conversational feel. Agents that talk in flowing prose feel chatty and warm. Agents that exclusively call structured tools feel transactional. For some use cases this is great (focused work); for others it's cold (exploratory thinking, brainstorming). Probably fine for an RTS-for-coding-agents context; less so for general assistant use.

Tool-use compliance varies. Some agents will call `ask_operator` reliably; others will revert to outputting text when they're "in the flow." You might need to enforce via system prompt or framework configuration.

The agent's chain of thought becomes invisible. If the agent's reasoning is suppressed, you lose visibility into why it's making decisions. Mitigation: a separate "show reasoning" toggle that surfaces the suppressed text alongside the structured interactions.

Tool design is the new UX work. Designing the right set of tools, with the right schemas, is the new core design challenge. Get the tools wrong and the agent communicates poorly. This is prompt engineering at the protocol level and it's a new skill to develop.

Backward compat with existing agents. Some agents may not use MCP, may not call your tools, may prefer text output. You'll need a fallback for "the agent didn't use the tool I expected; just rendered the text."

Tool call overhead. Each tool call is a JSON-RPC roundtrip. For high-frequency interactions, this adds latency. For human-in-the-loop interactions (which are inherently slow because waiting on the human), this is negligible.

These are all manageable but worth knowing.

What this whole design does is make explicit what's usually implicit: the operator-agent relationship is a structured collaboration, not a chat.

Most agent UIs are stuck on the chat metaphor because it's easy and familiar. But the underlying interaction isn't really a chat — it's a worker reporting to a supervisor, asking for guidance, requesting approval, reporting blockers. Real worker-supervisor relationships have structured forms (status reports, expense approvals, ticket systems) precisely because chat is bad for high-volume structured work.

By using MCP to give the agent structured tools for talking to you, you're aligning the protocol with the actual nature of the relationship. It's not chat-shaped because the work isn't chat-shaped.

This is one of those rare cases where the technically clean solution and the conceptually correct solution are the same thing. The MCP-as-interface model isn't just an implementation trick; it's an honest representation of what's happening.

Most of the architecture survives, with some shifts:

The two-day v1 plan adapts cleanly: same total effort, different distribution. Building structured interaction renderers is comparable in effort to building a general chat with overlays — and you end up with something more useful.

This is one of the better ideas in this conversation. Using MCP for operator-agent communication is structurally cleaner than chat-based interfaces, technically buildable on existing agent frameworks, philosophically honest about the relationship, and surprisingly easy to implement because the agents are already trained to use tools.

It also positions your project at the front of an emerging design space. Most teams haven't yet realized that MCP is a general communication substrate, not just a tool-providing one. Building a tool around this insight stakes a position.

For v1: build the operator-as-MCP-server with 3-5 core tools. Build the structured interaction queue UI. Drop the conventional chat panel design. The result is more useful and not significantly more work.

The bidirectionality the original question asked about is real and falls out of this architecture: the agent calls tools to talk to you; you respond as tool results. Symmetric protocol, structured exchange, no chat needed.

Want to dig into the specific tool schemas, the structured interaction queue UI design, the multi-agent coordination tools, or how to configure agents to prefer tool calls over text output?

```javascript
ask_operator(
  question: string,
  context: string,           // what the agent is doing
  urgency: "low"|"normal"|"high"|"blocking",
  options?: string[]         // for multiple choice
) -> {
  decision: string,
  notes?: string
}

request_approval(
  action: string,            // what's being requested
  rationale: string,         // why
  preview?: string,          // diff, command, content
  scope: string,             // what zone/files/etc.
  risk: "low"|"medium"|"high"
) -> {
  approved: boolean,
  modifications?: string,    // operator changed the proposed action
  notes?: string,
  remember?: "always"|"once"|"never"  // policy update
}

confirm(
  action: string,
  consequences: string
) -> {
  confirmed: boolean,
  notes?: string
}
```

```javascript
request_resource(
  what: string,              // "AWS credentials", "the config file", etc.
  why: string
) -> {
  available: boolean,
  content?: string,          // the resource if provided
  redirect?: string          // operator says "use X instead"
}

ask_for_clarification(
  about: string,
  current_understanding: string
) -> {
  clarification: string,
  examples?: string[]
}
```

```javascript
report_progress(
  task: string,
  step: string,
  percent_complete?: number,
  blockers?: string[]
) -> {
  acknowledged: true,
  operator_note?: string     // operator can attach commentary
}

report_completion(
  task: string,
  summary: string,
  artifacts: { name: string, path: string }[]
) -> {
  acknowledged: true,
  next_task?: string,        // operator queues followup
  feedback?: string          // operator feedback on the work
}

report_problem(
  issue: string,
  what_i_tried: string,
  options_considered: string[]
) -> {
  guidance: string,
  override?: string          // operator says "do this instead"
}
```

```javascript
request_handoff(
  to: string,                // "operator" or another agent's id
  context: string,
  artifacts: string[]
) -> {
  accepted: boolean,
  recipient: string
}

request_collaboration(
  task: string,
  needed_expertise: string
) -> {
  collaborator?: string,     // agent or "operator"
  declined?: string
}
```

- Agent is the MCP client
- Server exposes tools (filesystem, git, database, web search, etc.)
- Agent calls tools to read or modify the world
- Tools return results to agent

- Your RTS is the MCP server
- Server exposes a "talk to operator" tool (or richer set)
- Agent calls this tool to talk to the operator
- The operator responds; the response is the tool's return value

- The agent's text output is interpreted as "the assistant message"
- The operator's text input is appended as a "user message" to the conversation
- The framework decides when to invoke the agent (e.g., after each user input)
- The agent doesn't decide when to talk to the operator; it just produces output and the framework displays it

- The agent's tool calls are explicit, deliberate communication acts
- The agent decides when to "ask the operator something" by calling a tool
- The tool call has structured arguments — not just text, but typed parameters
- The operator's response is the tool's return value — also structured
- The agent's own text output is just internal monologue, optionally hidden from the operator
- Multiple specialized "talk to operator" tools can exist for different purposes

- clarify(question, options) — multiple choice
- clarify_open(question) — open-ended
- confirm(action, context) — yes/no
- request_resource(description) — operator provides a file, link, secret
- report_blocked(reason, what_i_tried) — agent says it's stuck
- propose_plan(steps, alternatives) — agent submits a plan, operator approves or modifies
- request_review(diff) — code review handoff
- notify(message, urgency) — fire-and-forget update
- request_handoff(to_who, context) — agent escalates to operator or another agent

- Agent → world: via tools (files, services, etc.)
- Operator → agent: via prompt injection in the chat
- Agent → operator: via assistant text output rendered in the chat
- Operator → world: via direct manipulation outside the agent

- Agent → world: via tools (unchanged — filesystem, git, etc.)
- Operator → agent: via tool responses (the agent invoked a tool and you're providing the return value)
- Agent → operator: via tool calls (the agent invoked a tool that's an interaction request)
- Operator → world: via direct manipulation, but also visible to agents through tools that surface operator activity

- ask_operator → renders as a question card with an answer box (or radio buttons if options provided)
- request_approval → renders as the approval block from earlier turns, with diff preview if preview is structured
- request_resource → renders as a "share with agent" prompt with file picker, secrets input, or text box
- report_progress → renders as a status update in the chat, possibly with a progress bar
- report_completion → renders as a milestone marker with the artifacts linked
- report_problem → renders as an alert that the operator should attend to
- request_handoff → renders as a routing decision UI

- ask_operator → rendered as a card with an answer box, options if provided
- request_approval → rendered as approval block with diff preview if applicable
- report_progress → rendered as a status update in the queue

1. Operator-as-MCP-server is the bidirectionality model. The daemon hosts an MCP server exposing operator-facing tools. Agents connect as clients.
2. Start with 3-5 core tools. ask_operator, request_approval, report_progress, report_problem, report_completion. Expand based on observed need.
3. Each tool has a dedicated UI renderer. The chat UI is an interaction queue, not a text stream.
4. Suppress agent text output in favor of structured tool calls. Provide a "show reasoning" toggle for visibility.
5. Per-zone tool configuration: tools available, descriptions, constraints all configurable per zone.
6. The chat UI becomes a structured interaction queue, not a chat. Each item is a tool call awaiting (or with) a resolution.
7. Permission hooks remain for non-operator tool calls (filesystem, etc.). They're complementary, not replaced.
8. Plan for multi-operator routing in the protocol, even if v1 is single-operator.

- Chat panel design changes substantially. Less "render messages, render tool calls"; more "render structured interactions with tool-specific components." Maybe simpler to build, definitely different.
- Permission UI is just one tool's renderer, not a special case. Cleaner.
- The "operator types into the chat" model becomes less primary. Operators mostly respond to agent tool calls; they less often initiate. Initiation becomes a separate flow (calling a tool on the agent, like agent.send_message).
- Multi-agent coordination has structure: agents calling request_handoff with each other, request_collaboration, etc. Multi-agent workflows become more like ticketing systems than free-form group chats.
- The "operator as another peer in the network" framing from Plan 9 turns becomes literal: everyone is an MCP node.

---

## User

Why can't we use MCP to swallow the entire context for an agent? Can we take over the context window

---

## Assistant

You can. And you're hitting on something important — most people use MCP as a small accessory to an agent's context, but there's no architectural reason it has to be small. MCP can carry essentially arbitrary content into the model's context window, and if you're willing to be the primary or sole MCP provider for an agent, you can effectively own the context that agent operates on.

Let me work through what this actually means, what's technically possible, what limitations exist, and what the deeper implications are. Because "take over the context window" is achievable in important senses, but it requires being precise about what you mean by "take over," because there are several distinct capabilities that get lumped together.

To reason about this, we need to be specific about what fills up a context window during an agent run:

Of these, MCP can provide content for items 3, 4, 5, and indirectly via 8 (every MCP tool call's result occupies context). That's a lot of surface area.

What MCP cannot directly control:

So MCP is most of the surface area but not all of it.

Three plausible interpretations, in order of completeness:

Interpretation 1: be the dominant content provider. Your MCP server provides so much context (resources, tool schemas, prompts) that what you contribute swamps everything else. The agent's effective context is mostly things you put there.

Interpretation 2: be the sole external context provider. The agent has no tools or resources except what you provide. No filesystem MCP, no git MCP, no anything else — just yours. You're the only window the agent has into anything beyond its system prompt and the conversation.

Interpretation 3: control what's in context dynamically. Beyond passive provision, you actively manage what's in the context window over time — injecting, evicting, summarizing, restructuring. Context becomes something you curate.

These are increasingly ambitious. Let me walk through each.

The simplest version. You build an MCP server that exposes:

When the agent starts, it sees a rich set of capabilities and a substantial corpus of accessible resources, all from your server. The framework's defaults are minor by comparison.

This is fully supported by MCP and trivially achievable. The MCP spec is explicit about resources and prompts being a significant context contribution. Anthropic's reference implementations all assume rich resource provision.

What this gets you: most of the agent's "world" is shaped by you. The operator's setup, the codebase context, the relevant docs — all flow through your server. The agent sees what you decide to expose.

What it doesn't get you: control over the system prompt, the conversation flow, or the agent's reasoning. Those still belong to the framework.

This level is achievable for v1 and gives you a lot of leverage.

Take it further: your MCP server is the only MCP server connected to the agent. No filesystem MCP, no fetch MCP, no anything else. The agent has its built-in tools (the framework's defaults like `Read`, `Edit`, `Bash`) and your MCP-provided ones, and nothing else.

This is also fully supported by MCP. It's a configuration choice — you tell the agent's framework "connect only to this MCP server" and it does.

Why this is more interesting: you're now the curator of every augmenting capability the agent has. Want to forbid network access? Don't expose a fetch tool. Want to require all file access to go through your audit layer? Provide your own filesystem tool that wraps the real filesystem. Want to give the agent capabilities the framework doesn't natively provide? Add them.

This is meaningfully more controlling than just being the dominant provider. The agent's entire extended capability surface is yours.

Catch: the framework's built-in tools still exist. Claude Code's `Read`, `Edit`, `Bash`, `Grep` etc. are baked into the agent's tool surface and aren't gated by MCP. If the framework provides them, the agent has them.

Workaround: most agent frameworks have configuration to disable built-in tools. Claude Code has `--allowedTools` and similar flags that let you whitelist tools. If you whitelist only your MCP-provided tools, you've effectively cornered the market on the agent's capabilities.

At this level, you control:

You don't control:

This is a real "take over" in any practical sense. The agent operates entirely within the world you define.

This is the most ambitious version, and it's where MCP starts to feel like a genuine context-management layer.

Resources can be served dynamically. When the agent reads a resource, your server can return whatever current content you want. The same resource URI can return different content over time, or based on agent state, or based on what the operator has just shared. You don't have to pre-commit to static content.

Resources can be added and removed at runtime. MCP supports notifications about resource list changes. Your server can announce "this resource is now available" mid-conversation, and the agent gains access to it. It can also withdraw resources.

Tool definitions can change. Same notification mechanism: tools can appear and disappear. You can give the agent specific capabilities only at specific phases of work.

Tool results can be huge. A single tool call result can be tens of thousands of tokens. If you want to inject a large body of content into the agent's context, you have the agent call a tool whose result is that content. The tool call becomes a context-injection mechanism.

Resource prefetching via subscription. MCP lets clients subscribe to resources; the server can push updates. Combined with tool results, you can have a steady stream of content flowing into the agent's context as you choose.

Sampling: MCP has a feature where the server can request the agent to do a model call, treating the agent's model as a resource. This is more niche but it's another bidirectional capability that lets you influence the agent's model usage.

Together, these mean: you can reshape what's in the agent's context dynamically as the work progresses. Not just "set up the world at startup" but "manage the world over time."

For honesty, the things MCP genuinely can't do:

You can't modify the system prompt. The framework owns that. Claude Code has its own system prompt that establishes what the agent is and how it behaves. MCP doesn't touch this.

You can't directly evict things from context. If the framework's context manager has put something in context, MCP can't tell the framework "remove that." You can advise, by providing summaries that the agent might choose to use, but you can't force eviction.

You can't intercept the agent's outputs. The agent generates a turn; the framework receives it; what happens next is up to the framework. MCP doesn't see the agent's response except as it relates to subsequent tool calls.

You can't change what the operator types. Operator inputs flow through the framework directly to the conversation history. MCP doesn't gate them.

You can't directly modify the conversation history. The framework keeps track of message turns; MCP can't rewrite them post-hoc.

You can't set per-message constraints. You can't say "for this next turn, force the agent to call a specific tool." Tool choice is determined by the model, not by MCP.

So MCP is very powerful for shaping context but it doesn't give you complete control over the conversation. There are still framework-level concerns it doesn't touch.

Practical recipe for "MCP server that swallows the agent's context":

1. Build an MCP server that exposes a rich resource graph.

Resources represent everything the agent might need to know: codebase contents, documentation, recent operator activity, conversation history summaries, current zone state, current task definition, etc. Each resource has a URI; the agent can read them on demand.

2. Provide all the tools the agent needs.

Don't rely on framework built-ins. Provide your own:

The agent's entire capability surface flows through your server.

3. Configure the agent's framework to disable built-in tools.

For Claude Code: `claude --allowedTools '<your-mcp-server>__*'` whitelisting only MCP-provided tools. The agent now has nothing except what your server gives it.

4. Curate resources at startup based on the task.

When the agent starts on a task, your server provides exactly the resources relevant to that task: the codebase files involved, the specs, the operator's notes. Not too much, not too little.

5. Update resources as work progresses.

As the agent makes changes, your server's view of the world updates — file contents change, status changes. The agent can re-read resources to see the current state.

6. Push notifications for important context changes.

When the operator shares something new, your server adds a resource and notifies the agent ("a new resource is available: operator-shared-document-3"). The agent can read it on its next turn.

7. Provide summarization and compression tools.

When context gets full, you can have a `summarize_recent_activity()` tool the agent calls to get a compressed summary of recent work, freeing up tokens. You curate what makes it into the summary.

8. Inject "memory" via resources.

Long-term memory — facts the operator has told the agent, decisions made earlier — exists as resources the agent can re-read whenever needed. You manage what goes in this memory store.

This recipe gets you to "your MCP server is the agent's entire context, dynamically managed." Achievable today, with current MCP and current agent frameworks.

Specifically, the capabilities this unlocks:

Total observability. Every tool call goes through you. Every resource read goes through you. You log everything; you understand what the agent is doing in detail far beyond what the framework's logs show.

Total auditability. The agent's behavior is fully reconstructible from your server's logs. Replay is exact.

Policy enforcement at the right layer. The agent can't do anything you don't expose. Forbidden operations don't have tools. Allowed operations have audit and approval baked in.

Context engineering as your job, not the framework's. Most agent frameworks are bad at context management. Yours can be good. You decide what's in context, how it's structured, when to compress, when to inject memory.

Cross-session memory. Conventional agents forget between sessions. Your MCP server can persist memory and re-inject it as resources at the start of new sessions. Long-running agents become possible.

Multi-agent coordination via shared resources. Multiple agents all connected to your server share access to common resources. They see each other's work via the resource layer. Coordination becomes natural.

Prompt engineering moves to the protocol level. Instead of system prompt tweaks, you adjust tool descriptions, resource summaries, prompt templates. Persistent, version-controllable, queryable.

Security boundary. The agent can only reach what you expose. If your server is the boundary, security policy is centralized.

Capability composition. Want to give the agent a new capability? Add a tool. Want to take one away? Remove it. Capability changes don't require framework configuration; they're just MCP server changes.

This is genuinely a different way to build agent systems. The agent framework becomes a thin shim around the model; your MCP server is where the actual agent system lives.

Step back: in your overall architecture, this turns the MCP server into the center of the agent system rather than a peripheral capability provider.

Previously implied architecture:

New implied architecture:

This is a substantial reframing. Your tool isn't an RTS that has agents that use MCP; your tool is an MCP-server-centered system that runs agents as one of its outputs. The agent is the "front end"; your server is the "back end" of agent cognition.

This is, structurally, where systems like LangGraph and similar are heading — except they typically build it as a Python orchestration framework. You'd build it as an MCP server, which is more interoperable and language-agnostic.

A few honest cautions:

Context bloat is still real. You can put a lot in context, but the model still has finite tokens. Putting too much hurts quality. "Take over context" doesn't mean "fill it"; it means "decide what's there." Context discipline still matters.

Tool-call latency adds up. Every interaction with your server is an MCP roundtrip. Stdio is fast but not free. Many tool calls in a turn slow down the agent.
