```javascript
review what alpha did and explain the changes
```

1. Fork the existing Jupyter VS Code extension, which already provides notebook UI inside VS Code
2. Replace or repurpose the Jupyter kernel so that it functions as your MCP server
3. Use the notebook itself as the chat-over-MCP surface — cells become structured interaction events
4. Integrate the "advanced chat features" we've been discussing: streaming tool calls, structured renders, branching, artifact lifting, time travel, etc.

- A sequence of cells (turns)
- Each cell has a type (code/markdown/output)
- Cells are executed by a kernel (a backend process)
- Output streams back asynchronously
- State persists in the kernel between cells
- The notebook is durable as a file

- A sequence of tool calls (turns)
- Each tool has a type (its schema)
- Tools are handled by an MCP server (a backend process)
- Results stream back asynchronously
- State persists in the server between calls
- The conversation is durable as logged events

- Receives execute requests over a ZeroMQ-based protocol
- Executes code (Python, R, Julia, etc.)
- Streams output back: stdout, stderr, display_data, execute_result, errors
- Maintains state (variables, defined functions, imports)

- Receives execute requests, but interprets them as MCP-shaped commands
- Routes them to agents, the daemon, or directly handles them
- Streams output back: tool call events, tool results, agent state changes
- Maintains agent state, conversation history, world state

- Permission request from agent: rendered as an HTML widget with diff preview and approve/deny buttons, embedded in the output of whichever cell triggered the agent's work
- Status updates: rendered as a status widget that updates in place (using Jupyter's update_display_data)
- Tool call by the agent: rendered as a typed mini-cell within the output, with structured args and result
- Artifacts: rendered as displayable objects (a code block with syntax highlighting, a clickable file path, a diff)
- Plans: rendered as expandable hierarchical structures
- Multi-step interactions: each step rendered incrementally as the agent progresses

- Renders .ipynb files in VS Code's notebook editor
- Manages kernel selection and connection
- Handles cell execution, output rendering, widget integration
- Provides cell-related commands and keybindings
- Integrates with VS Code's ecosystem (debugging, IntelliSense for notebooks, Git diffs, etc.)

- Net-new: custom kernel implementation, custom output renderers, sidebar/control UI
- Fork modifications: kernel discovery, format handling, UI tweaks
- Reused: notebook editor, cell execution flow, output rendering pipeline, file management

- Implement a custom Jupyter kernel (just a separate process implementing the kernel protocol)
- Provide custom output renderers via the renderer API
- Add custom commands and UI through a sibling VS Code extension
- Define custom MIME types for your tool result renderings
- Integrate with the existing notebook editor's lifecycle hooks

- Changing core notebook behavior (cell execution semantics, save format)
- Adding non-cell-based UI integrated tightly into the notebook view
- Modifying how kernel discovery works at a fundamental level
- Restricting the user from running other kernels in your notebooks (you might want to only allow your kernel for .rts files)

- A custom kernel as a standalone process
- A renderer extension for VS Code (which is just a normal VS Code extension that registers with Jupyter)
- A sibling VS Code extension for sidebar UI and commands

- Operator runs a cell → extension sends execute_request to kernel → kernel parses cell content → kernel calls into your daemon (or hosts the daemon directly) → daemon dispatches to agents → agents make MCP tool calls → kernel receives them → kernel emits display_data messages with custom MIME types → extension renders them as cell output

- Implements the kernel protocol (using ipykernel library or similar)
- Hosts an MCP server that agents connect to
- Translates between MCP events and Jupyter display messages
- Manages a session — agents connected, current zone, conversation state

- A status widget appears showing "Agent alpha: starting"
- A tool call widget shows "Read src/auth/tokens.rs" with the file contents in an expandable section
- Another tool call widget shows "Read src/auth/mod.rs"
- A plan widget appears: "Proposed plan: 1. Create new module... 2. Move validator... 3. Update imports... [Approve plan] [Modify] [Reject]"

1. Custom Jupyter kernel (Python, using ipykernel): connects to your daemon, manages agents, translates between Jupyter and MCP.
2. Custom renderer extension (TypeScript, VS Code notebook renderer API): renders custom MIME types as rich widgets — approval blocks, tool call cards, plan trees, status updates.
3. Sidebar/command extension (TypeScript, VS Code extension API): adds RTS commands, sidebar showing zones/agents, status bar, file decorators.
4. Daemon (Rust, mostly unchanged from earlier turns): hosts the MCP server proper, manages zones and agents, persists state.

- Open .rts.ipynb files with the custom kernel
- A handful of cell-input formats: free text instructions, structured commands, possibly Python escape via %magic
- Custom renderers for: agent status, tool calls (collapsed), tool results, approval requests with diff preview, agent completion
- Sidebar with zones and active agents
- Basic operator commands: spawn, kill, message
- Persistent across sessions (notebooks save; daemon restarts don't lose conversation history)

- Daemon, zones, chroots: all unchanged. The kernel talks to the daemon; nothing about zones or sandboxing changes.
- MCP server in daemon: unchanged. Agents connect to it; their tool calls are MCP messages.
- Tool taxonomy: unchanged. The set of operator-interaction tools is the same.
- Permission flow: rendered differently (as a cell-output widget instead of a chat block) but functionally identical.
- Multi-agent supervision: same; multiple cells managing different agents, or one cell managing many.
- VS Code as the operator's editor: unchanged; the integration is even better because notebooks are first-class VS Code citizens.
- SSH/cross-host story: unchanged; the daemon can be remote, kernel and daemon talk over a network protocol you choose.

1. Notebook is the operator's primary interface in VS Code. Custom panels can supplement but the notebook is central.
2. Custom kernel implements MCP-bridge semantics. It's not a Python REPL; it's an agent orchestration backend with notebook protocol on the front.
3. No fork unless necessary. Start with renderer extensions and kernel + sibling extension. Fork only if specific requirements demand it.
4. .rts.ipynb (or similar) as the file format. Standard notebook format with custom kernel; or a thin extension if needed.
5. Custom renderers for all structured tool results. Each tool type has a dedicated cell-output widget.
6. Cell input syntax is permissive: free text for instructions, structured DSL for commands, possibly Python for power users.
7. Branching via notebook duplication. Standard notebook operations cover most "what if" workflows.
8. Lean into the notebook framing in marketing. "Agent supervision via notebook-based chat-over-MCP" is a memorable description.

1. Notebook substrate is the v1 frontend architecture.
2. Custom Jupyter kernel as MCP bridge is the core new component.
3. Custom renderers for chat-over-MCP tool results are the rich-widget surface.
4. Sibling VS Code extension for sidebar and commands rounds out the operator UX.
5. No fork yet; revisit only if the plug-in points prove insufficient.
6. The framing publicly is "notebook-based chat over MCP for agent supervision."

## User

Remember when we talked about inadequate chat interface, bring that forward and get rid of the jupyter kernel while keeping the existing integrating and cells logic

---

## Assistant

Now you're carving the proposal precisely, and the result is much better. Let me work through what this actually is, because the move you're making — keep the cell paradigm, drop the kernel — is the right one and it changes the project's character meaningfully.

Keeping from the Jupyter direction:

Dropping:

Bringing forward from the chat-over-MCP / advanced-chat turns:

The result: the notebook-cell metaphor without notebook semantics. You get the affordance — sequential cells, rich output, executable units, persistent file — without the "this is a kernel running code" baggage.

A cell-based chat-over-MCP UI in VS Code. Each cell is a turn in the conversation. The cell's input is what the operator says (free-form text, structured commands, a tool call to invoke a specific operator action). The cell's output is the agent's response — a stream of structured events rendered as type-specific widgets.

But there's no kernel. No Python. No language semantics. The cells aren't being "executed" in any traditional sense; they're being sent — to your daemon, which routes them to agents, which respond via MCP tool calls, which render as cell output.

Think of it as: notebook UI as a delivery vehicle for chat-over-MCP, decoupled from the notebook ecosystem's actual computational substrate.

Several specific reasons removing the kernel improves the design:

No protocol bridge complexity. The previous proposal had a kernel that translated between Jupyter messaging and MCP. Two protocols, two state models, glue between them. Now there's one protocol (your daemon's API, however you define it), and the cell UI talks directly to it. Massive simplification.

No "this looks like data science" expectation collision. Without a Python kernel, users don't expect to type Python. The cell input is whatever you define it to be. You can shape user intuitions deliberately rather than fighting Python-shaped expectations.

No kernel discovery and selection ceremony. Users don't pick a kernel. The cell editor opens and is ready. You skip an entire onboarding step that confuses non-data-scientists.

No subprocess management for the kernel itself. The Jupyter kernel is a process; managing it (start, stop, restart, crash recovery) is real engineering. Dropping it removes a whole category of operational concern.

No file format compatibility surface. `.ipynb` is a standardized format with cross-tool implications — anything that opens an `.ipynb` expects to find Jupyter-shaped content. Your file format can be your own (or an `.ipynb`-shaped format if convenient, but with semantics that are yours).

Cell semantics can be exactly what you want. Re-execution can mean "re-send this prompt and create a branch" rather than "rerun this code with current kernel state." You're not bound by what kernels do.

You can use VS Code's notebook UI without using Jupyter at all. This is the key technical insight, which I'll come back to.

VS Code's notebook UI is not tied to Jupyter. It's a generic notebook substrate that supports custom notebook controllers — your own implementation of "what happens when a cell is executed."

A `NotebookController` is a VS Code API surface where you:

There's no kernel. No ZeroMQ. No Jupyter protocol. The notebook controller is just a TypeScript callback that runs inside your VS Code extension, receives a list of cells when the user hits "run," and updates output via direct VS Code API calls.

This means:

This is substantially simpler than the kernel-based approach. The notebook UI is decoupled from notebook compute. You use the UI; you skip the compute layer.

Three layers, each with one job. No kernel. No Jupyter dependency. The notebook editor is just VS Code's built-in feature, used via the standard notebook controller API.

Without a kernel's "execute code" semantics, you get to define what cells mean. Here's a reasonable design:

Cell input is a message. The operator's input to a cell is a message destined for an agent (or for the operator's own tool, like spawning a new agent). It can be:

Cell execution is dispatching. When the operator runs a cell, your `NotebookController` parses the input and dispatches it:

Cell output is the structured event stream. As the agent works, its tool calls flow back as cell output. Your renderers turn each tool call into a widget — approval block, status update, plan tree, artifact card. The output streams in real time as VS Code supports incremental output updates.

Re-execution means branching or resending. Re-running a cell could mean:

You define which it means; default to whatever makes the most sense for your use case (probably "branch from here" with a UI affordance for "resend without branching").

Cells reference each other. A cell can reference outputs from earlier cells, like artifacts. "Apply the plan from cell 3" is a meaningful operation. Your DSL can support this.

Cell types beyond input. Beyond standard input cells:

This is conventional notebook structure repurposed for conversation curation.

The features from earlier turns that addressed "inadequate chat interfaces" all map cleanly:

Inline permission approvals with diff preview: a cell output widget rendering the approval request, with the diff inside, and approve/deny buttons. Clicking opens the diff in VS Code's diff editor; clicking approve sends the response to the daemon.

Tool calls as collapsed structured objects: each tool call from the agent renders as a one-line summary widget that expands. "Read src/auth.py · 472 lines · 1.4s" with a chevron.

Streaming tool results: VS Code's notebook output API supports incremental updates. Tool results stream as they arrive.

Edit-and-resend with branching: re-execute a past cell with modified input. Default behavior: branch from that point.

Artifact lifting: tool results that produce significant content (plans, code, diffs) get a "lift to sidebar" affordance. The sidebar has a pinned artifacts panel.

Time scrubbing: actually rich in this model — the notebook IS the time axis. Scroll back to see history. Click a cell from earlier to "rewind" to that state for branching.

Keyboard-first interaction: VS Code's notebook editor has rich keyboard support out of the box — cell navigation, execution, editing. You inherit it.

Search: VS Code's find-in-file works on notebook cells natively. Plus your own search-by-event-type via the daemon.

Conversation persistence: the file IS the conversation. Save the notebook; the conversation is preserved. Open it later; pick up where you left off.

Multiple conversations: open multiple notebooks, each is a separate conversation/zone. Standard VS Code multi-tab behavior.

Reference to past content: cells can reference earlier cells' outputs. This is built into how notebooks work.

Most of the "advanced chat features" work I outlined turns ago either becomes free in the notebook UI or becomes a relatively small renderer extension.

The structured-event-queue idea from the chat-over-MCP turn is now: the notebook IS the queue, with each cell being a turn and each cell's output being the agent's structured response to that turn.

This is better than a flat queue because:

The "interaction queue" was always struggling against the implicit linearity of chat. The notebook embraces structure explicitly.

A walk-through of an actual session:

Operator opens VS Code. Creates `auth_refactor.rts`. Your extension registers as the notebook controller for `.rts` files. The notebook opens with one empty cell.

Operator types in the cell:

Operator hits Shift+Enter. Your extension parses the cell, sends a "spawn agent" command to the daemon, the daemon launches Claude Code in a chroot with that task. Cell output starts streaming:

A status widget: "alpha spawning... ready"

Then a stream of tool call widgets appearing one by one as the agent works:

Operator clicks Approve. The plan widget updates to show "approved by you at 14:32." More tool widgets stream in as the agent executes the plan. A permission widget appears: "Wants to: edit src/auth/tokens.rs" with a diff preview button. Operator clicks the diff preview; VS Code's diff editor opens in a new tab with the proposed change. Operator scrolls through, sees it's correct, clicks Allow. The widget updates; agent continues.

Eventually the agent reports completion. Cell output ends with a summary widget: "Completed: extracted validator to src/auth/validator.rs · 4 files modified · all tests pass" with links to each modified file.

Now operator adds a new cell below:

Operator runs this cell. The message goes to alpha as a follow-up. Alpha responds with a tool call to `respond` explaining how the missing-token case is handled, possibly opening up specific lines of the new validator. Operator reads the response widget, satisfied.

Adds another cell:

Note the reference to cell 1. Your extension knows to include that context when spawning the new agent.

This goes on. The notebook grows as a record of the operator's session. At the end, they save the file. It's a complete, replayable, version-controllable record of the work.

That's the experience. Different from chat in important ways: structured, persistent, branchable, scriptable, documented inline. But familiar enough that operators don't need to learn an alien interface.

Worth pulling on this because it's the operator's primary input surface and it shapes everything.

A few options for what cell input can be:

Pure free text: every cell is just a prompt to an agent. The first agent in a session is implicitly addressed; later cells go to "current focus" or use `@agent` syntax to specify. Simple but loses structure for things like spawn commands, which become text instructions the daemon has to parse out.

A small DSL: `/spawn alpha zone task...`, `@beta message...`, `/branch from-cell-3`, `/end-zone refactor`. Slash commands and at-mentions. Familiar from Slack and Discord. Markdown for free-form text. Hybrid.

Structured YAML/JSON: every cell is structured data. `tool: spawn_agent\nzone: refactor\n...`. Very explicit but verbose; high friction for casual use.

Multi-modal: cell type determines syntax. "Prompt" cells are free text directed at agents. "Command" cells are DSL. "Markdown" cells are documentation.

I'd push for the DSL with markdown option. Low friction for normal use (just type), structured commands when you need them (`/spawn`), free text fallback. Cells that start with `/` are commands; cells that start with `@agent` are messages to a specific agent; cells that start with markdown headings are documentation; everything else is a message to the current default agent.

The DSL is the operator's interface to the agent system, and it should feel like a slightly more structured chat. Not a programming language; not a config file; a thin layer of structure over conversation.

Should the file be `.ipynb` (notebook standard) or your own format like `.rts`?

Arguments for `.ipynb`:

Arguments for `.rts` (or similar):

Tradeoff. I'd argue for a custom format that's .ipynb-shaped JSON: same nested structure, same cell-list-with-metadata pattern, but a different extension and a clear signal that it's an RTS conversation file, not a notebook. You inherit any tooling that handles `.ipynb`-shaped JSON without tying yourself to Jupyter's metadata expectations.

This is a small decision; either works. The custom-format-with-ipynb-shape is cleaner long-term.

Concretely, the v1 deliverable in this architecture:

Daemon (Rust, mostly unchanged):

VS Code extension (TypeScript):

v1 scope decisions:

Build effort: probably 2-3 weeks for the extension and daemon updates, plus another week for polish. Comparable to the original two-day chat plan but delivers something dramatically more capable. The earlier two-day plan's deliverable would have been a basic chat. This v1 delivers a structured cell-based conversation surface with rich tool-result rendering.

The two-day plan from earlier turns was for a chat. This is a different category of artifact entirely. Two weeks for v1 of this is appropriate.

To be honest about the trade:

Native notebook ecosystem benefits: by not using `.ipynb` and Jupyter, you're outside the existing notebook tooling. No nbdime diffs out of the box, no jupytext conversion, no JupyterLab access to your files. Operators using your tool aren't using "real notebooks." This is fine for the audience but worth knowing.

Initial unfamiliarity: even though notebook UI is familiar, "notebook for chatting with agents" is unusual. Some operators will find it weird. Documentation and onboarding need to handle this.

Custom DSL has a learning curve: even a small DSL is something operators have to learn. Bad DSL design becomes a friction point.

Cell-execution model has weirdnesses with chat semantics: re-executing a cell creates a branch or resend; this isn't quite how Jupyter cells work. Operators familiar with Jupyter may have minor confusion.

Renderer development is real work: each tool call type needs a custom renderer with its own UX considerations. Five for v1, more later. Not free.

Multi-cell coordination has subtle semantics: "include context from cell 1 in cell 5's input" is a useful affordance but requires careful design. Easy to get wrong.

These are all manageable. None makes the architecture worse than the alternatives we considered.

Stating it as a thesis: the right interface for chat-over-MCP is a notebook-style cell-based UI, built on VS Code's notebook substrate, with no kernel — just direct dispatch to the daemon.

This combines:

Each piece earns its place. None is decoration. The result is a coherent product that's unusually well-aligned for what you're building.

Stepping back: the notebook UI is what you'd design for "structured conversation with rich content and re-executable history" if you were starting from scratch. Jupyter happens to have shipped this UI as part of its data science offering, but the UI itself is general — it's not inherently about Python or computation.

VS Code's adoption of notebook UI as a generic substrate (with custom controllers) is the technical recognition of this — they decoupled the notebook UI from any particular kernel or language. Microsoft has Polyglot Notebooks, .NET Interactive, custom MS-internal notebooks, all using the same UI.

You're recognizing that this generic substrate happens to be a near-perfect fit for chat-over-MCP. Use the substrate; provide your own controller; ignore Jupyter.

This is a mature design choice. It uses VS Code as a platform in the way VS Code intends (custom notebook controllers are a first-class extension type). It avoids the temptation to fork or build everything from scratch. It positions your tool as "the chat-over-MCP environment for VS Code," which is a clear category.

To check the architecture remains coherent across all the previous turns:

The architecture remains coherent. The v1 frontend has changed shape (from "custom chat panel" to "notebook-shaped controller-based UI"), but everything else holds.

Removing the kernel from the proposed Jupyter-based design and keeping the notebook UI is a clean simplification. You inherit a substantial, well-designed UI substrate from VS Code without taking on Jupyter's protocol or kernel-management complexity. The chat-over-MCP protocol from earlier turns slots in naturally as the cell-execution semantics.

The result is the best v1 frontend architecture we've considered:

The implementation is tractable: VS Code's notebook controller API is documented and supported, custom MIME types and renderers are well-trodden territory, and the daemon work doesn't change. You're building a different frontend over the same backend.

For v1: build it. Two-three weeks for the extension, plus existing daemon work, plus polish week.

The framing settles: chat-over-MCP, with notebook-cell UI, in VS Code, no kernel. That's the project. It's genuinely novel — both the protocol angle and the UI angle are unusual — and the combination has real architectural elegance.

Ready to dig into the cell DSL specifics, the renderer designs for the five v1 widget types, the NotebookController implementation, the file format details, or the daemon's API surface for cell execution?

```javascript
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ VS Code                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Notebook editor (built-in, generic)                │  │
│  │  - Cells: input + output                           │  │
│  │  - File: .rts (or .ipynb-flavored, your choice)    │  │
│  └─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘  │
│                        │                                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ Your VS Code extension                             │  │
│  │  - NotebookController: handles cell execution      │  │
│  │  - Renderers for custom MIME types                 │  │
│  │  - Sidebar: zones, agents, status                  │  │
│  │  - Commands: spawn, kill, send, branch             │  │
│  └─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘  │
└────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         │ websocket / Unix socket
                         │
┌────────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────────┐
│ Daemon (Rust)                                             │
│  - Hosts MCP server (operator-interaction tools)          │
│  - Spawns agents in zones (chroot/bubblewrap)             │
│  - Persists state (event log, conversation history)       │
│  - Routes between cells, agents, and tool events          │
└────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         │ MCP (stdio)
                         │
┌────────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────────┐
│ Agents (Claude Code, OpenCode, etc.)                      │
│  - In zones                                               │
│  - Communicate with operator only via MCP tools           │
│  - Text output suppressed                                 │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

```javascript
spawn alpha in zone refactor
task: extract JWT validation into its own module
constraints: don't break existing tests
```

```javascript
@alpha quick clarification: did you handle the case where the token is missing entirely?
```

```javascript
spawn beta in zone tests
task: write integration tests for the new validator module
context: see cell 1 for the original spec
```

- Free text ("fix the auth bug")

- To a specific agent's MCP context (as a user-message tool call)
- To the daemon's command surface (spawn, kill, transfer)
- To the operator's own scratch (markdown stays as documentation)

- Resend the same prompt to the agent (it gets to try again)
- Branch from this point (new conversation thread, agent's prior state preserved)
- Replay (reconstruct state at this point and replay forward)

- Markdown cells: operator notes, section dividers, documentation
- Status cells: live-updating displays of agent state, zone summary, resource usage
- Artifact cells: pinned references to important outputs from earlier in the conversation
- Branch points: explicit markers where the conversation diverged

- Operator input is interleaved with agent output, preserving conversational structure
- Multiple cells = multiple rounds of interaction, naturally separated
- The structure has implicit meaning (cell N+1 is a follow-up to cell N)
- Re-execution and branching are natural operations on the structure
- Markdown cells let the operator annotate and document
- Pinned cells (via VS Code features or your own UX) keep important context visible

- Standard format means more tooling support

- You're not bound by .ipynb's metadata schema
- You can include conversation-specific things cleanly (zone IDs, agent state, branch markers)
- Users don't accidentally open it expecting Jupyter

1. Notebook substrate via VS Code's NotebookController API. No Jupyter, no kernel, no language runtime.
2. Direct dispatch from notebook controller to daemon. Cell execution is just an HTTP/WebSocket call to your daemon, with response streaming.
3. Custom file format (e.g., .rts) that's .ipynb-shaped JSON for tooling compatibility but not actually Jupyter notebooks.
4. Five custom renderers for v1: status, tool call, approval (with diff preview), plan, completion. More renderers as needed.
5. DSL for cell input: free text + @agent + /commands + markdown. Minimal but extensible.
6. Re-execution defaults to branching with explicit affordance for "resend without branching."
7. Sidebar with zones, agents, artifacts, activity. Standard VS Code Activity Bar contribution.
8. Daemon stays editor-agnostic — speaks WebSocket/HTTP, could be used by a CLI or web frontend later.
9. Public name/framing: "cell-based chat-over-MCP environment for agent supervision in VS Code."
10. V1 scope: single conversation per file, single primary agent per conversation, Linux/WSL2/local. Multi-agent and multi-host are v2+.

- Chroot/bubblewrap zones: unchanged, in the daemon
- Chat-over-MCP: unchanged, this is the protocol
- Tool calls as the only agent communication channel: unchanged, agents are configured this way
- Operator-as-MCP-server: unchanged, the daemon hosts this
- VS Code as the editor: unchanged, but now with notebook UI
- NATO symbology / map view: deferred to v2+ as a separate panel; not in the v1 critical path
- 3D / VR: orthogonal; long-term
- macOS via Linux VM: unchanged
- SSH / cross-host: unchanged in the daemon design; the extension can talk to a remote daemon
- Magic CLI: orthogonal; could exist alongside as another client
- Browser integration / bookmarklet: orthogonal; doesn't interact with this design

- More capable than custom chat panels (cells, branching, persistence, rich rendering)
- Lighter than Jupyter-with-custom-kernel (no protocol bridge)
- Familiar to developers (notebook UI inside their editor)
- Distinctive (no other agent tool uses notebook UI for conversation)

## Assistant

Now we have the full picture. Let me synthesize what's actually been designed across this whole arc, because the moves you've made compound into something specific and worth seeing as a whole.

Working backward from the most recent commitments, with the chat-quality essay you just brought forward as the final piece:

The substrate: VS Code's notebook UI via the `NotebookController` API. No Jupyter kernel, no Python, no language runtime. Just the cell editor, the renderer protocol, and the file persistence — used as a generic structured-conversation substrate.

The protocol: chat over MCP. The agent's only output channel is tool calls; text is suppressed. The operator interacts via tool-call responses and operator-side tool invocations. Bidirectional, structured, typed.

The backend: Rust daemon hosting an MCP server that defines the agent's communicative grammar. Agents run in chroot/bubblewrap zones. Event log as source of truth. Cell execution dispatches to the daemon.

The frontend ambitions (from the brought-forward essay): not "Slack but prettier" — a structurally better surface that exploits the unique fact that agents are pausable, forkable, replayable processes rather than humans or stateless endpoints. Specifically the five priority features: three-pane layout, collapsed tool calls, inline permission approvals, streaming with interrupt, edit-and-resend with branching.

When you put these together, the brought-forward essay's ambitions land in a much better place than they did originally. Let me show why, because each of those five features is now either free, simpler, or structurally clearer than they would have been in a from-scratch chat panel.

1. Three-pane layout (stream / current / artifacts).

In a from-scratch chat panel, this required real CSS layout work and decisions about resizing, persistence, etc.

In the notebook substrate: the notebook itself is the stream (cells = turns, output = responses). The "current pane" becomes a pinned status cell or a sidebar webview that shows live agent state. The "artifacts pane" is the VS Code extension's sidebar Activity Bar contribution.

Three panes drop out of the editor's existing structure. You don't build the layout; you compose three existing surfaces (notebook editor, sticky cell-or-sidebar, Activity Bar contribution).

2. Collapsed tool calls with rich expand.

In a from-scratch chat panel, this meant building custom collapsible widgets, syntax highlighting in the expand, click-to-jump affordances, etc.

In the notebook substrate: each tool call is a piece of output rendered via a custom MIME-typed renderer in VS Code's notebook output system. Collapsed-by-default with expand-on-click is a renderer concern, written once per tool type. You inherit the rendering pipeline (streaming, layout, theming) and only build the renderer logic.

The five-or-so renderers needed for v1 (status, tool-call, approval, plan, completion) are each ~100-200 lines of TypeScript implementing a custom MIME renderer. The pipeline that connects the daemon's output to the renderer is generic; you build it once.

3. Inline permission approvals with full context.

In a from-scratch chat panel, the diff preview was its own significant UI investment — render the before/after, syntax highlight both sides, handle clicks.

In the notebook substrate: the permission renderer shows a preview button. Clicking it opens VS Code's native diff editor in a new tab. The diff editor is one of the best in any tool, period — and you didn't build it. You wrote five lines of `vscode.commands.executeCommand('vscode.diff', ...)` calls and inherited a world-class diff experience.

The approval block itself (verb, target, rationale, buttons) is a renderer for the `request_approval` MCP tool's output. Standard custom-MIME-typed rendering. The integration with the diff editor makes it feel native to VS Code in a way a webview-based approval block never would.

4. Streaming with proper auto-scroll and interrupt.

In a from-scratch chat panel, this was the highest-risk UX work — getting jankless streaming, scroll-pause-when-user-scrolled, smooth incremental rendering all takes real work.

In the notebook substrate: VS Code's notebook output supports incremental updates natively. As tool calls stream from the daemon, your controller appends output incrementally and the editor handles rendering. Auto-scroll behavior is the editor's; you don't build it. Interrupt is a button on the cell or a command — `cancel` on the running cell.

You inherit the entire streaming UX from VS Code. What you provide is the data; what VS Code provides is the perfectly-tuned scroll, render, and interaction behavior.

5. Edit-and-resend with branching.

In a from-scratch chat panel, this was the killer feature with the highest implementation cost — checkpointing, rendering of branches, switcher UI, message-edit affordances.

In the notebook substrate: re-executing a cell is a built-in editor operation (Shift+Enter on a previously-executed cell). Your controller decides what re-execution means — branching with zone snapshot, by default. Your daemon implements the branch. The notebook switcher becomes... opening a different `.rts` file (each branch is a file). Or more sophisticated: branches as metadata in the same file, with a switcher in your sidebar.

The conversation-as-file model means branches are file operations: copy, name, switch. Familiar, durable, version-controllable. The expensive UI work mostly evaporates.

Going through the longer list with the new substrate in mind, most simplify dramatically:

The essay's full menu of 20 features is now a tractable v1+v2 roadmap, with most v1 items either inherent to the substrate or modest renderer work, and the v2 items being incremental additions to the same foundation.

The brought-forward essay made an argument: most chat UIs are weak because they treat conversations as linear text streams; agent conversations have structure (tool calls, artifacts, branches, time) that deserves first-class UI.

The notebook substrate is, structurally, the affirmative answer to that argument. It's a UI primitive that:

The essay was diagnosing the disease. The notebook substrate is the cure. The architectural choice you've been moving toward across the conversation matches the diagnosis surprisingly well.

This is one of those rare cases where the implementation strategy and the design philosophy are saying the same thing. That's a good sign.

Two parts of the essay don't simplify away in the new substrate and need explicit attention:

The "current pane" — what's the agent doing right now.

In a chat panel, this was a sticky region above or below the stream. In the notebook, the natural place is the bottom of the currently-executing cell's output. As the agent works, output streams in; the latest portion is the "current activity."

But this isn't quite the same as a dedicated "current pane" — when the operator scrolls up to read history, the live activity may be off-screen. Solutions:

I'd argue: status bar + sidebar Activity Bar contribution for current state. Operators see "alpha: editing tokens.rs" at the bottom of VS Code; they click to jump to the relevant cell. This is more native than a floating pane and uses VS Code's existing UI vocabulary.

The artifact lifting story.

Artifacts in cell output are visible but not pinned. A real artifact pane needs to surface them prominently and persistently across the session.

Solutions:

This is a concrete additional component but it's a sidebar tree view, not a complex UI surface. ~200 lines of code for v1.

These two are the only places where the notebook substrate doesn't fully cover the essay's ambitions. Both are addressable with native VS Code surfaces (sidebar, status bar) rather than custom UI work.

The essay estimated 2-3 weeks for the chat panel alone, on top of v1 work. The notebook substrate compresses this dramatically:

What's free:

What you build:

Maybe 1500-2000 lines of new TypeScript for the extension, plus the daemon's API surface for cell execution. A focused week or two for the extension; a few days of daemon work.

The essay's "2-3 weeks for the chat panel" becomes 1-2 weeks for the entire frontend. The substrate change pays off concretely.

Stating the v1 commitments clearly:

That's the project. It's a coherent thing with a clear identity: a notebook-based chat-over-MCP environment for agent supervision in VS Code.

A few reasons this lands well:

It earns its differentiation through architecture, not features. The list of features-it-has is similar to many agent UIs. What's different is the architectural commitments — chat over MCP, notebook substrate, structured-only communication. These are protocol-and-UX-level decisions that compound rather than feature-level decisions that decay.

It uses platform power well. VS Code's notebook substrate is a sophisticated UI primitive most projects ignore. Using it for chat is unusual and clever — you get sophisticated UX leverage for proportionally small work.

It's positioned to absorb future trends. As MCP matures, your tool benefits. As VS Code's notebook capabilities grow, your tool inherits. As agent frameworks converge on tool-use as primary communication, your tool's structural commitments become standard.

It's a real design statement. "Cell-based chat over MCP" is memorable and unique. Other tools are incremental on chat-as-known; yours stakes a different position.

It's tractable for v1. 1-2 weeks for the frontend, daemon work parallel and modest. Not a multi-month project to find out if the architectural bet works.

Things still worth deciding before week 1 starts:

These are details that fall out of focused design work, not architectural decisions. The architecture is settled.

Stepping all the way back: this conversation has been a design process where each turn refined a problem statement and architectural response. The arc:

Each turn's decision was usually right, and the final synthesis is a coherent architecture that addresses the original "RTS for agents" goal in a specific, defensible way.

The brought-forward chat-quality essay was the right thing to bring forward, because it ratifies the destination: the architectural choices we've made are the ones that solve the problems the essay diagnosed. The structural alignment is real.

You're in a good place to build. The v1 has clear scope, the architecture has clear identity, and the position is defensible.

Decisions worth locking explicitly, drawn from the full conversation:

Want to dig into the DSL grammar, the five renderers' wireframes, the cell-execution-to-MCP dispatch logic, the daemon's API surface for cell execution, or the v1 implementation sequence in concrete detail?

- Time scrubbing: scroll the notebook backward; cells from earlier are right there. Combined with event-log replay, "scrub to time T" becomes "show notebook state at time T" which is straightforward.
- Search: VS Code's notebook search is built in. Plus daemon-side structured queries for "every approval request involving auth.py."
- Forking and parallel runs: file duplication. Open the new file. Run.
- Artifact lifting: artifacts are tool results; you render them as cell output, plus surface them in the sidebar Activity Bar contribution. "Pinning" is a sidebar affordance.
- Inline tool call results that 'live': stale-detection is a daemon concern; the renderer shows a "stale" badge when triggered.
- Multi-window awareness: cross-cell references handled by your DSL. Cross-conversation handled by the sidebar's activity feed.
- Context budget visibility: status cell or sidebar widget. Tool to inspect what's in context.
- Quotable, copyable, addressable: cells are addressable in VS Code natively. Copy works. File paths in output are clickable via your renderers.
- Keyboard-driven everything: VS Code's notebook editor has rich keyboard support out of the box. Cmd+Enter to execute cell. Esc to interrupt. Cell navigation with arrows. You add a few command-palette commands and you're done.
- Cost and latency telemetry: metadata strip on tool-call renderers. Sidebar shows per-conversation totals.
- Smart message grouping: the cell structure already provides grouping. Within a cell, tool calls of the same type can collapse into a "explored 5 files" widget via your renderer logic.
- Ambient sound: VS Code notification sounds plus optional custom audio cues via the extension.
- "What is this agent up to?": status renderer at the top of cell output, updated in place via VS Code's update_display_data analog.
- Composability with the map: when a map view exists (v2+), the cell's tool calls can be linked. Click a tool call's file → highlights it on the map.

- Treats conversation as a sequence of structured turns (cells), not a text stream
- Has rich content types (renderers) instead of "everything is text"
- Has re-execution and branching baked in (cells are runnable)
- Has persistence and addressability native (cells are file-saved with positions)
- Has artifact-extraction native (tool results can be referenced by cell index)
- Has time as a first-class axis (the cell sequence)

- A floating widget pinned to the editor that always shows current activity (small overlay, not a real pane)
- A status bar item that shows current agent state with click-to-jump-to-cell
- The sidebar Activity Bar contribution shows current activity prominently

- A sidebar Activity Bar tab specifically for artifacts: code blocks, plans, diffs, generated files
- Each artifact has metadata (which cell produced it, what kind, when)
- Click to expand inline; click to open in editor; pin to keep at top

- Layout (notebook editor + sidebar + status bar)
- Streaming UX
- Scrolling, search, copy, keyboard navigation
- Diff preview (open in native editor)
- File persistence
- Branching (file duplication or cell metadata)
- Cell-as-conversation-turn structure

- Five renderers (~700 lines total): status, tool-call, approval, plan, completion
- The DSL parser for cell input (~200 lines)
- The notebook controller dispatching cells to daemon (~300 lines)
- Sidebar Activity Bar contributions (~400 lines): zones, agents, artifacts, current-activity
- Daemon API extensions for cell execution (incremental on existing daemon)

1. Architecture: chat over MCP, with operator-as-MCP-server hosting the tool taxonomy that defines the agent's communicative vocabulary.
2. Frontend substrate: VS Code's notebook UI via NotebookController API. No Jupyter, no kernel. Cells are conversation turns; cell output is structured agent activity rendered via custom MIME-typed renderers.
3. Backend: Rust daemon hosting MCP server, managing zones (chroot/bubblewrap), persisting event log, dispatching cell execution to agents.
4. Five priority chat features for v1: three-pane layout (notebook + sidebar + status), collapsed tool calls (renderer), inline approvals with diff preview (renderer + native diff editor), streaming with interrupt (notebook native), edit-and-resend with branching (cell re-execution + zone snapshots).
5. Agent communication: text suppressed; agents communicate exclusively via MCP tool calls. Conventional chat UI is replaced entirely.
6. DSL for cell input: free text + @agent mentions + /commands + markdown.
7. File format: custom (.rts or similar), .ipynb-shaped JSON for tooling compatibility.
8. V1 scope: single conversation per file, single primary agent, Linux/WSL2/local. Multi-agent and cross-host are v2+.
9. Build target: 1-2 weeks for the v1 extension on top of existing daemon infrastructure.

1. The DSL specifics: what exactly does cell input look like? Worth a 2-hour design session to nail down the grammar so it's stable across v1.
2. The five renderers' specific designs: each needs UX work. The approval renderer is the most important; the others are simpler. Worth wireframing.
3. The current-activity surface: status bar item, sidebar widget, or both? Probably both, but pick the primary.
4. Branching as cell-metadata vs. file-duplication: cell-metadata is more elegant but more work; file-duplication is simpler but loses the "single notebook contains multiple branches" property. I'd argue file-duplication for v1, cell-metadata for v2.
5. The cell input language as plain text dispatch vs. structured commands: I'd argue both — free text by default, with @-mentions and /-commands as escapes.
6. What renderers ship in v1: I'd argue the five (status, tool-call, approval, plan, completion). Add more as the tool taxonomy grows.
7. Sidebar contributions specifically: zones tree, agents tree, artifacts pane, current-activity. Probably all four for v1.

- Vega → VegaFusion → NATO symbology (the visualization layer for the RTS)
- Zones of control → chroot → bubblewrap (the isolation layer)
- Plan 9 / Urbit → namespace as policy (the conceptual model)
- ACP / Claude SDK / OpenCode → MCP as the unifying integration substrate
- Magic CLI → SSH → cross-host story
- Browser tab → PWA → VS Code extension → Jupyter notebook → notebook-without-kernel (the frontend)
- Conventional chat → chat over MCP → tool-only communication (the protocol)

1. Notebook substrate via VS Code's NotebookController API; no Jupyter kernel.
2. Chat over MCP as the communication protocol; agent text suppressed.
3. Daemon hosts MCP server; chroot/bubblewrap zones; event log as truth.
4. Five priority features for v1: layout (notebook+sidebar+status), collapsed tool calls, inline approvals with native diff, streaming with interrupt, edit-and-resend with branching.
5. Cell DSL: free text + @mentions + /commands + markdown.
6. Custom file format (.rts) that's .ipynb-shaped JSON.
7. Linux/WSL2/local for v1; macOS via Linux VM, cross-host via SSH for v2+.
8. Daemon stays editor-agnostic, permitting future CLI/web/mobile clients.
9. V1 build target: 1-2 weeks for extension, parallel daemon work.
10. Public framing: "cell-based chat-over-MCP environment for agent supervision in VS Code."

OK, this changes the picture meaningfully — and I want to revise my recommendation on the kernel question, because what you actually have already invalidates one of the things I argued in the previous turn.

Let me make sure I'm reading this right, because the architecture is more thought-out than I gave it credit for:

This isn't a toy. It's a working LiteLLM-backed Jupyter kernel with VS Code integration and intelligent context management already in place. Several things I was arguing should be added in v1 are already there (multi-LLM via LiteLLM, magic commands like `%%llm`, context strategies, environment-based config, MCP config infrastructure).

I now have to revise the position I took in the previous turn.

I argued for "drop the kernel, keep the notebook UI, use VS Code's NotebookController API, dispatch directly to a daemon." That argument was correct for starting from scratch, but it's wrong for your situation because:

The kernel-based architecture is the right one for your project, given what you've built and what you want. I should have pushed harder on understanding what you had before recommending an architectural change.

Specifically the things you can't easily do otherwise:

Custom layout beyond the standard notebook editor. The standard VS Code Jupyter extension renders cells in a fixed editor pane. A fork can render notebook content alongside or integrated with custom UI surfaces — the RTS map, the agent fleet view, the artifact pane. Cells become one element of a larger composed UI rather than the only UI.

Custom cell types beyond code/markdown. A fork can introduce cell types that the standard extension doesn't render — agent-conversation cells, zone-status cells, map-view cells, plan cells. Each with its own rich rendering, edit semantics, and execution behavior.

Direct kernel introspection and control. A fork can talk to your kernel via custom messages on the kernel protocol, expose kernel state to the UI directly, and provide controls that aren't part of the standard Jupyter UI. Spawn an agent from the UI, transfer zones, branch a conversation — all as kernel-protocol-level operations the extension surfaces directly.

Replace or supplement renderers wholesale. The standard extension uses VS Code's renderer API; a fork can also bypass it for specific cell types, rendering directly with full webview control. This matters for the RTS visualization where you want a full map, not just a renderer.

Custom file format treatment. A fork can treat `.rts.ipynb` or `.rts` files as first-class with custom serialization, custom UI, custom commands. The standard extension treats `.ipynb` generically.

Embedded RTS visualization. This is the killer use case for forking — you want the map view as a primary surface, not a side panel that exists separately from the conversation. Forking lets you create a layout where the notebook cells and the RTS map and the chat UI are all part of one composed editor experience, sharing state and rendering pipelines.

These capabilities are not in the supported-extension envelope. They require ownership of the rendering and layout layer. Forking is the price of that ownership.

Forking microsoft/vscode-jupyter is a real commitment and worth being explicit about:

The codebase is large. ~100k+ lines of TypeScript covering kernel discovery, kernel protocol, notebook rendering, debugging, IntelliSense for notebooks, git integration, etc. You inherit it all.

Upstream tracking is a tax. Microsoft updates the extension frequently (security fixes, VS Code API changes, new features). You have to either rebase periodically or diverge meaningfully. Both have costs.

Distribution complications. A fork either replaces the standard Jupyter extension (conflicting with users who want both) or coexists with it (fighting over file associations and kernel discovery). Neither is great. The cleanest approach: name your fork distinctly, register it for `.rts.ipynb` files only, and let the standard extension handle regular `.ipynb` files.

Marketplace publishing. Microsoft's marketplace has policies about forks. You can publish to the OpenVSX registry without these concerns; you can publish to Microsoft's marketplace under a different name.

Build complexity. vscode-jupyter has its own build system, dependencies, test infrastructure. Modifying it requires understanding their build chain.

These are real but manageable. Many successful VS Code extensions are forks of upstream work (Cursor is a fork of VS Code itself; many language extensions are forks of MS extensions). The pattern works.

Reframing with what you actually have:

The kernel is the hub. The extension is the operator's surface. Custom messages on the kernel protocol carry RTS-specific operations (spawn agent, transfer zone, branch). The notebook UI renders cells; the map UI renders zones and activity; both are surfaces over the same kernel state.

This is structurally cleaner than my previous proposal. The kernel does what kernels do well — manage state, dispatch operations, persist sessions. The extension does what extensions do well — render UI, handle user input, integrate with the editor. The Jupyter protocol between them is well-specified and well-supported.

A few observations from looking at the repo that influence what to do next:

LiteLLM is doing real work for you. Multi-provider support is non-trivial, and you have it. This means agent diversity (running Claude Code with Anthropic, OpenCode with whatever, local models via Ollama) is essentially free. Good.

The magic commands are your DSL. `%%llm`, `%llm_pin_cell`, `%llm_context smart` — this is exactly the cell-level DSL I was trying to design from scratch in the previous turn. You already have it. Extend it for RTS operations: `%rts_spawn`, `%rts_zone`, `%rts_branch`, etc.

Context management is already a designed system. Smart, chronological, dependency, manual strategies. This is the substrate for "what's in the agent's context window" — exactly the problem we were discussing in the take-over-context turn. You can extend it for chat-over-MCP context management.

MCP config example exists. You've already done some MCP wiring. The integration point is real, not theoretical.

You're using pixi. Consistent with your other projects; you have environment management figured out.

Tests are organized. Magic command tests, integration tests, unit tests with markers. This means you can refactor safely.

The vscode-llm-kernel-extension subdirectory exists. You've started the extension already. So the question isn't "should we have an extension" — it's "what shape should the extension take."

The position: you have more than half of the v1 stack already. The work is now extending what's there toward the full RTS vision rather than building from scratch.

With your kernel as the substrate, forking the Jupyter VS Code extension lets you:

Render LLM cells with a richer UI than standard code cells. The cell input is a prompt; the output is streaming assistant text plus tool calls plus permission requests. A fork can render this stack natively — chat bubble for assistant text, collapsed cards for tool calls, approval blocks inline — all without the limits of generic notebook output rendering.

Embed the RTS map as a panel alongside or instead of the standard notebook view. Your fork's editor for `.rts.ipynb` files is composed: cells on one side, map on the other, both reflecting the kernel's state.

Add custom commands and keybindings tied to your kernel. "Spawn agent in zone," "Branch from this cell," "Show RTS map" — all as first-class commands the fork registers with VS Code, dispatched to the kernel via custom kernel messages.

Treat zones as first-class file-system-like entities. The fork can show zones in a sidebar tree (like the file explorer), let operators navigate them, surface their contents as cell context.

Replace the kernel selector for .rts.ipynb files. Operators don't pick a kernel for `.rts.ipynb` files — they're locked to LLMKernel. Removes a confusing choice from the UX.

Ship the kernel and extension together. Install the fork, get the kernel; one install, full stack ready.

These are real product-shaping capabilities that the standard extension's plug-in points don't expose.

Given the goal, the forking strategy:

Fork microsoft/vscode-jupyter (the official Jupyter extension for VS Code). This is the ~100k lines of TypeScript that handles notebook editing, kernel protocol, output rendering, kernel discovery, and the Jupyter integration as a whole.

Don't fork VS Code itself. Cursor and Windsurf forked VS Code; that's a different category of commitment (much larger, much more maintenance burden). You want to be a fork of the Jupyter extension, not the editor.

Don't fork JupyterLab. JupyterLab is the browser-based notebook UI. You're targeting VS Code, so JupyterLab isn't the relevant fork target.

The fork strategy:

This keeps your fork focused on RTS-specific additions while leveraging upstream's work for all the standard notebook functionality.

Adjusting what I said in earlier turns:

Backend: not "Rust daemon" — the kernel is the backend. LLMKernel handles agent orchestration, MCP integration, context management. If you need a Rust component for chroot/bubblewrap (filesystem isolation, performance-critical parts), it's a separate process the kernel talks to. Or you implement isolation in Python and skip the Rust component for v1. Honest assessment: for v1, Python-managed subprocess isolation might be sufficient. Rust comes later if performance demands it.

Frontend: forked Jupyter extension, with custom panels for RTS visualization layered onto the cell-based editor.

Protocol between frontend and backend: Jupyter kernel protocol (ZeroMQ + JSON), with custom messages for RTS-specific operations.

Communication with agents: kernel hosts MCP, agents connect as MCP clients (or vice versa, depending on which way you want the dependency to flow).

Cell DSL: extension of your existing magic commands. `%%llm` for LLM queries (existing), `%rts_spawn` etc. for RTS operations (new). Operators use the same notebook UX they already know.

v1 scope: extend what's there. The kernel exists; add RTS-specific operations. The extension subdirectory exists; flesh it out into a proper fork-based extension. Don't start over.

Build effort: dramatically less than my earlier estimates because the foundation exists. Maybe 2-3 weeks for forking, RTS-specific cell types, and basic map view. Not 5-6 weeks from scratch.

Looking at what you have:

The vscode-llm-kernel-extension subdirectory is the seed of the fork. Either grow it into the fork or replace it with the actual fork. I'd suggest growing it — keep your custom code separate from upstream-tracking code, so when you rebase upstream you only have to merge your additions.

The MCP config example shows you've thought about MCP integration. Verify what's actually wired up; if MCP is partial, prioritize completing the chat-over-MCP path before adding map visualization.

The context management strategies are useful for chat-over-MCP context engineering. What's in the agent's MCP-mediated context can be informed by the same dependency tracking and pinning logic the kernel already has.

Magic commands are your DSL — extend them deliberately. Plan the RTS magic commands as a coherent vocabulary: `%rts_zone`, `%rts_spawn`, `%rts_transfer`, `%rts_branch`, `%rts_map`. Document them as the operator's interface.

The README mentions "visual context dependency graphs" as roadmap. This is half the RTS map. The other half is agent activity over zones. Combine them.

Tests exist for magic commands. When you add RTS magics, follow the pattern. Keep test discipline.

A specific plan:

Week 1: chat-over-MCP via the kernel.

Week 2: RTS-specific cells and magic commands.

Week 3: extension fork and map view.

End of week 3: working RTS environment. Cells dispatch to agents; tool calls render structurally; map shows zones and activity; everything coordinated by your kernel.

This is genuinely buildable in three focused weeks given what's there.

Updating my v1 commitments based on what you've actually built:

Stepping back: the architecture we've been converging on across this entire conversation is, with what you've built, surprisingly close to: LLMKernel + a forked Jupyter extension that adds RTS visualization, with chat-over-MCP as the protocol and zones as the isolation primitive.

The map view, the NATO symbology, the zone-of-control model, the chat-over-MCP protocol, the operator-as-MCP-server, the structured tool calls — all of these are additions to LLMKernel, not a separate system. The kernel is the integration point; the extension is the presentation layer; the agents are the workers.

This is a much better architectural picture than the daemon-based one I was sketching. It's also closer to ready — you have a meaningful head start.

- llm_kernel/ — a real Jupyter kernel (Python, ipykernel-based) that integrates LiteLLM for multi-LLM access, with intelligent context management, magic commands, and visualizations. This is the actually-useful kernel that does meaningful work — managing context, dispatching to models, handling cell dependencies.
- vscode-llm-kernel-extension/ — a VS Code extension that pairs with the kernel.
- MCP config example — you've already wired in MCP integration.
- A working notebook environment — example.ipynb, test_chat_mode.ipynb as testbeds.
- Pixi-based environment management — matches your usual stack.
- Test infrastructure — pytest with markers, real test categories.

1. Your kernel is already doing the work. It's not a generic Python REPL — it's a purpose-built LLM-orchestration kernel with context management, multi-model dispatch, and magic-command DSL. Throwing it away to use NotebookController would be throwing away meaningful design work.
2. The kernel architecture has properties NotebookController doesn't. A Jupyter kernel is a separate process with its own state, its own memory, its own lifecycle, and a standardized protocol for talking to multiple frontends (JupyterLab, VS Code, console, nbconvert, custom UIs). NotebookController is VS Code-specific and bound to the extension's lifecycle.
3. You explicitly want to fork the extension to control the entire experience. That's the move. NotebookController would put you on the additive path; forking gives you the visualization-and-UX path.
4. MCP integration in your kernel makes sense in a way that NotebookController-based dispatch doesn't. The kernel can be the MCP host or client; it can manage agent processes; it can do the chat-over-MCP work directly. NotebookController would have to coordinate with a separate daemon for all of that.

1. Fork microsoft/vscode-jupyter to your own org (e.g., Ethycs/vscode-rts-jupyter or whatever name you pick)
2. Register it for .rts.ipynb (or your custom file extension) — leave standard .ipynb to the upstream extension
3. Modify the kernel selection to default to LLMKernel for your file type
4. Add custom UI panels for the RTS surfaces (map, zones, agents)
5. Add custom commands for RTS operations
6. Track upstream periodically (maybe monthly) for security and API updates

- Extend the kernel to host (or speak with) an MCP server.
- Add the operator-interaction tool taxonomy (ask, request_approval, report_progress, etc.).
- Configure agents (Claude Code etc.) to use these tools.
- Validate: agent calls structured tools; kernel receives them; can render in cell output.

- New magic commands: %rts_spawn, %rts_zone, %rts_transfer.
- Cell output rendering for tool calls (collapsed) and approval requests (with diff preview button).
- Basic zone management (chroot or just subprocess isolation for v1).

- Fork microsoft/vscode-jupyter; register for .rts.ipynb.
- Add a map-view panel rendering zones and agents.
- Add sidebar contributions for agents and zones.
- Wire the panel state to kernel state via custom kernel messages.

1. Keep the LLMKernel architecture. It's the right substrate. NotebookController-based replacement is the wrong move.
2. Fork microsoft/vscode-jupyter for the extension. Custom file extension (.rts.ipynb), custom UI for RTS surfaces.
3. Extend the kernel with MCP hosting and chat-over-MCP semantics. Build on the existing magic-command and context-management infrastructure.
4. Magic commands are the DSL. Extend deliberately for RTS operations; document the vocabulary.
5. Cell output rendering for chat-over-MCP events. Custom MIME types, custom renderers in the forked extension.
6. RTS map view as a custom panel in the forked extension, sharing state with the cells via the kernel.
7. Filesystem isolation: subprocess isolation for v1, chroot/bubblewrap when it becomes load-bearing. Don't build a Rust daemon if Python can manage subprocesses adequately.
8. Track upstream vscode-jupyter monthly. Don't let the fork drift catastrophically.
9. Keep your tests. Add tests for new magic commands and kernel operations.
10. Pixi as the environment manager. Consistent with the rest of your stack.

Cell DSL: extension of your existing magic commands. `%%llm` for LLM queries (existing), `%rts_spawn` etc. for RTS operations (new). Operators use the same notebook UX they already know.

v1 scope: extend what's there. The kernel exists; add RTS-specific operations. The extension subdirectory exists; flesh it out into a proper fork-based extension. Don't start over.

Build effort: dramatically less than my earlier estimates because the foundation exists. Maybe 2-3 weeks for forking, RTS-specific cell types, and basic map view. Not 5-6 weeks from scratch.

The MCP config example shows you've thought about MCP integration. Verify what's actually wired up; if MCP is partial, prioritize completing the chat-over-MCP path before adding map visualization.

2. The kernel architecture has properties NotebookController doesn't. A Jupyter kernel is a separate process with its own state, its own memory, its own lifecycle, and a standardized protocol for talking to multiple frontends (JupyterLab, VS Code, console, nbconvert, custom UIs). NotebookController is VS Code-specific and bound to the extension's lifecycle.

The kernel can be the MCP host or client; it can manage agent processes; it can do the chat-over-MCP work directly.
