## Assistant

Good. Now I have enough to walk you through the actual architectural surface of vscode-jupyter and where the load-bearing decisions sit when you fork. This is more concrete than the previous turn — the extension has well-documented internal architecture and the right places to cut into it are not where you'd guess.

The codebase has four main code areas: notebooks (the integration with VS Code's notebook editor and controllers, responsible for `.ipynb` notebooks managed by VS Code's builtin serializer), interactive-window (the alternative split-editor view), webviews (extension-side and webview-side code for Variable view, data viewer, plot viewer), and standalone (decoupled features like the public API and IntelliSense) Claude API Docs.

The codebase is also split by environment via file extensions: `*.node.ts` for desktop-only code, `*.web.ts` for web-only, and unsuffixed files for shared isomorphic code, with separate entry points (`src/**/serviceRegistry.node.ts` for desktop) Claude API Docs. This matters because when you fork, every architectural decision has a desktop and a web answer, and you have to pick which environments you're targeting.

The kernel architecture has three load-bearing components: discovery (finding kernel specs on the user's machine, connecting to remote Jupyter servers, turning Python environments into Jupyter specs), kernel launcher (launching the selected kernel with proper environment), and execution (handling messages between extension and kernel) GitHub.

The kernel-discovery split is real and has different implementations: globally installed kernel specs are started as separate processes by default and the extension connects through ZeroMQ; Jupyter servers created outside the extension are connected via the Jupyter Server API; Python environments can be used as kernels by installing ipykernel and starting them as processes GitHub.

Then there's an extensibility layer: a public API for external partners to interface with, including capabilities like registering remote Jupyter server URI providers via `jupyterRemoteServerProvider` in package.json contributes and calling `registerRemoteServerProvider` from extension activation Openclaw.

That's the lay of the land. Now let me work through where the architectural decisions actually sit when you fork.

Given your goal — keep the kernel (LLMKernel), control the entire experience including RTS visualization, custom file format treatment, custom UI surfaces — there are exactly four architectural cuts you have to make. The rest of the fork is just inheritance of upstream's decisions.

Three options, ordered by how aggressive the fork is:

Option A: Coexist with upstream. Your fork registers under a different extension ID (`ethycs.vscode-rts-jupyter`), claims a different file extension (`.rts.ipynb` or `.rts`), and runs alongside Microsoft's extension. Operators install both. Standard `.ipynb` opens in Microsoft's; `.rts.ipynb` opens in yours.

This is the lightest touch. You get to make changes without breaking anyone's standard Jupyter workflow. The fork is a sibling, not a replacement.

Option B: Replace upstream for users who install yours. Your fork claims `.ipynb` itself, conflicting with the upstream extension. Operators have to disable Microsoft's. Aggressive, breaks normal workflows, only justified if your changes really do need to own the standard file type.

Option C: Hard fork with renaming. You fork, rename everything, target a separate audience (your tool's users specifically), and don't try to interoperate with upstream. Full ownership. Most maintenance burden but most freedom.

For your case, Option A is clearly right. The RTS use case is distinctive enough that a separate file extension is honest and avoids stepping on standard Jupyter usage. The "decision" then collapses into "what file extension and naming."

The upstream extension supports several kernel sources: globally installed kernel specs (your case for LLMKernel), Jupyter servers (remote), Python environments via ipykernel injection, and the Python extension's environment management. When you pick a kernel in the kernel picker, that kernel is associated with the file and automatically selected next time; without selection, the extension reads notebook metadata and picks a "suggested" kernel NVIDIA Developer.

For your fork, three sub-decisions:

Sub-decision 2a: Lock the kernel choice for .rts.ipynb files. Operators don't pick a kernel — your file format is bound to LLMKernel by metadata. This removes a confusing step and prevents users from accidentally running RTS notebooks against vanilla IPython.

Sub-decision 2b: Drop or keep the other discovery paths. The Jupyter-server discovery, remote-kernel support, and Python-environment integration are all plumbing for the standard data-science workflow. For RTS you don't need any of it. Cutting it simplifies the fork dramatically — but you lose remote-kernel support if you ever want it later.

I'd argue: keep the global-kernel-spec path (where LLMKernel lives), drop or hide the others for .rts.ipynb files. They can stay in the codebase for standard `.ipynb` if you decide to handle those too, but for RTS files the kernel is fixed.

Sub-decision 2c: Custom kernel-startup logic. When LLMKernel starts for an RTS notebook, it might need extra environment setup — MCP server config, zone bindings, agent registry. This is a custom step that goes in the kernel launcher path. Fork or extend the launcher to do this for RTS files.

This sub-decision is concrete code: a new conditional in the launcher that, for RTS files, sets specific env vars and command-line flags. ~50 lines of TypeScript.

This is the big one for the RTS visualization. The upstream extension renders cells via VS Code's built-in notebook editor (which uses NotebookController and renderer extensions). For RTS visualization, you have three places to intercept:

Intercept point A: Output rendering only. Custom MIME types in your kernel emit RTS-specific output (a tool call, a permission request, a status update). Your fork registers renderer extensions that handle these MIME types. The notebook editor itself is unchanged; only what's inside cell output is different.

This is the lightest intervention. The fork mostly inherits upstream's editor, just adds renderers. Renderers can be quite rich — they're webviews with full HTML/CSS/JS — so you can do meaningful UI inside them.

Intercept point B: Add custom panels alongside the editor. A new sidebar contribution, a custom webview panel for the RTS map, status bar items. The notebook editor renders cells normally; the map view sits beside it. The two communicate via your fork's coordinator code.

This is moderate intervention. The notebook editor stays untouched; the RTS map is a new VS Code surface alongside it. Coordination between the two is your responsibility.

Intercept point C: Replace or significantly modify the notebook editor itself. This is what would be required if you wanted, say, the cells to share screen real estate with the map view in a single composed editor surface, or if you wanted custom cell types beyond code/markdown that the standard editor can't render.

This is heavy intervention. The `notebooks` directory is "responsible for integrating Jupyter kernels into .ipynb notebooks managed by VS Code builtin serializer" Claude API Docs — modifying this means changing how the editor itself works, fighting VS Code's builtin notebook system, possibly running into the limits of what extensions can customize.

For v1, Intercept points A and B together are almost certainly enough. Custom renderers handle the rich tool-call output inside cells; a sibling panel hosts the RTS map. You don't need to modify the notebook editor itself, just compose around it.

The map-view panel is structurally similar to the existing webview-based features (Variable view, data viewer, plot viewer) YouTube, which gives you a template — your fork already has the patterns for "extension-side and webview-side code for a feature panel" in the `webviews` directory.

If you ever need Intercept C, you'd be looking at the `notebooks` directory's controller and editor integration. But you can defer that decision until you find a specific feature that requires it.

The RTS visualization (map view) needs to reflect the same state as the notebook (cells). When the agent makes a tool call, both the cell output and the map should update. When the operator approves a permission in the cell, the map should reflect the approved action. When the operator clicks a zone in the map, the relevant cells should highlight.

This cross-surface state coordination is the hardest design problem in the fork, and it's not solved by Jupyter's architecture — Jupyter assumes one editor showing one notebook, not a composed view.

Three approaches:

Approach A: Kernel as the source of truth. Both the notebook cells (via the kernel protocol) and the map view (via custom kernel messages) read from and write to the kernel's state. The kernel publishes state-change events; both surfaces subscribe.

This is the cleanest approach and matches the kernel's nature as a stateful process. Your kernel maintains the world model (zones, agents, events); both UI surfaces are views over it. State sync happens via kernel messages.

Approach B: Extension-side state with kernel as data source. The fork maintains its own state model in TypeScript, populated from the kernel. UI surfaces subscribe to the extension's state.

This decouples the UI from kernel protocol details but adds a synchronization layer (kernel state → extension state → UI). Extra complexity, minimal benefit unless you have non-kernel state.

Approach C: Per-surface state, no shared model. Cells render their own state; map renders its own state; operators use both independently. No coordination beyond what each surface gets from the kernel separately.

Simplest but loses the integration that makes the dual-view valuable.

For v1, Approach A is right. The kernel is already the central state holder; surfaces are views; coordination is via kernel messages with a custom message type for RTS-specific events. This matches the architecture you already have with LLMKernel.

The implementation is concrete: define a custom kernel message type (e.g., `rts.state_update`) that the kernel emits when zones, agents, or events change. The fork's coordinator code subscribes to these messages and dispatches updates to both the cell renderers and the map panel. ~300 lines of TypeScript.

Pulling these decisions together, here's what your fork actually has to do, concretely:

1. Branding and metadata changes. Rename the extension, change the publisher, update IDs. Register `.rts.ipynb` (or whichever extension you pick) as a new file type. This is mechanical but tedious — search-and-replace on `ms-toolsai.jupyter` to your IDs across `package.json`, manifests, command names. ~half a day.

2. File-type-specific kernel binding. For `.rts.ipynb` files, lock the kernel selection to LLMKernel; hide other kernel options. Add custom kernel-startup logic (env vars, MCP config, zone setup). ~one day.

3. Custom MIME type renderers. Define MIME types for RTS-specific outputs: `application/vnd.rts.tool-call+json`, `application/vnd.rts.permission-request+json`, `application/vnd.rts.status+json`, `application/vnd.rts.plan+json`, etc. Implement renderer extensions for each. ~one week for five renderers, with iteration.

4. RTS map view panel. New webview panel registered as a sibling of the cell editor. Initial content can be a simple p5.js or canvas-based map showing zones and agents. Subscribes to kernel state updates via custom messages. ~one week for a v1 map.

5. Sidebar contributions. Activity Bar item for RTS, with tree views for zones, agents, recent activity. Standard VS Code extension API; not Jupyter-specific. ~two days.

6. Custom kernel messages for RTS state. Define and implement the message types. Wire kernel emission in LLMKernel; wire reception in the fork. ~two days.

7. Cross-surface coordination. When a cell updates, optionally update the map; when the map is clicked, optionally focus a cell. ~two days.

8. Cut what you don't need. The fork inherits a lot of code for things you don't use: Python environment integration, Jupyter server discovery, IPyWidgets, notebook diff tools (maybe keep), data viewer, variable viewer, Interactive Window. You don't have to remove these — they can stay dormant, only activated for standard `.ipynb` files. But if you want a focused fork, prune them. ~one week for aggressive pruning.

Total scope: ~3-4 weeks of focused work for a v1 fork that does what you want, leveraging your existing LLMKernel.

The pruning is optional for v1. You can leave upstream's code alone, just register your overrides for `.rts.ipynb` files, and ship a working fork in ~2-3 weeks.

The upstream extension is large and rapidly evolving. Some practical fork strategy:

Fork from a specific stable commit, not main. Pick a recent release tag (their "stable" releases come every few weeks). This gives you a known-good baseline.

Plan to rebase periodically, not constantly. Microsoft pushes a lot of changes; tracking every commit is exhausting. Aim for a quarterly rebase against a recent stable release, with security-fix cherry-picks in between.

Identify the parts you actually modified. Keep a clear list: what files you changed, what you added new, what you removed. This makes rebases manageable. ~10-20 changed files for v1, probably; new files in their own directories.

Use the extension-environment split deliberately. The desktop-only paths (`*.node.ts`) are simpler than the web-environment paths (`*.web.ts`). For v1, target desktop only. Don't fork the web-environment code; let it stay broken or deleted. Web support comes later if it ever does.

Don't try to track every upstream feature. They'll add things; you don't need them. Pick what's relevant to your use case and let the rest drift.

Ship via OpenVSX, not VS Code Marketplace. Microsoft's Marketplace has policies that may complicate publishing a fork. OpenVSX is the open-source registry that VSCodium and Cursor use. The Jupyter extension is already on OpenVSX Substack as a precedent.

Stating them concretely so you can match against them as you work:

These are the architectural commitments. Everything else is implementation detail, working within these constraints.

Concrete week-1 plan, given the architecture:

Day 1: orientation. Clone vscode-jupyter. Build it from source. Run it locally. Open the codebase in VS Code; trace how a cell execution flows from user input through kernel discovery, launching, and output rendering. You're learning the codebase, not changing anything yet.

Day 2: rebrand and register. Fork the repo. Change extension ID, publisher, IDs. Register `.rts.ipynb` as a file type. Build and install your fork; verify it loads.

Day 3: kernel binding. Ensure that opening `.rts.ipynb` files automatically uses LLMKernel without prompting. Test the flow.

Day 4-5: first custom MIME renderer. Pick one (the tool-call renderer is a good first target). Define the MIME type. Make LLMKernel emit it for tool calls. Implement the renderer extension. Verify cell output shows your custom rendering.

End of week 1: you have a working fork that opens RTS files in your custom environment with LLMKernel, and one type of structured output renders custom in cell output.

This is the foundation. Weeks 2-4 build on it.

Forking vscode-jupyter is real work but tractable when you focus the architectural decisions on the four cuts that matter: coexistence with upstream, kernel binding for your file type, rendering intervention at the output (not editor) level, and kernel-state-as-truth for cross-surface coordination.

The fork doesn't need to touch most of upstream's code. The big areas — Python environment management, remote Jupyter server discovery, Interactive Window, IPyWidgets, debugging — can stay dormant or be cut later. Your additions sit in custom MIME renderers, a webview-based map panel, sidebar contributions, and a small amount of kernel-startup customization for your file type.

Total v1 fork scope: 3-4 weeks of focused work, with a usable foundation in week 1.

Decisions worth locking before you start cutting code:

Want to dig into the custom MIME renderer implementation specifics, the map view's webview communication pattern, the kernel-state message protocol design, or the detailed week-by-week build plan for the fork?

1. Coexistence with upstream: register a new extension ID and a new file extension (.rts.ipynb or similar). Don't claim standard .ipynb.
2. Kernel binding: lock LLMKernel for RTS files. Cut the other kernel-discovery paths from the RTS-file flow. Keep them for standard .ipynb only if you support those.
3. Custom kernel-startup: set RTS-specific env vars, MCP config, and zone setup when LLMKernel starts for an RTS file.
4. Rendering interception at output level: custom MIME types and renderers in cell output. Don't modify the notebook editor itself.
5. RTS map as sibling webview panel: model the map view on the existing webview features (Variable view, Plot viewer). Use the same extension-side + webview-side code pattern.
6. Sidebar Activity Bar contribution: new tree views for zones, agents, recent activity. Standard VS Code extension API.
7. Kernel state as source of truth: define custom kernel messages for RTS state. Both cells (via output) and map view (via webview messages) subscribe to kernel state.
8. Desktop-only for v1: drop or ignore the web environment code paths. Target Node-environment desktop VS Code.
9. Extension-environment split: maintain the upstream pattern of *.node.ts files for your additions, even if not strictly needed yet, for future flexibility.
10. Pruning is optional: leave upstream code dormant for v1; prune later if it gets in the way.

1. File extension: .rts.ipynb (compatible with .ipynb tooling) or .rts (clean break)? I'd argue .rts.ipynb.
2. Branding: name and publisher for the fork.
3. OpenVSX vs Marketplace: OpenVSX is the cleaner publishing target.
4. Desktop-only commitment: confirm and remove web-environment burden from your concerns.
5. First MIME type: which one to render first? Tool calls, probably, since they're most frequent.
6. Kernel-state message format: define the custom kernel message types for RTS state.
7. Map view technology: p5.js, canvas, SVG, or webview-embedded React? Pick once.

## Assistant

Now the constraints are sharp and the design clarifies into something with a clear shape. Let me work through what falls away and what remains, because the subtractive frame produces a meaningfully different fork than the additive one I was sketching.

Subtractive forking has a different character than additive. Additive forks tend to bloat: the original codebase plus your additions, with everything fighting for attention. Subtractive forks have a clarifying property: every line that survives is there because it earned its place by serving the new purpose. The fork ends up smaller than the original, more focused, with a clearer thesis.

For vscode-jupyter, subtractive is the right call because most of what's there is not relevant to your use case. Python environment management, IPyKernel injection, remote Jupyter server discovery, Anaconda integration, conda detection, IPyWidgets rendering pipeline, the Interactive Window, the data viewer, the variable viewer, the plot viewer, debug-by-line for Python, the entire Python-extension integration layer — none of these serve LLMKernel-as-sole-kernel.

What's left after the cuts is: a notebook editor that opens .rts.ipynb files and runs them against LLMKernel, with custom MIME rendering for LangSmith-shaped events, and a map view as a separate tab. That's a much smaller artifact than the original extension, and the shape is right.

Let me enumerate, because the cuts are the substance of the architectural decision:

Kernel discovery and selection. Upstream supports finding kernels in many places: Jupyter kernel specs, Python environments via the Python extension, remote Jupyter servers, conda envs. With LLMKernel as the sole kernel, you delete all of this. The kernel is hardcoded; there's no picker, no discovery, no selection logic. Saves substantial code in the `kernels` directory and removes the entire dependency on the Python extension.

Python extension integration. Upstream coordinates with `ms-python.python` for environment management, interpreter selection, ipykernel installation, and Python language services. None of this applies to LLMKernel. The fork drops the Python extension as a dependency entirely. This removes complex initialization code and a substantial inter-extension communication layer.

Remote Jupyter server support. Connecting to remote Jupyter servers, the URI provider system, the server-discovery UI — all gone. RTS notebooks run locally with LLMKernel; remote work is something different (handled at the daemon/SSH layer, not the kernel layer).

IPyWidgets. The IPyWidgets bridge is a large, complex subsystem for rendering interactive widgets defined in Python. RTS doesn't use it; you have your own rendering via custom MIME types. Cut it.

The Interactive Window. Upstream supports a split-editor view with a notebook on one side and a Python file on the other for "send to interactive." Not relevant to RTS. Cut.

Variable Viewer, Data Viewer, Plot Viewer. These are webview-based features specific to data-science workflows. RTS doesn't have variables in the Python sense; doesn't have dataframes; doesn't have matplotlib plots. Cut.

Notebook debugging. "Run by line," debug by cell — these are Python debugging features. LLMKernel doesn't need them. Cut.

Export/import beyond .ipynb. Upstream supports converting notebooks to HTML, PDF, Python scripts, etc. via nbconvert. RTS files don't need most of these. Maybe keep export-to-Markdown for sharing transcripts. Cut the rest.

The web environment. Upstream targets both desktop (Node) and web (web worker) VS Code. RTS is desktop-only for v1; the entire `*.web.ts` codebase can be deleted. This alone is a substantial reduction.

IntelliSense for notebooks. Upstream provides language services for cells (autocomplete, hover, go-to-definition). LLMKernel cells aren't Python in the conventional sense; they're prompts and structured commands. Cut the language-service integration; you'll provide your own completion for the DSL if you want it.

Notebook diff tool. Maybe keep this — it's useful for git diffs of `.rts.ipynb` files. Honest assessment: probably keep, it's relatively self-contained and useful.

Conda environment activation, kernelspec installation flows, troubleshooting commands for Python issues. All gone — there's no Python ecosystem to integrate with from the fork's perspective.

The kernel picker UI. Removed entirely. RTS files have one kernel; selection is automatic.

Most of the notebook controller's flexibility for non-Python kernels. Upstream has paths for Julia, R, .NET Interactive, etc. Cut. LLMKernel is the only kernel.

What remains after these cuts is, roughly:

That's a small percentage of the original codebase. The fork becomes lean.

This is a strong choice and worth examining specifically because it changes how renderers work and what LLMKernel emits.

LangSmith's tracing format (and similar formats from Langfuse, OpenTelemetry GenAI, OpenInference, etc.) is a structured representation of LLM/agent events that's becoming standardized. The shape:

Different providers structure these slightly differently but the core abstractions (typed runs, hierarchical tracing, structured I/O) are convergent. The OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions are pushing toward standardization.

Using this format as your I/O is genuinely smart for several reasons:

Familiar to developers building with LLMs. Anyone using LangChain, LangGraph, LlamaIndex, OpenInference, or just structured logging with their own LLM apps has seen this shape. The cognitive cost of "what does this event mean" is low.

Composable with existing tooling. Operators can pipe RTS event streams into LangSmith, Langfuse, Phoenix, Arize, or any observability tool that ingests this format. Your fork becomes part of an ecosystem rather than a silo.

Standardized enough to be a real protocol. You're not inventing a format; you're picking one that's increasingly canonical. Future agents and frameworks that emit LangSmith-shaped output integrate naturally.

Hierarchical tracing comes for free. The `parent_run_id` field gives you nested operations: an agent run contains tool calls, which contain LLM calls, which contain prompts. The map view can render this hierarchy; the cell output can render it; both speak the same data.

Distinguishes types naturally. `run_type` gives you the categorical distinction you need for type-specific renderers. Tool runs render one way; LLM runs render another; agent runs another.

Errors are first-class. Failed runs have `error` populated; renderers can show errors distinctively without requiring a separate error event type.

It's JSON. Trivially serializable, debuggable, queryable. Operators can `jq` event streams. Persistence is just newline-delimited JSON or a simple log structure.

Practically, this means: LLMKernel emits LangSmith-shaped run records as kernel output. The fork's MIME-type renderers consume them. The map view consumes them. The persistence layer stores them.

The existing Jupyter MIME-type system is good for this; you just rename and re-purpose it.

Standard Jupyter MIME types include `text/plain`, `text/html`, `application/json`, `image/png`, etc. — content-format-oriented. Your repurposed scheme is event-type-oriented:

The renderer registration is the same as upstream's — VS Code's renderer extension API — just with your MIME types. Renderers receive the JSON blob and produce DOM. Standard pattern.

The simplification: instead of inventing many fine-grained MIME types, you can have one primary MIME type (`application/vnd.rts.run+json`) and dispatch within the renderer based on `run_type` and tags. Fewer renderers to register; more flexibility within them. The renderer is a state machine over the LangSmith schema rather than a separate component per event type.

I'd argue for the consolidated approach: one renderer for application/vnd.rts.run+json that handles all run types via internal dispatch. Simpler to maintain; easier to extend (add a new `run_type` and the renderer's dispatch handles it without registering anything new). This matches how LangSmith's own UI handles arbitrary runs.

Making the map view a separate tab (rather than a sidebar panel or embedded in the notebook editor) is a clean choice. Specifically:

A VS Code "tab" in this context is most likely a custom editor or a webview panel that opens in the editor area. Not a sidebar (those are narrower and constrained); not a panel (those are the bottom-of-screen output/terminal area). The editor area, where notebooks open, is where the map should live too.

This means:

This is the right placement for the map. It treats the map as a peer to the notebook, not a subordinate. The operator's normal "split editor" workflow gives them the dual-view layout. They can close the map and just use the notebook; they can have multiple maps open for different workspaces; standard editor behavior.

The implementation is via the Custom Editor API or the Webview Panel API, depending on whether the map is tied to a file or is a free-floating view. For RTS, I'd argue:

The map is a webview panel, not a custom editor. Reasons:

Webview panels open in tabs but aren't tied to files. Standard pattern. ~150 lines of TypeScript for the registration and lifecycle, plus the webview content (HTML/JS for the actual map rendering).

The communication is: webview panel posts messages to extension host (operator clicks), extension host posts messages to webview (state updates from kernel). The extension host is the bridge between kernel state and the map's rendering.

The fork is, essentially, the notebook editor + a webview panel for the map + sidebar contributions + custom MIME renderers. Everything else is cut. The kernel does the work. The fork is a presentation layer.

With the subtractive frame and LangSmith I/O, cell content has a specific shape:

Cell input: prompts to the agent system, in some DSL. Free text is interpreted as a user message to the active agent. `@agent` mentions route. `/commands` invoke RTS operations directly. Markdown cells are documentation.

Cell output: a stream of `application/vnd.rts.run+json` blobs as the kernel processes the cell. Each blob is rendered by your single renderer based on its `run_type`:

Streaming is handled by `application/vnd.rts.run-update+json` blobs that update an in-progress run's state. The renderer maintains an in-progress UI that updates as new updates arrive.

The notebook file format remains `.ipynb` JSON. Cells store their executed outputs, which are arrays of MIME-typed displays. For RTS, those displays are the LangSmith blobs. The file is human-readable and grep-able because it's still JSON.

Worth being explicit: with LangSmith-shaped events and `.ipynb` cells, there are two persistence layers:

The notebook file (`.rts.ipynb`): stores the cell sequence, including executed outputs. This is the durable record of the conversation. Operators see it as a file; they can version-control it, share it, edit it.

The kernel's runtime log: as runs happen, LLMKernel can also persist them to a side file (newline-delimited JSON of all runs, including hierarchical structure). This is the event log for debugging, replay, and audit. It's separate from the notebook file and can be much more detailed.

The notebook file is what operators see; the kernel log is what debugging uses. Both are LangSmith-shaped JSON. Same format, different scopes.

This separation lets you keep the notebook file clean (only the cell-level content) while still having full event-level fidelity in the side log.

Keeping clear which is which:

Cut entirely:

Repurposed:

Added new:

This split makes the fork's identity clear: it's a lean notebook editor for LangSmith-shaped agent transcripts, with LLMKernel as the runtime and a map view as the secondary surface.

Subtractive forks have a meaningfully different maintenance profile than additive ones.

Less to track upstream. Microsoft's vscode-jupyter ships frequent changes to all the things you've cut: Python integration improvements, IPyWidgets fixes, remote-server features, etc. If you've cut those subsystems, you don't have to merge their updates. Only the parts you've kept need to track upstream.

Smaller surface for breaking changes. When VS Code's APIs change, the cut subsystems can't break because they don't exist. Only the kept subsystems can break. The fork is more stable against external churn.

Easier to reason about. The codebase is smaller. New contributors (or future-you) can understand it. The behavior is predictable because there's less of it.

Harder to reverse cuts later. If you ever decide "actually, we want remote Jupyter server support back," you have to either re-merge that subsystem from upstream or reimplement it. This is a real cost; subtractive choices are less reversible than additive ones. But for your case the cuts are unlikely to want reversing.

Forking ethics. A heavily subtracted fork is, in practice, a different product. The naming, the audience, the use case are all different. You're not "an alternative Jupyter extension"; you're "an LLM-orchestration notebook environment." The clearer that distinction, the better for everyone.

A few risks worth naming:

Upstream contribution flow becomes one-directional. You won't be sending pull requests back to vscode-jupyter — your changes are too divergent. This is normal for hard forks but worth acknowledging.

Operators who try to use it as a generic notebook editor will be disappointed. The fork is `.rts.ipynb`-specific, opinionated, and doesn't do the things they'd expect from a Jupyter extension. The naming and documentation need to make this very clear so people don't install it expecting normal Jupyter behavior.

Some cuts will hurt later. You'll occasionally miss something — IPyWidgets for some interactive UI, a debugger for the kernel itself, something else. Re-adding cut subsystems is real work. Plan for surprises.

The .ipynb format choice constrains things. Sticking with `.ipynb` JSON gives you tooling compatibility but also commits you to its quirks (multiline JSON strings as arrays, base64-encoded outputs, etc.). If you ever want a custom format, that's a future migration.

LangSmith format isn't fully standardized. The format is converging but not unified. You're picking a canonical shape; future events from other providers may not exactly fit. You'll need a normalization layer that maps various provider outputs into your canonical schema. Manageable but non-trivial.

Tracking upstream's bug fixes selectively is annoying. When Microsoft fixes a bug in the kept subsystems, you need to identify it and merge it. Subtractive forks need a discipline of "regularly review upstream's recent commits, cherry-pick relevant fixes." A few hours per month, ongoing.

These are the costs of the approach. None is a dealbreaker.

If you're committing to the subtractive fork, here's a specific list of directories and files to delete (or set aside) early:

Delete entire directories:

Delete files:

Keep with modifications:

Add new:

This is rough; the real exercise is going through the codebase with this lens and making informed cuts. Probably 2-3 days of focused work to do the major reductions, with smaller cleanups continuing for another week.

The result: a fork that's maybe 30-40% the size of the original, with a clear structure, focused purpose, and lean maintenance burden.

A focused statement:

The fork is a lean LangSmith-aware notebook editor that runs LLMKernel cells, with a map view tab for spatial agent supervision. Operators write prompts and structured commands in cells; LLMKernel processes them and emits LangSmith-shaped run records; the fork renders those records inline (in cells) and spatially (in the map). The conversation persists as a `.rts.ipynb` file. There's no Python ecosystem integration, no remote kernels, no widgets, no debug-by-line — just the notebook + LLMKernel + map view, refined.

This is small enough to ship. The total fork size after subtraction is maybe 30k-40k lines of TypeScript (down from 100k+), most of which is unchanged from upstream and just inherited. Your additions are a few thousand lines of focused new code. Maintenance is tractable. Upstream tracking is selective.

Subtractive forking is the right approach for what you're doing. The vscode-jupyter codebase has substantial value for the parts you keep (notebook editor, kernel protocol, MIME renderer infrastructure, webview pattern) and substantial drag in the parts you'd cut. The cut-list is large but well-defined.

LangSmith-shaped JSON as the I/O format is a smart choice that aligns with where the LLM-tooling ecosystem is heading, simplifies your renderer to a single dispatching component, and gives you composability with observability tools.

LLMKernel as the sole kernel removes a significant fraction of upstream's complexity (discovery, selection, multi-language support) and makes the fork's purpose clear.

The map view as a tab (webview panel in the editor area) is the right placement — peer to the notebook, splittable, focused.

The total v1 work is roughly: 1 week to do the cuts and fork, 1 week to wire LLMKernel and the LangSmith renderer, 1 week to build the map view tab, 1 week of polish. Four weeks for a focused, lean, working tool with a clear identity.

Decisions worth locking before starting:

Want to dig into the LangSmith schema specifics, the renderer's internal dispatch design, the cut list with specific commit hashes to subtract from, the map-view webview's communication protocol, or the file format details for `.rts.ipynb`?

```json
{
  "id": "uuid",
  "trace_id": "uuid",
  "parent_run_id": "uuid",
  "name": "string",
  "run_type": "llm | tool | chain | retriever | agent | embedding",
  "start_time": "ISO8601",
  "end_time": "ISO8601",
  "inputs": { ... },
  "outputs": { ... },
  "events": [...],
  "tags": [...],
  "metadata": { ... },
  "error": null | { "message": "string", "stack": "string" }
}
```

```javascript
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ VS Code (with your forked LLMKernel-only Jupyter extension)  │
│                                                              │
│  Editor area:                                                │
│    [ notebook tab: .rts.ipynb ]  [ map view tab: webview ]   │
│                                                              │
│  Sidebar (Activity Bar):                                     │
│    [ RTS: zones tree | agents tree | recent activity ]       │
│                                                              │
│  Status bar:                                                 │
│    [ active agent count | pending approvals ]                │
└────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                             │ kernel protocol (ZeroMQ)
                             │ + custom messages for map state
┌────────────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────┐
│ LLMKernel (Python)                                           │
│                                                              │
│  Emits LangSmith-shaped JSON blobs as run events             │
│  Manages agents, zones, MCP integration                      │
│  Persists conversation history (the .ipynb file is the       │
│   transcript; raw runs may also persist as side files)       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

- The notebook editor integration (cells, output, persistence)
- The kernel protocol implementation (ZeroMQ message handling)
- The MIME-type renderer extension API
- The webview infrastructure for custom panels (map view will use this)
- The .ipynb JSON serialization
- Basic notebook diff support (optional)
- The desktop entry points

- application/vnd.rts.run+json — a complete LangSmith-shaped run record (any run_type)
- application/vnd.rts.run-update+json — a streaming update to an in-progress run (partial input/output, status change)
- application/vnd.rts.permission-request+json — operator-action-required event (which is just a run with run_type "tool" and a specific name like "request_approval", but rendered specially)
- application/vnd.rts.map-event+json — map-view-specific events (zone state changes, agent spawns)

- Operator opens .rts.ipynb notebook → notebook tab appears in editor area
- Operator runs "RTS: Open Map" command → map view tab appears in editor area, alongside the notebook tab
- Operator splits the editor area horizontally → notebook on the left, map on the right
- Both tabs are first-class editor surfaces; either can be the active focus

- The map's content is derived from kernel state, not from a file
- One map view per session, not one per file
- The map represents the current world (zones, agents, activity), not a saved document
- Operators don't "save the map"; they save notebooks

- llm runs render as messages/text from the model
- tool runs render as collapsed structured-call cards (with type-specific UI for permission requests, approvals, etc.)
- chain runs render as nested expandable trees
- agent runs render as a status header
- error runs render as red alert blocks

- Python environment management
- Kernel discovery and selection UI
- Remote Jupyter server support
- IPyWidgets bridge
- Interactive Window
- Variable, data, plot viewers
- Notebook debugging
- Web environment
- Conda integration
- Most of the IntelliSense layer

- MIME types (renamed to application/vnd.rts.*, repurposed for LangSmith blobs)
- Notebook editor integration (now only handles .rts.ipynb and uses LLMKernel)
- Webview infrastructure (used for the map view)
- Notebook output rendering pipeline (now consumes LangSmith blobs)
- The .ipynb JSON serialization (notebook = transcript)

- LangSmith-shaped run renderer (one MIME-type renderer with internal dispatch by run_type)
- Map view webview panel
- Sidebar Activity Bar contributions for RTS
- Custom commands for RTS operations
- Kernel-state synchronization between cells and map

- src/interactive-window/ — Interactive Window
- src/webviews/extension-side/variablesView/ — Variable viewer
- src/webviews/extension-side/dataViewer/ — Data viewer
- src/webviews/extension-side/plotting/ — Plot viewer
- src/standalone/intellisense/ — IntelliSense
- src/standalone/notebookExtensibility/ — Some extensibility might survive; review
- src/platform/interpreter/ — Python interpreter discovery
- src/kernels/jupyter/ — Jupyter server support; review carefully
- src/kernels/raw/ — Maybe keep partially (kernel launching), cut discovery

- Anything matching *.web.ts — entire web environment
- src/standalone/api/ — selectively, since you may want to keep simple API exposure
- Various Python-extension-coordination files in src/platform/

- src/notebooks/ — the core notebook editor integration (this is what you're building on)
- src/kernels/ execution/messaging code (handles the kernel protocol)
- src/platform/common/ — shared utilities
- src/platform/api/ — base extension scaffolding

- src/rts/ — your additions: map view, RTS commands, sidebar, custom renderers

1. Subtractive fork strategy. Cut aggressively; keep only what serves the RTS use case.
2. LLMKernel is the only supported kernel. Hardcoded; no kernel selection UI; remove kernel discovery.
3. .rts.ipynb is the file extension. Compatible with .ipynb JSON tooling; signals RTS-specific.
4. LangSmith-shaped JSON as the I/O format. One canonical schema for runs, with run_type dispatch.
5. MIME types repurposed but minimal. Probably one primary MIME type (application/vnd.rts.run+json) with internal dispatch, plus a streaming-update variant. Not a separate MIME per event.
6. Map view as a webview panel in a tab. Editor area, peer to the notebook, splittable. Not a sidebar.
7. Desktop-only. Cut the entire web environment.
8. Cut the Python extension dependency. No coordination with ms-python.python.
9. Cut IPyWidgets, Variable Viewer, Data Viewer, Plot Viewer, Interactive Window. Pure removals.
10. Keep notebook diff support. Useful for git workflows; relatively self-contained.
11. Persistence in two layers: .rts.ipynb for cell-level transcript, side log for full run-level event stream.
12. Sidebar Activity Bar contribution for RTS. Zones, agents, recent activity.
13. Custom commands for RTS operations. Spawn agent, transfer zone, branch, etc.
14. OpenVSX as the publishing target. Sidesteps Microsoft Marketplace concerns.

1. Subtractive cut-list confirmed: kernel discovery, Python extension integration, IPyWidgets, Interactive Window, Variable/Data/Plot viewers, web environment, IntelliSense, debugging, remote-server support all cut.
2. LangSmith-shaped JSON is the canonical run format.
3. One primary MIME type (application/vnd.rts.run+json) with internal dispatch by run_type. Plus a streaming variant.
4. Map view as a webview panel in the editor area. Tab-style; peer to notebook.
5. .rts.ipynb as the file extension. .ipynb-shaped JSON; RTS-specific marker.
6. OpenVSX publishing target. Not Microsoft Marketplace.
7. Desktop-only. Cut the web environment.
8. Cut Python extension dependency entirely. Standalone.
9. Persistence: notebook file for cell-level, side log for run-level.
10. Total scope: 4 weeks for v1.

---

## User

How should we store RTS mode layouts?

---

## Assistant

Good question to surface now, because layout storage is the kind of thing that's easy to get wrong in a way that compounds — and it has architectural implications that touch the file format, the daemon's responsibilities, and how the map view loads.

Let me work through what "RTS mode layout" actually means, what needs persistence, and where each piece of state should live. There are several distinct things bundled together in the question and they want different storage strategies.

Worth decomposing because not all of it is the same kind of state:

Spatial layout — positions of zones, agents, files, services on the 2D map. Where things are drawn. This is the geometry of the visualization.

Camera state — pan, zoom, current focus, selected items. The operator's current view of the map. This is the "where am I looking" state.

Visual configuration — color schemes, symbol styles, edge rendering options, what's visible vs hidden, label sizes, themes. The operator's display preferences for the map.

Filter and query state — which agents are highlighted, which time range is shown, which event types are surfaced. The operator's "what am I looking at" state.

Workspace organization — multiple maps open simultaneously, split-screen arrangement, which notebook is paired with which map. The operator's "how am I working" state.

Saved viewpoints / bookmarks — "show me the auth refactor area," "show me everything from yesterday's incident." Named spatial+temporal+filter snapshots.

Annotations — operator's notes on the map, highlighted regions, drawn arrows, manual labels. Operator-authored content layered on the map.

Replay state — when scrubbing time, the current scrub position. Ephemeral but worth being explicit about.

These are seven distinct kinds of state with different persistence properties. Lumping them all into "layout" is the trap; treating them separately gives clean answers.

Each kind of state belongs in one of four places, and the choice of place is the architectural decision:

Inside the .rts.ipynb file — for state that's part of the conversation's identity. If you share the file, this travels with it.

Alongside the file (sidecar) — for state that's session-specific but tied to the conversation. If you share the file, this can be stripped or shared optionally.

In VS Code workspace state — for state that's per-operator-per-workspace. Survives reloads, doesn't travel with files.

In VS Code global state — for state that's per-operator across all workspaces. The user's preferences.

Here's how I'd map the seven kinds of state to the four storage places:

This gives you a clear story: the conversation file stays clean (it's the transcript), the sidecar file holds spatial/saved-views (sharable when you want), workspace state holds the operator's session ephemeral state, global state holds preferences.

Let me work through each in more depth.

The notebook file is the conversation transcript. It's already standardized as Jupyter-shaped JSON. The temptation is to stuff layout state into the notebook's metadata field, since `.ipynb` allows arbitrary metadata.

Resist this for most layout state. Reasons:

What should go in the notebook file's metadata:

Conversation identity: zone IDs referenced by the conversation, the agent ID(s) that are part of this conversation, the kernel version. Stuff that's intrinsic to "what this conversation is."

Annotations that are conversation content: if the operator annotated a spot on the map as part of the conversation ("this is where the bug was found"), that's part of the conversation. But operator's general doodles or drawings while exploring? Those go in the sidecar.

Saved structural state: "this conversation is paired with these zones" — yes. "I had the camera zoomed to 1.5x at coordinates (340, -120)" — no.

The principle: notebook metadata is for things that, if stripped, would change what the conversation is. Layout doesn't change what the conversation is; it changes how it was viewed.

A sidecar file is the right home for the bulk of layout state. The pattern: notebook is `auth_refactor.rts.ipynb`; sidecar is `auth_refactor.rts.layout.json` (or `.rts.ipynb.layout`, your choice).

What goes in the sidecar:

Spatial positions of all entities in the conversation's scope: zones (their hulls/centroids), agents (their positions), files (their positions in the workspace map), edges and their styling, service nodes, etc. This is the "geometry" of the visualization.

Saved viewpoints: named camera positions with optional filters and time ranges. "Auth area," "yesterday's incident," "before the refactor." Operator-curated.

Persistent annotations: text notes, drawn arrows, highlighted regions. Stuff the operator wants to keep but isn't part of the conversation's content.

Layer visibility: which optional overlays are on (heatmap, edges-by-recency, etc.) for this specific notebook.

Sidecar files are nice because:

A simple convention: the sidecar lives next to the notebook (`<name>.rts.ipynb` and `<name>.rts.layout.json`). When the notebook opens, the fork looks for the sidecar; if absent, generates a default layout. When the notebook is closed, the fork writes the sidecar if layout state changed.

VS Code provides workspace state via its `Memento` API — key-value storage scoped to the workspace. Use this for:

Camera state per notebook: where each open map was looking when last viewed. Restored on reopen. Doesn't travel with the file (different operators want different views).

Filter and query state: which agents the operator was highlighting, what time range they were filtering to, which event types were on. Per-session preference.

Recently-used viewpoints: which saved viewpoints from the sidecar the operator has been using.

Per-notebook UI state: which sidebar sections are expanded, which artifacts are pinned, what panel layout the operator has.

Workspace state is the right place for "operator's session preferences for this workspace." It's persistent (survives VS Code reloads) but local (different operators on the same files have different preferences). VS Code's API handles the storage; you just call `context.workspaceState.get/update`.

This is also the right place for things you might want to invalidate selectively: if your map data model changes in a way that breaks old camera positions, you bump a version key and start fresh. The user's preferences don't survive a schema break, but the conversation does.

VS Code provides global state via `context.globalState`. Use this for:

Visual preferences: theme choice (dark/light/custom), color schemes, default symbol styles, default zoom level. Things the operator decides once and applies everywhere.

Default layouts: a "default visual configuration" that applies to new conversations.

Operator identity: name, default agent preferences, default zone behavior. Per-user metadata that shouldn't be per-file.

Recently opened RTS files: a recent-files list scoped to RTS notebooks specifically.

Global state is small, stable, and operator-scoped. Keep it small; don't put per-conversation things here.

The storage taxonomy is clear; the loading sequence is where the work is. When the operator opens an RTS notebook:

Step 4 is the interesting one because layout might lag behind notebook content. The notebook references zones and agents that might not be in the layout sidecar (newly created since last save). Your fork needs to gracefully extend the layout when new entities appear (auto-place new zones in available space, etc.) and write back the updated layout when the notebook closes.

This isn't hard but it's worth being deliberate. The layout sidecar is partial — it stores known positions, defaults are computed for unknown ones. Don't error when sidecar entries are missing; fill them in.

Subtle but important: layouts will evolve. The fields in your layout schema will change as features are added. Operators will have layout files from old versions.

The standard answer is a `version` field at the top of the layout sidecar:

When the fork reads a layout file, it checks the version. If older, it migrates (best-effort) to current; if newer, it warns and falls back to defaults. Standard schema-evolution pattern.

For v1, lock the schema deliberately. Don't get clever; pick simple structures for spatial positions, viewpoints, annotations. You can always add fields; removing or changing existing fields is hard.

A reasonable v1 layout schema (just for sketching):

Simple, extensible, versioned. v1 ships with this; future versions add fields.

Operators open a new RTS notebook. There's no sidecar. What does the map look like?

You have to compute a default layout from the notebook's content. This is auto-layout, and it's a real algorithm:

For zones: arrange by relationship — zones that share files are placed near each other. Force-directed layout works here. Default zone shape is a hull around its files.

For files: hierarchical layout based on filesystem path. Files in the same directory cluster. Subdirectories nest visually.

For agents: positioned at their current locus of activity. If an agent is touching files in zone X, the agent appears near zone X. If multiple agents are working, spread them so they don't overlap.

For edges: drawn as straight lines initially. Bundle later if density warrants.

For camera: zoom to fit, with all zones visible.

The default layout is computed once when no sidecar exists, then saved as the initial sidecar. From there it's user-modifiable.

The default layout is genuine engineering work. Plan for it as a v1 task in itself — maybe a few days. Force-directed layout libraries exist; you don't have to invent the algorithm.

When the operator drags a zone, moves an agent, or saves a viewpoint, the change has to flow back to storage. The pattern:

The debounce is important — every drag event shouldn't trigger a disk write. Wait for the user to pause (300ms), then write. Or write only on notebook save.

Workspace-state-level changes (camera position) update on a different cadence — VS Code's `Memento.update()` is fine to call on every camera change; it's a fast in-memory update that VS Code persists in the background.

For viewpoints (named saved positions), the operator explicitly creates them. "Save current view as 'auth area'" → fork captures camera + filter state, writes to layout sidecar with the chosen name.

A real concern: as a conversation runs for hours or days, more zones, agents, files appear. The layout has to grow with the conversation without operator intervention.

Auto-place new entities: when a new zone is created mid-conversation, auto-position it (force-directed layout, finding empty space). Operator can drag it later.

Auto-resize zone hulls: as files move into a zone, the hull updates to encompass them. This happens continuously, not just on save.

Don't move existing entities without permission: if the operator has positioned a zone at a specific spot, don't auto-move it on each new entity. Stability is important; layouts should feel stable.

Periodic compaction: as entities are removed (zone closed, agent terminated), positions can be reclaimed. Don't shrink the canvas continuously; offer a "compact layout" command that re-runs auto-layout for active entities.

These are continuous-update behaviors that the layout system has to handle. They're not trivial but they're standard auto-layout territory.

The operator opens two notebooks side by side. Each has its own layout sidecar; the maps are independent.

But they might overlap in content (same zones, same files referenced by multiple notebooks). What happens?

Independent layouts: each notebook's map is its own world. Same zone might appear at different positions in different notebooks. This is honest — different conversations have different focuses.

Cross-notebook reference: a notebook can reference a "global" layout file that's workspace-scoped, not file-scoped, providing common positions for entities that recur. Optional, advanced.

For v1, independent layouts. Cross-notebook coordination is a v2+ feature. Notebook A's map and notebook B's map are independent; if both reference zone X, they each have their own position for it. Less elegant but simpler.

There's an interesting framing worth considering: the layout file is not just position storage but a visualization spec. It says how the conversation should be rendered, including positions, but also potentially:

Treating layout as a richer spec means the same notebook content can be rendered differently for different purposes:

These can be saved as different viewpoints within the layout sidecar, or as separate layout files (`auth_refactor.rts.layout.code-review.json`, etc.).

For v1, I'd hold this in reserve — implement basic spatial layout + saved viewpoints. The richer "layout-as-visualization-spec" comes later as you understand what operators actually want.

The kernel doesn't store layout. The kernel manages live state — zones, agents, events, conversation history. Layout is presentation, not data.

But the kernel emits events that imply layout updates: "zone X created," "agent Y spawned," "file Z added to zone X." The fork's layout system listens for these and updates the layout accordingly (auto-placing new entities, updating zone hulls).

The kernel can have an opinion about initial placement (e.g., "this agent belongs to zone X" → place near zone X), but the layout itself is the fork's responsibility. The kernel exposes facts; the fork renders them.

This separation is clean. Kernel state is the truth about the world; layout is the truth about how the operator chooses to view it.

Pulling it all together:

To keep this tractable, v1 only implements:

Sidecar storage: the JSON file with the v1 schema (spatial positions, named viewpoints, simple annotations). Read on open, write on save.

Workspace state: camera position per notebook. Filter state. Just two or three Memento keys.

Global state: theme preference, default zoom. Maybe two or three keys.

Auto-layout: a simple force-directed implementation for zones; hierarchical for files within zones; agents anchored to zones. Works "well enough."

No annotations in v1: defer the drawing/annotation UI. Layout is positions only.

No multiple layouts per notebook: one sidecar per notebook, one current layout. Multi-layout is v2.

No cross-notebook coordination: each notebook is independent.

That's tractable in maybe 3-4 days of focused work for a v1 layout system. Plus maybe 2-3 days for auto-layout if you don't pull in a library.

Layout storage decomposes into seven distinct kinds of state, mapping to four storage locations: notebook file (minimal), sidecar JSON (most spatial state), VS Code workspace state (session ephemera), VS Code global state (preferences). Each is the natural home for its kind of state.

The notebook stays clean. The sidecar holds the geometry. Workspace state holds session memory. Global state holds preferences. Versioning lets schemas evolve. Auto-layout fills gaps when state is missing.

For v1, lock simple JSON schemas, implement basic auto-layout, write sidecars on save, store camera state in workspace memento. This is a few days of work; it scales to the full layout vision over time without architectural rework.

Decisions worth committing:

Want to dig into the auto-layout algorithm specifically, the JSON schema for the sidecar in more detail, the migration strategy for schema evolution, or how the layout system communicates with the map view's webview?

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "spatial": { ... },
  "viewpoints": { ... },
  "annotations": [ ... ]
}
```

```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "spatial": {
    "zones": {
      "zone_id_1": {
        "type": "polygon",
        "points": [[x1, y1], [x2, y2], ...],
        "color": "#abc",
        "label_offset": [dx, dy]
      },
      "zone_id_2": { ... }
    },
    "agents": {
      "agent_id_1": { "x": 100, "y": 200, "anchor": "zone_id_1" }
    },
    "files": {
      "/path/to/file.py": { "x": 50, "y": 60 }
    },
    "edges": {
      "rendering": "default" | "bundled" | "hidden"
    }
  },
  "viewpoints": {
    "viewpoint_name": {
      "camera": { "x": 0, "y": 0, "zoom": 1.0 },
      "filters": { "agents": ["agent_id_1"], "time_range": [...] },
      "description": "..."
    }
  },
  "annotations": [
    {
      "id": "uuid",
      "type": "note" | "arrow" | "region",
      "position": [x, y],
      "content": "...",
      "created_at": "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

- The notebook file is small and clean; it shouldn't bloat with display state
- Sharing the notebook (git, collaborator) shouldn't force them to inherit your camera position
- Layout-as-metadata is a leak — what's "the conversation" gets entangled with "how I was viewing it"
- Notebook diffs become noisy if camera state changes every save

- They're optional. The notebook works without them; the operator just gets a default layout.
- They're shareable selectively. Send a colleague the notebook + sidecar; they get your layout. Send just the notebook; they start fresh.
- They're git-friendly. Commit them or don't, your choice. They diff well as JSON.
- They don't bloat the notebook.
- They're independently editable. Layout-management tools can update them without touching the conversation.

1. Notebook editor opens the .rts.ipynb file — VS Code's standard pipeline, with your fork as the editor for this file type.
2. Kernel starts up — LLMKernel launches, connects, reports ready.
3. Map view opens (if the operator triggers it) — webview panel in a tab.
4. Layout sidecar is loaded — your fork reads <name>.rts.layout.json if present; populates the map view's spatial state.
5. Workspace state is consulted — last camera position, filter state restored.
6. Global state applied — visual preferences, theme.
7. Live state subscribed — kernel emits updates; map view receives them.

1. Operator interaction in the map view
2. Webview message to the extension host: "user moved zone X to (a, b)"
3. Extension updates in-memory layout state
4. On save (or after debounce): write layout sidecar to disk

- Which event types to show (filter)
- How to bundle edges (rendering hint)
- What labels to display (display config)
- What time range is the "default view" for this conversation
- Color encoding choices (categorical vs activity-based)

- "Code reviewer's view": time range filtered to recent changes, agents hidden, only zones and edits shown
- "Debug view": all events shown, time scrubbing enabled
- "Presentation view": clean labels, no internal events, focused camera

1. Notebook file (.rts.ipynb): minimal layout metadata — zone references, agent IDs in the conversation. No camera, no positions, no annotations.
2. Sidecar file (<name>.rts.layout.json): spatial positions, saved viewpoints, persistent annotations. Optional; auto-generated default if absent. Versioned schema.
3. VS Code workspace state: per-notebook session state — camera position, current filters, pinned artifacts, panel arrangement.
4. VS Code global state: visual preferences, themes, default styles, recently opened files.
5. In-memory only: replay/scrub position, hover states, transient selection.
6. Auto-layout for new entities: force-directed for zones, hierarchical for files, anchored for agents. Stable on existing positions.
7. Sidecar updates on save and on debounce: not on every drag.
8. Workspace state updates frequently: camera position is fine to update on each pan.
9. Versioned schemas with migration paths: especially for the sidecar.
10. Independent layouts per notebook in v1: cross-notebook coordination deferred.
11. Layout as visualization spec is a future direction: v1 just does positions and viewpoints.
12. Default layout is auto-computed when no sidecar exists; saved as initial sidecar.

1. Sidecar JSON file for spatial state, named viewpoints, annotations.
2. Simple v1 schema, versioned, with migration support.
3. VS Code workspace state for camera/filter/session.
4. VS Code global state for preferences.
5. Auto-layout for new entities with stability for existing ones.
6. Layout writes debounced on save; workspace-state updates frequent.
7. No annotations or multi-layout in v1; defer to v2.
8. Independent layouts per notebook in v1; cross-notebook coordination deferred.
9. Layout-as-visualization-spec is a future direction; v1 just does positions and saves.

---

## User

Store rts layout through a tree. Store agent state and config as a graph, store chat flow as Jason

---

## Assistant

Now the storage architecture has a clean shape — three different data structures for three genuinely different kinds of state, each chosen because it matches the structure of what it represents. Let me work through why this decomposition is right, what each structure looks like concretely, and where the integration points sit.

This is a real architectural insight worth naming. Most systems would default to "one storage scheme, applied uniformly" — store everything as JSON, or store everything as a graph, or stuff everything into a database. That fails because different kinds of state have different shapes, and forcing them into one structure either bloats the structure or distorts the data.

Your decomposition matches each data type to its natural shape:

Layout is hierarchical. Zones contain files. Files belong to zones. Workspaces contain zones. The map view itself is a composition of regions and sub-regions. This is a tree — and operations on layout (adding a file to a zone, moving a zone within a workspace, nesting groups) are tree operations. Forcing a tree into JSON or a graph distorts it; trees have native operations (insertion, traversal, subtree manipulation) that JSON and graphs don't express cleanly.

Agent state and config is a graph. Agents have relationships: parent-child (one agent spawned another), peer (two agents collaborating), client-server (one consumes another's output), supervisor (one approves another's actions). Configuration also forms a graph: which tools an agent has, which MCP servers it connects to, which zones it's bound to, which other agents it can communicate with. These relationships have cycles (agent A might depend on B which depends on A). Graphs handle this; trees and JSON don't.

Chat flow is JSON. Conversations are sequences of structured events with optional nesting. LangSmith blobs are already JSON. Streaming protocols are JSON. Persistence as JSON is canonical. Operations on chat (append, query, replay, branch) are sequence/tree-of-records operations that JSON handles natively.

Each structure is the right fit. The architecture is honest about the data's actual shape rather than imposing uniformity for storage convenience.

Let me work through what the layout tree actually looks like, because the choice has implications for how things are addressed and updated.

The natural hierarchy:

This is a tree because:

Concretely, the layout tree as JSON-serializable structure (because storing trees in JSON is fine; the structure is the tree even if the serialization format is JSON):

The format is JSON, but the structure is a tree. Operations on it are tree operations: traverse, find by path, replace subtree, insert child, remove subtree.

Tree-shaped operations the layout system supports:

Tree libraries handle this well. You don't need a database; you need a tree library and JSON persistence.

The layout tree lives in the sidecar file — `.rts.layout.json` next to the notebook, as discussed last turn. The format is what we just sketched: hierarchical JSON with explicit type tags and children arrays.

Workspace state (camera position, current filter) is not in the tree. It's session-ephemeral, in VS Code's workspace memento. The tree captures structure; the memento captures view state.

Updates to the tree happen on debounce (drag a zone → wait 300ms → write tree to disk) or on explicit save. Writes are atomic — write to a temp file, rename. Standard pattern.

When the notebook opens, the fork loads the tree, populates the map view's spatial state. When new entities appear (kernel notifies of new zones), the tree is extended (auto-layout finds a position, adds a node). When entities are removed, the tree is pruned (or the node is marked inactive — debatable design choice, lean toward pruning for cleanliness).

Agents and their configuration are the graph layer. This is where the relationships matter more than hierarchy, where cycles can exist, and where you want graph-native operations.

What's a node, what's an edge:

Nodes in the graph:

Edges in the graph:

This is a multi-typed, multi-relation graph. Some edges have direction (parent_of); some are symmetric (collaborates_with); some have properties (a "has_tool" edge might have permissions metadata).

The right storage for this is some kind of graph store. Options:

Option A: In-memory graph with JSON serialization. Use a library like `graphology` (TypeScript) or `networkx` (Python) for in-memory operations; serialize to JSON for persistence. This is the simplest viable path. A typed graph with named edges, stored as JSON, queried via library APIs.

Option B: Embedded graph database. Something like `kuzu`, `memgraph` embedded mode, or LMDB-backed graph storage. More features (Cypher-like queries, indexing, ACID), more setup cost.

Option C: Triple store. RDF-style storage where everything is `(subject, predicate, object)` triples. Very flexible; less ergonomic for typed access.

For v1, Option A is right. In-memory graph with JSON persistence. Libraries are mature, no database to manage, queries are just function calls. When you need more (large graphs, complex queries), upgrade to Option B.

Concretely, the graph might look like:

Same JSON serialization as the layout tree, but the structure is a graph. Operations on it are graph operations:

A graph library handles all of these efficiently. You don't need a real database for v1.

This is more interesting than the layout tree, because agent state is runtime state, not persistent state. Agents are alive or dead; their status changes constantly; new edges form as they collaborate.

Two storage layers:

The kernel's in-memory graph: the live, current state. Nodes and edges as agents spawn, configurations change, relationships form. The kernel maintains this; the fork queries it.

Persistent graph snapshots: periodically (or on shutdown), the kernel serializes its graph to a side file (`.rts.agents.json`). This survives kernel restarts. On restart, the kernel restores from the snapshot.

The graph isn't in the notebook file (it's not part of the conversation's identity — agents may be reused across notebooks). It's not in the layout sidecar (different concern). It's its own persistence layer, kernel-owned.

The fork queries the kernel's graph for rendering: "show me all agents in zone X" → kernel walks the graph → returns a list. The fork renders. When the operator drags an agent to a different zone, that's a graph mutation: kernel updates the edge ("in_zone" pointed from agent to old_zone is replaced with one pointing to new_zone).

This separation is clean: the kernel owns runtime agent state; the fork displays it. The graph is a runtime data structure that happens to persist for resilience.

The third leg. Conversations are sequences of LangSmith-shaped run records. JSON is the natural format because:

The structure is fundamentally a sequence with optional tree-shaped nesting (a run can have child runs). JSON handles this natively.

What's stored:

Storage layers for chat flow:

Inside the .rts.ipynb file: cell-level runs. Each cell that's been executed has its outputs as MIME-typed displays. This is what makes the notebook the durable conversation transcript.

A side log (event log): append-only stream of every run, including ones that don't end up in cells (intermediate runs, child runs the cell renderer collapses, etc.). Format is JSONL. This is the kernel's full event history.

The notebook file is curated; the event log is comprehensive. They serve different purposes — the notebook for sharing/reading, the event log for debugging/replay.

Standard event-log structure. Queryable with `jq`. Replayable by reading sequentially. Branchable by reading up to a point and forking.

The interesting part. These three storage systems aren't independent — they reference each other.

Layout tree → agent graph: when the layout renders an agent symbol on the map, it asks the agent graph "what's this agent's status, what zone is it in, what tools does it have?" The graph provides the rendering metadata.

Layout tree → chat flow: when the layout renders a file's "recent activity" indicator, it queries the chat flow "what runs touched this file recently?" The chat flow provides the activity events.

Agent graph → chat flow: when an agent is rendered with its current activity, the graph references the chat flow for the agent's most recent runs.

Chat flow → agent graph: each run record references the agent that produced it. The chat flow is annotated with agent IDs that resolve to nodes in the graph.

Chat flow → layout tree: when a tool call references a file, the renderer wants to show the file on the map — looking up the file's position in the layout tree.

These cross-references mean the three structures are loosely coupled but not independent. They share IDs (agents have stable IDs across all three; files have stable paths; zones have stable IDs). Each structure is the source of truth for its own concern; the others reference it by ID.

The three-structure design works because each concern has a natural data shape, and IDs are the glue that holds them together. No single structure tries to capture everything; each does its part.

Updating the storage taxonomy from last turn with this three-structure decomposition:

The `.rts/` directory becomes a workspace-scoped store for kernel persistence. It's per-workspace (different RTS workspaces have their own agent graphs and event logs). It's not shared across workspaces (each is self-contained). It's gitignored by default but can be checked in if the operator wants version control.

The notebook file and its layout sidecar are file-scoped. Operators share them by sending the files; layout travels with the notebook (optionally) but agent graph and event log are workspace-scoped.

This separation is clean: per-file content (notebook, layout sidecar) for things specific to one conversation; per-workspace state (`.rts/` directory) for things spanning multiple conversations in the same workspace; per-operator state (VS Code Mementos) for preferences and session ephemera.

Each structure has its own version field and migration path. They don't have to evolve in lockstep.

Independent versioning means you can evolve schemas independently. Add a field to the layout tree without touching the agent graph. Standard pattern for federated schemas.

For v1, lock simple schemas. v1 layout tree has zones, files, agents, viewpoints. v1 agent graph has nodes (agents, zones, tools, operator) and edges (spawned, in_zone, has_tool, supervises). v1 chat flow uses LangSmith schema v1 (whatever the canonical version is at v1 ship date).

Future versions add fields. Migration code lives in the kernel and the fork; it reads old versions and converts to current.

A few operations touch all three:

Spawning an agent:

All three structures updated by one operation.

Transferring a file between zones:

Three structures, one operation.

Branching a conversation:

The most complex operation. Three structures plus filesystem operations.

These multi-structure operations need transactional consideration: if step 3 fails after steps 1-2 succeed, the structures end up inconsistent. For v1, you can rely on best-effort with operator-visible errors (something failed, please retry). For v2, real transactions across structures matter.

The other interesting case: queries that span structures.

"Show me every agent that touched auth.py":

"Show me the full state at time T":

Cross-structure queries are real but tractable. The kernel can serve these by orchestrating across its data sources.

LLMKernel's job expands. It's now the source of truth for:

The fork's job:

The kernel does data work; the fork does presentation work. The two communicate via the kernel protocol (with custom RTS-specific messages for graph queries and updates) and via shared filesystem (the kernel writes `.rts/agents.json`, the fork reads it; the fork writes the layout sidecar, the kernel reads it for context).

To make this concrete, here are starter schemas for each structure:

Layout tree v1:

Agent graph v1:

Chat flow v1 (LangSmith-shaped JSONL):

Three schemas, three storage locations, one coordinated system.

To keep this practical:

Layout tree: no library needed. JSON is fine. Tree operations are 50 lines of TypeScript or Python (find, walk, insert, remove, move).

Agent graph: `graphology` (TypeScript) for the fork's in-memory queries; `networkx` (Python) in the kernel. Both serialize to JSON.

Chat flow: no library needed. JSONL is fine. Append, read, parse.

Optional: a lightweight JSON schema validator for each structure (`ajv` in TypeScript, `jsonschema` in Python). Validates loaded files; catches schema drift early.

No databases. No SQL. No graph databases. Just three data structures with JSON persistence and library support for in-memory operations.

Pulling together:

Stepping back: this three-structure decomposition gives you a system where each kind of state has the right representation. Not forcing a tree into a graph, not forcing a graph into a tree, not flattening either into JSON-as-bag-of-fields.

The result:

This is the kind of decomposition that pays compound interest. Each new feature you build will fit in one of these structures (with maybe some IDs in the others). Schema evolution is local. Bugs are contained. Refactoring is bounded.

Concrete v1 work for the storage layer:

Layout tree (~2-3 days):

Agent graph (~2-3 days):

Chat flow (~1-2 days):

Cross-structure coordination (~2-3 days):

Total v1 storage layer: ~1.5 weeks of focused work, parallelizable between fork (layout tree) and kernel (graph + chat flow).

Three structures, three storage choices, three sets of operations. Each fits the data's natural shape. JSON is the persistence format for all three (so tooling is uniform), but the in-memory representations are what matters: tree for hierarchy, graph for relationships, sequence for events.

The decomposition is right because it respects what the data actually is. A layout is a tree. Agent state is a graph. Chat flow is a sequence. Forcing any of them into the wrong shape would create friction; matching them to their right shape produces a clean system.

For v1, no databases. Just JSON files with in-memory libraries. Total ~1.5 weeks for the storage layer. Scales naturally to v2+ with more sophisticated needs (real graph database, real time-travel for layouts, etc.) without architectural rework.

```javascript
Workspace (root)
├── Visual configuration (theme, defaults, render settings)
├── Map regions (top-level layout containers)
│   ├── Zone "refactor"
│   │   ├── Position (centroid, hull, color)
│   │   ├── Files (children of the zone)
│   │   │   ├── auth.py (position relative to zone)
│   │   │   ├── tokens.rs (position)
│   │   │   └── ...
│   │   ├── Agents anchored to this zone
│   │   └── Sub-zones (if you want nesting)
│   ├── Zone "tests"
│   │   └── ...
│   └── Commons (unclaimed territory)
│       └── Files not yet in any zone
├── Saved viewpoints (named camera + filter states)
│   ├── "auth area"
│   ├── "yesterday's incident"
│   └── ...
└── Annotations (operator-authored content)
    ├── "this is where the bug was found" at (x, y)
    └── ...
```
