## Assistant

Yes — and "paper telephone" is a precise frame for what you're building, sharper than I'd articulated. Let me work through what it actually means, because the metaphor is doing real work and clarifies several things that were fuzzy.

The reference is to the children's game (also called Telephone, Chinese Whispers, or in some cultures "broken telephone") where a message passes through a chain of intermediaries, each one writing or saying it down before passing on, and you compare what came out the end to what went in. The relevant property: every link in the chain has the message in a stable, inspectable form. There's a paper trail. Each intermediary can read, modify, or refuse to pass on. The chain is observable end-to-end.

For your architecture, this maps onto:

Every arrow is bidirectional. Every node holds the message in a structured form (LangSmith blobs) that can be inspected, modified, or blocked before passing on. The kernel as PTY means it's not just a router — it's an active intermediary that mediates the entire interaction, with LiteLLM giving it the ability to dispatch to any model provider.

The "paper" is the LangSmith-shaped JSON record. Every link writes its part of the story onto this paper before passing it along. The trail is preserved and replayable.

This is a powerful framing because it makes the architecture's central property — mediated, observable, controllable bidirectional flow — concrete and namable. "Paper telephone" is what you say when someone asks what the system does.

A PTY (pseudoterminal) in Unix is the canonical example of a transparent intermediary: it sits between two processes pretending to be a terminal to each, while observing and potentially transforming everything that flows through. Programs talking through a PTY usually don't know they're being mediated.

Your LLMKernel-as-PTY does the analogous thing for LLM/agent interactions:

LiteLLM is the key piece for the model side. It abstracts over provider APIs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, local Ollama, etc.) so the kernel can intercept calls in a unified format regardless of which model is being used. The kernel becomes a single interception point for all LLM interactions.

This is a strong architectural commitment with real consequences:

Every LLM call passes through LiteLLM in the kernel. Not just observable — required. Agents don't talk directly to provider APIs; they talk to the kernel, which talks to providers via LiteLLM.

Every tool call passes through MCP, hosted by the kernel. Not just observable — required. Agents don't have direct filesystem access or shell access; they call MCP tools that the kernel proxies.

The kernel is the only path to anything outside. This is the PTY property. The kernel mediates the entire surface between the agent and reality.

Some implications worth being explicit about:

This is the "take over the context" architecture from earlier turns, but expressed more honestly: the kernel doesn't just try to take over context, it literally sits in the path of every interaction. There's no way around it because it is the path.

With this PTY framing, "bidirectional control" has specific meaning at every layer.

Operator → Agent direction (commands flowing out):

The operator never talks directly to the agent. The path goes operator → extension → kernel → agent, with the kernel as the active intermediary at the critical hop.

Agent → Operator direction (calls flowing in):

The agent never talks directly to the operator. The path goes agent → kernel → extension → operator → extension → kernel → agent.

Agent → Reality direction (LLM and tool calls flowing through):

The agent never talks directly to model providers or to filesystems/shells. The kernel is the single point of egress and ingress.

This is full mediation. Every arrow in the system has the kernel as a hop. Every interaction has a paper trail.

The reason this is implementable rather than theoretical: each component speaks well-known protocols at each interface.

Each interface is a known protocol. The novelty is in combining them with the kernel as the hub. No protocol invention required; just careful integration.

LiteLLM specifically deserves attention here. Its role is critical: it presents a unified interface to the kernel for "LLM calls" regardless of which provider the agent wants. Agents configured for Claude, GPT-4, Gemini, local Ollama, or anything LiteLLM supports all flow through the same kernel interception point. The kernel doesn't have to know which provider; it just sees "an LLM call" with a standard shape.

Your phrasing "we don't really care what the model context does as long as we can control its functionality" is sharp and worth dwelling on. With paper telephone architecture, "control its functionality" decomposes specifically:

Control over what the agent can do: the kernel's MCP server is the only tool surface. What's exposed, the agent can do; what's not, the agent cannot. By choosing which tools to host or proxy, you control the agent's capabilities precisely.

Control over what the agent sees: LiteLLM intercepts every model call. The kernel can modify prompts, inject context, redact sensitive info, append instructions. The agent's "view of the world" is shaped at this layer.

Control over what the agent's outputs reach: every tool call is mediated. The kernel can refuse, modify, queue, or redirect. Outputs only happen through the kernel.

Control over the agent's lifecycle: kernel spawns agents, can pause/resume them, can kill them, can replay them.

Control over time: the entire interaction is a LangSmith log. Replay is real. You can rerun an interaction with different inputs at any point.

This is genuinely strong control without requiring you to understand or modify the agent's reasoning. The agent thinks whatever it thinks; you control the I/O surface around it. That's the framework's bet, and it's well-founded.

A few things still need explicit answers:

Streaming protocol details. With LiteLLM, streaming responses from models are a real thing. How do streaming LLM responses translate into streaming run records that the extension renders incrementally? simdjson handles parse but the protocol for incremental updates needs to be defined. ~Half day of design work.

Error propagation. When an LLM call fails (rate limit, network error, model error), how does it surface? Through the LangSmith run record's `error` field, presumably. But how does it propagate to the operator's view? Cell output with error rendering? Notification? Both? ~Half day.

Multi-agent coordination in v1. You can spawn multiple agents but they're independent. Does the kernel manage cross-agent state? For v1, probably no — each agent is its own conversation. But this should be explicit. ~Half day.

Tool authorization model. The kernel proxies filesystem tools. Does every filesystem call require operator approval? That'd be obnoxious. Some filtered subset (writes outside working dir, dangerous commands)? ~Half day.

Cost and rate limit handling. LiteLLM can apply per-provider rate limits and cost tracking. v1 should at least surface these. ~Half day.

These are the last small design questions. Each is a half-day of writing-the-decision-down. Roughly 2.5 days of design work to fully resolve.

The "control" you have isn't about understanding the model's reasoning — it's about controlling the I/O surface so completely that the model's reasoning is irrelevant for your purposes.

```javascript
Operator ⇄ Extension ⇄ LLMKernel (PTY + LiteLLM) ⇄ Agent ⇄ Model
```

- From the agent's perspective: it's making an LLM call (via LiteLLM, which is itself an abstraction over many providers) or a tool call (via MCP). It doesn't know the kernel is observing or potentially modifying.
- From the operator's perspective: they see structured events flowing into the extension. They don't know (and don't need to know) which model provider, which tool, what the raw protocol looks like.
- From the kernel's perspective: it sees everything. It can log, modify, block, redirect, replay, or transform any interaction.

- Agents are configured to use the kernel's endpoints (LiteLLM API endpoint and MCP server) and only those. No direct API keys to providers. No direct shell access.
- The kernel becomes the trust boundary. What it allows, agents can do; what it blocks, they cannot.
- The kernel becomes the audit boundary. Everything is logged because everything flows through it.
- The kernel becomes the modification point. Want to inject context, redirect to a cheaper model, replay from a checkpoint — all happen in the kernel.

1. Operator types in a cell or interacts with a webview UI element (clicks approve, drags a zone)
2. Extension translates this into a structured event (MCP-shaped tool call to the kernel)
3. Extension sends the event to the kernel via the kernel protocol (or a direct MCP channel)
4. Kernel receives, validates, may modify, logs as a LangSmith blob
5. Kernel forwards to the agent — either as a system message injected into the agent's context, or as a tool result that the agent's MCP client receives, or as a new prompt
6. Agent receives, processes, takes action

1. Agent makes an MCP tool call (e.g., request_approval(action, rationale, preview))
2. Agent's MCP client sends it to the kernel's MCP server
3. Kernel receives, logs as LangSmith blob, may modify
4. Kernel decides: does this need operator response, or can the kernel handle it autonomously?
5. If operator needed: kernel forwards to extension as a structured event (as cell output, or as a dedicated UI surface)
6. Extension renders the event, prompts operator for response
7. Operator responds (clicks button, types reply, etc.)
8. Extension sends response back to kernel
9. Kernel forwards as the tool result back to the agent's MCP client
10. Agent continues processing

1. Agent decides to make an LLM call or tool call
2. For LLM calls: agent sends via LiteLLM-compatible API to the kernel's LiteLLM endpoint
3. For tool calls: agent calls MCP tools the kernel hosts (or proxies)
4. Kernel receives, logs as LangSmith blob, may modify (cache, redirect, transform)
5. Kernel forwards to the actual destination (the model provider via LiteLLM, or the actual tool implementation)
6. Result returns to kernel
7. Kernel logs the result, may modify
8. Kernel forwards back to the agent

- Agent ↔ LLM provider: agents use OpenAI-compatible API (standard) or provider-specific SDKs (Anthropic, etc.). LiteLLM proxies all of these. Configure the agent to point its API base at your kernel's LiteLLM endpoint, and the kernel intercepts every call.
- Agent ↔ Tools: agents use MCP (increasingly standard). Configure the agent's MCP client to connect to the kernel's MCP server, and the kernel intercepts every tool call.
- Kernel ↔ Extension: Jupyter messaging protocol with custom message types. Both sides know this protocol.
- Extension ↔ Operator: VS Code APIs (notebook editor, webviews, commands). Standard.

- Cache LLM responses across providers
- Redirect calls between providers (e.g., "if the agent asked for Claude but I want to test with GPT-4, redirect")
- Add tracing/logging in a uniform way
- Apply rate limits or cost controls uniformly
- Inject system prompts or modify messages before they reach the actual provider

- show_diff(file_a, file_b) — opens VS Code's native diff editor
- navigate_to(file, line) — moves cursor in the editor
- display_widget(widget_spec) — renders a custom widget in a cell
- prompt_operator(prompt_spec) — surfaces a UI element waiting for operator input, returns the response
- notify(message, urgency) — VS Code notification
- open_panel(panel_id) — opens or focuses a specific panel
- update_status(state) — updates status bar
- highlight(file, region) — highlights code in the editor

- execute_cell(cell_id, content) — process a cell's input
- pause_agent(agent_id) — suspend an agent
- resume_agent(agent_id) — resume
- provide_response(call_id, response) — answer an outstanding prompt_operator call (wait, this is on the extension side; never mind)

- Extension as MCP client of the kernel: extension calls kernel tools when operator does things ("operator just clicked approve" → call provide_response on kernel)
- Extension as MCP server to the kernel: extension exposes tools the kernel can call (show_diff, prompt_operator, etc.)
- Kernel as MCP server to the extension and agents: hosts the operator-interaction tools and the proxied filesystem/shell tools
- Kernel as MCP client of the extension (and possibly other servers): can call the extension's UI tools, can call third-party MCP servers for additional capabilities

1. Operator types in a cell. Extension creates a run record: { type: "operator_input", id: ..., trace_id: ..., inputs: { content: "..." } }. Sends to kernel.
2. Kernel receives, creates child run: { type: "agent_dispatch", parent_run_id: ..., inputs: { agent_id: ..., message: "..." } }. Forwards to agent.
3. Agent processes, makes LLM call. Kernel intercepts, creates child run: { type: "llm", parent_run_id: ..., inputs: { messages: [...], model: "..." } }. Forwards to LiteLLM, gets response, fills in outputs: { response: "..." }, completes the run.
4. Agent decides to call a tool (e.g., request_approval). Kernel creates child run: { type: "tool", name: "request_approval", parent_run_id: ..., inputs: { ... } }. This tool is an operator-interaction tool, so kernel forwards to extension.
5. Extension renders the request. Operator clicks approve. Extension creates run: { type: "operator_response", parent_run_id: ..., outputs: { approved: true } }. Sends to kernel.
6. Kernel completes the tool call run with outputs: { approved: true }. Returns to agent.
7. Agent continues, eventually completing the cell. Kernel completes all parent runs.

- Hosts operator-interaction tools (the chat-over-MCP protocol)
- Proxies system tools (filesystem, shell, search) that agents need
- Validates incoming MCP calls
- Routes calls to the right handlers
- Logs every call as a LangSmith run

- Hosts an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint
- Receives LLM calls from agents
- Forwards to actual providers via LiteLLM
- Logs every call as a LangSmith run
- Optionally caches, redirects, modifies

- Receives operator events from extension via Jupyter messaging
- Sends UI updates to extension via Jupyter messaging
- Hosts MCP-style tools that extension can invoke
- Calls extension's MCP-style tools as needed

- Spawns agent subprocesses
- Configures them (system prompt, MCP config, working directory)
- Monitors agent lifecycle
- Captures stdout/stderr (the agent's text output, suppressed from operator view)
- Restarts agents as needed

- Maintains in-memory state (running agents, current zone, conversation history)
- Reads .llmnb file on startup, reconstructs state
- Writes .llmnb file on save/close, persists state
- Maintains the LangSmith run log (in memory + periodic flush to file)

- Captures runs in a way that allows replay
- Can simulate a session from a captured log
- Used for testing and debugging

1. Operator opens an .llmnb file in VS Code with the forked extension
2. LLMKernel starts, hosts MCP server, LiteLLM endpoint, kernel protocol server
3. Operator types in a cell: "spawn an agent that fixes the bug in tokens.rs"
4. Extension sends to kernel; kernel creates run record; kernel spawns Claude Code with config pointing at kernel's MCP server and LiteLLM endpoint
5. Claude Code starts, makes an LLM call to plan its work; kernel intercepts via LiteLLM, logs, forwards to Anthropic, gets response, returns to agent
6. Agent reads tokens.rs via MCP filesystem tool; kernel proxies, logs
7. Agent decides to make a change; calls request_approval MCP tool with proposed diff
8. Kernel receives, forwards to extension; extension renders an approval block in cell output with diff preview button
9. Operator clicks "preview"; extension calls VS Code's diff editor; operator reviews
10. Operator clicks "approve"; extension sends approval back to kernel; kernel returns approval as tool result to agent
11. Agent applies the change via MCP filesystem tool; kernel proxies, logs
12. Agent reports completion via report_completion MCP tool; kernel forwards to extension; extension renders summary
13. Cell finishes; entire interaction is a tree of LangSmith runs in the .llmnb file
14. Operator commits the file via git
15. Later, operator wants to retry: creates a new branch, opens the file, kernel restarts agents from the file's state

1. Kernel's MCP server with operator-interaction tools (~3 days). The native tools: request_approval, ask_operator, report_progress, report_completion, report_problem, etc. Implemented in Python in LLMKernel.
2. Kernel's LiteLLM endpoint (~2 days). Already partially there in LLMKernel; needs to be exposed as an HTTP endpoint that agents can configure as their API base. Logging via LangSmith run records.
3. Kernel's MCP proxies for filesystem/shell tools (~3 days). Wrap a few essential tools (read_file, write_file, run_command) with kernel-mediated versions that log and can be approved/denied.
4. Agent process management (~2 days). Spawn agents (Claude Code in particular for v1) with config pointing at kernel's endpoints. Capture output. Lifecycle management.
5. Extension's notebook controller integration (~3 days). Wire the forked extension to use LLMKernel; cell input goes to kernel; cell output renders kernel responses.
6. Custom MIME renderers in extension (~5 days, parallel). Five renderers: status, tool call, approval (with diff button), plan, completion. Each is a webview component.
7. Bidirectional kernel-extension messaging (~3 days). Custom Jupyter message types for RTS-specific events. Extension exposes its UI tools (show_diff, etc.) to the kernel. Kernel forwards operator-interaction tool calls to extension.
8. .llmnb file format with LangSmith log embedding (~3 days). Read/write the file structure. Restore state on open. Write state on save.
9. Git integration for branching (~1 day, mostly documentation). Define the workflow; document for operators; test the branch-switch behavior.

1. Failure handling and kernel/notebook split (~3 days)
2. Replay harness (~3 days)
3. Doc-driven contract tests (~3 days)
4. Markov simulation tests (~3 days, can be parallelized with implementation)
5. Schema versioning framework (~2 days)

1. Magic command extensions (~2 days)
2. Setup documentation (~2 days)
3. Error messages and operator-facing UX (~3 days)
4. Real testing with at least one real workflow (~3 days)

1. Paper telephone is the architectural metaphor. Every interaction is mediated, observable, recordable, replayable.
2. LLMKernel is the PTY — sits in the path of every agent interaction with the world.
3. LiteLLM is the LLM intercept point. All model calls go through it. Kernel can modify, redirect, cache, log.
4. MCP server is the tool intercept point. All tool calls go through it. Native operator tools + proxied system tools.
5. Extension is also MCP-shaped. Both client and server. Bidirectional with the kernel.
6. LangSmith blobs are the universal data format. Every interaction is a run. Trees of runs capture call hierarchy.
7. Kernel is the single trust/audit/modification boundary. No bypass paths in v1.
8. Agents configured to use kernel endpoints exclusively. API base = kernel's LiteLLM endpoint. MCP server = kernel's MCP. No direct provider keys.
9. .llmnb file is the durable state. Kernel reads on startup, writes on save. Git is the version control layer.
10. v1 demonstration target: spawn → agent works → tool calls → operator approves → agent completes → cell records full LangSmith tree → file saves → reopen restores state. End-to-end working paper telephone.

1. Paper telephone as the architectural metaphor and demo target.
2. LLMKernel as PTY with LiteLLM (LLM side) + MCP (tool side) as the dual mediation surfaces.
3. Bidirectional MCP between kernel and extension, plus MCP between kernel and agents, plus LiteLLM between kernel and providers.
4. LangSmith run records as the universal data format, persisted in .llmnb files, version-controlled with git.
5. v1 demonstration target: spawn agent → mediated interaction → operator approval → completion → file save → branch → resume. End-to-end paper telephone, working.
6. Resolve the last small ambiguities (streaming, errors, multi-agent, authorization, rate limits) in the pre-week-1 design sprint.
7. Implementation timeline: 6-10 weeks calendar with Claude Code, parallelizable across tracks.

## User

Streaming, can't we use langsmith native streaming?

---

## Assistant

Good question — let me actually check what LangSmith's native streaming looks like before reasoning about it, because I shouldn't assume.

Good — I have a clearer picture now. Let me give you the honest answer because the framing of "LangSmith native streaming" needs unpacking.

LangSmith's tracing model isn't really a streaming protocol in the sense you might be thinking of. It's a two-phase run model: create the run with `post_run` (sending start_time, name, run_type, inputs, parent_run_id), then end the run with `patch_run` later (sending outputs and end_time) AI SDK.

The pattern is POST-then-PATCH. You create a run record with its inputs at the start, do whatever work happens, then patch the same run with outputs when complete. This isn't streaming the content — it's just two updates to the same run record over time: "started" and "completed."

For finer-grained progress, LangSmith has events within runs: "events can be used to log additional minor information about what occurred during a run (such as streaming and retry events), when that information doesn't merit an entire child run and is not the final output of the run." Langchain So token-by-token streaming, retries, intermediate signals — these are events appended to a run, not separate child runs.

There's also a multipart batch endpoint (`/runs/multipart`) for sending multiple post and patch operations together, which is how the SDK efficiently ships traces at scale, but that's a transport optimization, not a streaming protocol.

LangSmith's "native streaming" model is essentially:

This is a clean model and it does map cleanly to what you want for your `.llmnb` cell rendering. Specifically:

Your cell output is a sequence of these LangSmith-shaped records. The renderer reads them in order, builds up the final UI state, and shows progress as records arrive.

The previous turn's plan was: maintain a single JSON record per run; surgically edit it as the run progresses; use simdjson for fast parsing. That's a valid approach but it's reinventing what LangSmith already does — except LangSmith uses two-phase POST/PATCH semantics and append-only events instead of in-place edits.

The append-only event model is genuinely cleaner than in-place edits, for a few reasons:

Append-only is simpler. No "find this field, modify it, write back" logic. Just append events to a list. Crash-safe (partial writes don't corrupt previous events). Streaming-natural (each event is independent).

Append-only matches Jupyter's output model. Jupyter cells already accumulate output messages over time. Each LangSmith event becomes one Jupyter output message. The protocol fits.

Append-only is replayable. Replaying is just iterating events in order. No reconstruction logic. The harness you mentioned for kernel logs is essentially "read events, simulate state."

Append-only matches OTel. "LangSmith supports two trace ingestion paths. The first is the native LangSmith SDK... The second is OpenTelemetry, where applications export standard OTLP spans" Medium — OTel spans are append-only too. Adopting LangSmith's model puts you in the same shape as the broader observability ecosystem.

So yes — use LangSmith's native model. Drop the simdjson-edit-in-place idea. The append-only POST/event/PATCH pattern is structurally better.

Concretely, here's what each layer does in the LangSmith-native streaming model:

LLMKernel emits:

Extension's renderer:

LiteLLM streaming specifically:

This is genuinely cleaner than the previous turn's approach. You get streaming that:

The previous concern was: streaming updates touching the file constantly is expensive. The append-only model handles this elegantly:

Cell outputs are already the natural place for these events. Jupyter's mechanism for cell outputs handles incremental appending. Each LangSmith record becomes one output entry. The file isn't rewritten on each event; the output array gets a new entry appended via the standard Jupyter output pipeline.

The full event log (`metadata.rts.event_log` from earlier turns) becomes an array of LangSmith records. Append-only. Written periodically (snapshot interval) or on save. Crash recovery is "read what's there, resume from latest."

simdjson use case shifts: instead of "edit JSON in place" (which we're dropping), simdjson is for fast loading of the file at startup. When the operator opens an `.llmnb` with thousands of events in the log, simdjson parses it in milliseconds. That's a real use case but a different one than streaming writes.

Yes — use LangSmith's native streaming model. It's a better fit than the simdjson-edit approach for several reasons:

The "two-phase POST/PATCH plus events" model from LangSmith is the streaming protocol you want. simdjson keeps its role for fast file-loading at startup but isn't doing streaming work.

A concrete sketch of what this looks like in LLMKernel:

Roughly 50 lines for the run tracker. The renderer on the extension side has corresponding logic for handling start/event/complete messages and updating the DOM.

The display_id mechanism is Jupyter's standard for "this output replaces a previously-displayed output" — perfect fit for the run model where you want the run's UI to update in place rather than appending new outputs.

Yes, LangSmith's native streaming model is what you want. It's not a streaming protocol in the conventional sense — it's a two-phase POST/PATCH model with appendable events for fine-grained progress. This maps cleanly to:

The previous "simdjson + surgical edits" approach is replaced with "LangSmith's POST/event/PATCH semantics rendered via Jupyter's display_id mechanism." This is simpler, better-aligned with existing patterns, and easier to test.

simdjson keeps a role: fast file load at startup, when reading thousands of accumulated events. But it's not doing streaming write work; that's handled by append-only semantics.

Roughly 50-100 lines of Python in LLMKernel for the run tracker, plus corresponding logic in the extension's renderer for handling the phases. Modest implementation work for a substantial UX improvement.

Want to dig into the specific MIME type design (one type with phase field vs. three types), the renderer's state machine for handling phases, the LiteLLM-streaming-to-event mapping, or the replay harness implementation that uses this model?

```python
class RunTracker:
    def __init__(self, kernel):
        self.kernel = kernel
        self.runs = {}  # run_id -> current state
    
    def post_run(self, run_id, name, run_type, inputs, parent_run_id=None):
        record = {
            "id": run_id,
            "trace_id": ...,
            "parent_run_id": parent_run_id,
            "name": name,
            "run_type": run_type,
            "start_time": now(),
            "inputs": inputs,
            "events": [],
        }
        self.runs[run_id] = record
        # Emit to cell output via kernel display protocol
        self.kernel.send_response("display_data", {
            "data": {"application/vnd.rts.run.start+json": record},
            "metadata": {},
            "transient": {"display_id": run_id},
        })
    
    def add_event(self, run_id, event_name, data):
        event = {"name": event_name, "time": now(), "kwargs": data}
        self.runs[run_id]["events"].append(event)
        # Emit incremental update
        self.kernel.send_response("update_display_data", {
            "data": {"application/vnd.rts.run.event+json": event},
            "metadata": {"run_id": run_id},
            "transient": {"display_id": run_id},
        })
    
    def patch_run(self, run_id, outputs, error=None):
        self.runs[run_id]["end_time"] = now()
        self.runs[run_id]["outputs"] = outputs
        if error:
            self.runs[run_id]["error"] = error
        # Final update
        self.kernel.send_response("update_display_data", {
            "data": {"application/vnd.rts.run.complete+json": self.runs[run_id]},
            "metadata": {},
            "transient": {"display_id": run_id},
        })
```

1. post_run: open a run with inputs
2. events: optionally append intermediate signals (token deltas, retries, etc.)
3. patch_run: close the run with outputs

- When an agent run starts → kernel emits a post_run-shaped record into the cell output
- As the agent works → kernel emits events appended to that run (token deltas, intermediate states, child runs)
- When the run completes → kernel emits a patch_run-shaped finalization

- post_run records when starting an agent dispatch, LLM call, tool call
- events during long-running operations (token deltas from streaming LLM responses, intermediate progress)
- patch_run records when operations complete, with final outputs and timing
- All as MIME-typed displays in cell output: application/vnd.rts.run.start+json, application/vnd.rts.run.event+json, application/vnd.rts.run.complete+json (or one MIME type with internal dispatch on a "phase" field — your call)

- On run.start: render the run's initial UI (status indicator, name, inputs)
- On run.event: append to the run's display (token-by-token text, progress bar, intermediate state)
- On run.complete: finalize the run's UI (final outputs, timing, success/error)
- Maintains in-memory map from run_id to current rendered state; updates DOM as records arrive

- LiteLLM exposes streaming responses via standard OpenAI-compatible streaming APIs
- The kernel wraps these: when an agent makes a streaming LLM call, the kernel post_runs an LLM run, forwards the stream from LiteLLM, emits an event for each token chunk, then patch_runs when complete
- The cell output gets token-by-token updates rendered as they arrive

- Maps to a well-known model
- Composes with append-only file writes
- Replays cleanly from logs
- Could be exported to actual LangSmith later if you wanted external observability

1. It's a known, documented model that other tools and libraries already implement
2. Append-only semantics are naturally streaming without surgical edit logic
3. It composes with replay — events in order reconstruct state
4. It matches Jupyter's cell output model — each event is a display entry
5. It plays nicely with OpenTelemetry if you ever want broader observability
6. It's cleaner to implement — POST, events, PATCH instead of parse-modify-write

1. Drop the simdjson-edit-in-place approach for streaming. Use LangSmith's native two-phase model.
2. Three MIME types (or one with phase field) for run lifecycle: run.start, run.event, run.complete. Matches LangSmith's POST/event/PATCH model.
3. Append-only event semantics within runs. No in-place editing of run records.
4. Use Jupyter's display_id mechanism to make runs update in place in cell output, rather than appending new outputs per phase.
5. simdjson role narrows to fast file load at startup, not streaming writes.
6. metadata.rts.event_log in the file is an append-only array of LangSmith records.
7. Replay reads events in order, reconstructs state. Naturally falls out of append-only semantics.
8. OpenTelemetry export is plausible later since the format is compatible. Not a v1 concern but worth noting as a v2 option.

- Jupyter's display protocol with display_id for in-place updates
- Append-only file structure for the event log
- Replay semantics that fall out naturally
- The broader observability ecosystem (OTel-compatible)

1. LangSmith's POST/event/PATCH model is the streaming protocol.
2. Three MIME phases (start/event/complete) with display_id for in-place updates.
3. Append-only file storage.
4. simdjson for fast load, not for streaming writes.
5. Replay is iteration over append-only events.

## Assistant

Good — I have a clearer picture now. Let me give you the honest answer because the framing of "LangSmith native streaming" needs unpacking.

LangSmith's tracing model isn't really a streaming protocol in the sense you might be thinking of. It's a two-phase run model: create the run with `post_run` (sending start_time, name, run_type, inputs, parent_run_id), then end the run with `patch_run` later (sending outputs and end_time) AI SDK.

The pattern is POST-then-PATCH. You create a run record with its inputs at the start, do whatever work happens, then patch the same run with outputs when complete. This isn't streaming the content — it's just two updates to the same run record over time: "started" and "completed."

For finer-grained progress, LangSmith has events within runs: "events can be used to log additional minor information about what occurred during a run (such as streaming and retry events), when that information doesn't merit an entire child run and is not the final output of the run." Langchain So token-by-token streaming, retries, intermediate signals — these are events appended to a run, not separate child runs.

There's also a multipart batch endpoint (`/runs/multipart`) for sending multiple post and patch operations together, which is how the SDK efficiently ships traces at scale, but that's a transport optimization, not a streaming protocol.

LangSmith's "native streaming" model is essentially:

This is a clean model and it does map cleanly to what you want for your `.llmnb` cell rendering. Specifically:

Your cell output is a sequence of these LangSmith-shaped records. The renderer reads them in order, builds up the final UI state, and shows progress as records arrive.

The previous turn's plan was: maintain a single JSON record per run; surgically edit it as the run progresses; use simdjson for fast parsing. That's a valid approach but it's reinventing what LangSmith already does — except LangSmith uses two-phase POST/PATCH semantics and append-only events instead of in-place edits.

The append-only event model is genuinely cleaner than in-place edits, for a few reasons:

Append-only is simpler. No "find this field, modify it, write back" logic. Just append events to a list. Crash-safe (partial writes don't corrupt previous events). Streaming-natural (each event is independent).

Append-only matches Jupyter's output model. Jupyter cells already accumulate output messages over time. Each LangSmith event becomes one Jupyter output message. The protocol fits.

Append-only is replayable. Replaying is just iterating events in order. No reconstruction logic. The harness you mentioned for kernel logs is essentially "read events, simulate state."

Append-only matches OTel. "LangSmith supports two trace ingestion paths. The first is the native LangSmith SDK... The second is OpenTelemetry, where applications export standard OTLP spans" Medium — OTel spans are append-only too. Adopting LangSmith's model puts you in the same shape as the broader observability ecosystem.

So yes — use LangSmith's native model. Drop the simdjson-edit-in-place idea. The append-only POST/event/PATCH pattern is structurally better.

Concretely, here's what each layer does in the LangSmith-native streaming model:

LLMKernel emits:

Extension's renderer:

LiteLLM streaming specifically:

This is genuinely cleaner than the previous turn's approach. You get streaming that:

The previous concern was: streaming updates touching the file constantly is expensive. The append-only model handles this elegantly:

Cell outputs are already the natural place for these events. Jupyter's mechanism for cell outputs handles incremental appending. Each LangSmith record becomes one output entry. The file isn't rewritten on each event; the output array gets a new entry appended via the standard Jupyter output pipeline.

The full event log (`metadata.rts.event_log` from earlier turns) becomes an array of LangSmith records. Append-only. Written periodically (snapshot interval) or on save. Crash recovery is "read what's there, resume from latest."

simdjson use case shifts: instead of "edit JSON in place" (which we're dropping), simdjson is for fast loading of the file at startup. When the operator opens an `.llmnb` with thousands of events in the log, simdjson parses it in milliseconds. That's a real use case but a different one than streaming writes.

Yes — use LangSmith's native streaming model. It's a better fit than the simdjson-edit approach for several reasons:

The "two-phase POST/PATCH plus events" model from LangSmith is the streaming protocol you want. simdjson keeps its role for fast file-loading at startup but isn't doing streaming work.

A concrete sketch of what this looks like in LLMKernel:

Roughly 50 lines for the run tracker. The renderer on the extension side has corresponding logic for handling start/event/complete messages and updating the DOM.

The display_id mechanism is Jupyter's standard for "this output replaces a previously-displayed output" — perfect fit for the run model where you want the run's UI to update in place rather than appending new outputs.

Yes, LangSmith's native streaming model is what you want. It's not a streaming protocol in the conventional sense — it's a two-phase POST/PATCH model with appendable events for fine-grained progress. This maps cleanly to:

The previous "simdjson + surgical edits" approach is replaced with "LangSmith's POST/event/PATCH semantics rendered via Jupyter's display_id mechanism." This is simpler, better-aligned with existing patterns, and easier to test.

simdjson keeps a role: fast file load at startup, when reading thousands of accumulated events. But it's not doing streaming write work; that's handled by append-only semantics.

class RunTracker:
    def __init__(self, kernel):
        self.kernel = kernel
        self.runs = {}  # run_id -> current state
    
    def post_run(self, run_id, name, run_type, inputs, parent_run_id=None):
        record = {
            "id": run_id,

            "run_type": run_type,
            "start_time": now(),

        }

        # Emit to cell output via kernel display protocol

            "data": {"application/vnd.rts.run.start+json": record},
            "metadata": {},
            "transient": {"display_id": run_id},
        })
    
    def add_event(self, run_id, event_name, data):
        event = {"name": event_name, "time": now(), "kwargs": data}
        self.runs[run_id]["events"].append(event)
        # Emit incremental update
        self.kernel.send_response("update_display_data", {
            "data": {"application/vnd.rts.run.event+json": event},
            "metadata": {"run_id": run_id},
            "transient": {"display_id": run_id},
        })
    
    def patch_run(self, run_id, outputs, error=None):
        self.runs[run_id]["end_time"] = now()
        self.runs[run_id]["outputs"] = outputs
        if error:
            self.runs[run_id]["error"] = error
        # Final update

            "data": {"application/vnd.rts.run.complete+json": self.runs[run_id]},
            "metadata": {},
            "transient": {"display_id": run_id},
        })

## Assistant

Let me run through the full list and be honest about what's actually outstanding versus what's been settled. The picture has shifted considerably with the recent decisions.

Going through everything we've discussed:

MCP architecture — resolved. Kernel hosts MCP server with native tools (operator-interaction) and proxied tools (filesystem, shell). Acts as PTY mediator with LiteLLM as the LLM-side abstraction.

Bidirectional MCP between kernel and extension — resolved. Both endpoints expose MCP-shaped tools to each other. Paper telephone architecture.

Agent process management — resolved. LLMKernel manages agent subprocesses directly in Python. No separate Rust daemon for v1.

Sandbox/zone implementation — resolved (deferred to v2). Subprocess + working directory only.

System prompt strategy — softly resolved. Given "we don't care what context does," v1 doesn't need rigorous compliance verification. Best-effort prompt.

Streaming protocol — resolved. LangSmith POST/event/PATCH model with Jupyter `display_id` for in-place updates. simdjson role narrowed to fast file load.

Branching semantics — resolved. Git-based. Auto-save writes file; operator commits manually; branch switch kills running agents with confirmation.

Cell language and editor integration — resolved. Plain text with magic commands; markdown rendering for prose; custom MIME for structured events; one kernel type.

Notebook as live record — resolved. Kernel reads file on startup, reconstructs state, writes on save/close.

Observability — resolved. LangSmith blobs as universal log format. Replayable harness. Hard split between kernel failure and notebook failure.

Coexistence with Microsoft Jupyter extension — resolved. `.llmnb` exclusively; no claim on `.ipynb`.

Web environment — resolved. Desktop-only; manifest signals this.

Schema versioning — resolved (in principle). Versioning framework from day one even with single schema version.

File format — resolved. `.llmnb` as `.ipynb`-shaped JSON with `metadata.rts` namespace, custom MIME types for runs.

Storage structure — resolved. Tree for layout, graph for agents, JSON for chat flow, all embedded in `.llmnb`.

Testing strategy — resolved. Layered approach: doc-driven contracts, mocks, Markov simulation, fault injection, property-based tests, WebdriverIO for webviews. `@vscode/test-electron` + `@vscode/test-cli` for extension integration.

Subtractive cuts to fork — resolved. Specific cut list from earlier turns.

That's a substantial list of resolved decisions. The architecture is genuinely settled.

1. Week 0 is a real phase, not a planning placeholder. 5 days minimum, focused on resolving the 10 remaining blockers.
2. Risky integrations come first: LiteLLM proxy and Claude Code configuration are prototyped before any UI work.
3. End-to-end MVP is the week 1 milestone, not a later integration phase.
4. Acceptance criteria are written before implementation, defining "v1 done."
5. Tool taxonomy is locked as a written schema before any renderer work begins.
6. Custom message catalog is locked before kernel-extension wiring.
7. Replay harness is specified before logging implementation finalizes.
8. 20-30% slack is built into the timeline for unknown unknowns.

## User

## Assistant

Good frame. Bell System engineers thought about communication systems with a discipline that's directly applicable to what you're building, and the analogy to TCP/IP for LiteLLM is tighter than it might first appear. Let me work through each.

Worth grounding the reference because it's not just rhetorical. Bell Labs and Western Electric, from roughly the 1920s through the 1970s, built the most reliable, longest-lived, most-interoperable communication system in human history. They did this with a specific engineering discipline that's worth naming because the principles apply directly:

Layered abstractions with crisp boundaries. Voice calls were carried over circuits, circuits over trunks, trunks over carriers, carriers over physical media. Each layer was specified independently. A change at one layer didn't propagate across layers if the boundary was respected.

Standards before deployment. The Bell System didn't ship something and then standardize it. They wrote specifications first. RFC-style documents (called "Bell System Practices" and "Technical References") preceded equipment. Engineers in different parts of the system could build to spec and trust the boundaries.

Backward compatibility as a discipline. A telephone made in 1920 had to work on the network in 1980. This wasn't accidental — it was engineering policy. New capabilities were added without breaking old equipment.

Provisioning, operations, maintenance, administration as first-class concerns. Not just "does the call work" but "can a technician diagnose it from a central office, can it be billed, can it be configured remotely, can it be upgraded in place."

Interoperability through specification rigor. The reason a Bell System phone in California worked with a Bell System phone in New York wasn't tribal knowledge — it was that both ends conformed to the same written specifications.

Failure analysis built into design. Every component had documented failure modes. Diagnostics were designed in. Replacement was a planned operation, not an emergency.

These principles map directly to what you're building. Let me apply them to each blocker.

A Bell System engineer assigned this would write a Technical Reference document. Specific structure, specific discipline. Let me sketch what that looks like for your project.

The document would be something like "RFC-1: Operator-Agent Communication Tool Specification, Version 1.0" with this structure:

Section 1: Scope and Purpose. What this document specifies (the tools by which agents communicate with operators) and what it doesn't (transport mechanisms, rendering details, error recovery — those have their own documents).

Section 2: Definitions. Operator. Agent. Tool. Run. Trace. Each defined precisely so later sections don't drift. "An operator is the human party in the conversation, accessing the system through a designated UI surface (the extension)." "A tool is a named, typed RPC mechanism for structured communication, conforming to MCP semantics." Etc.

Section 3: Design Principles. What this taxonomy is trying to achieve. "Tools encode communicative intent, not implementation details." "Tools are versioned independently." "Tools degrade gracefully: agents that don't recognize a new tool fall back to documented alternatives." "Tools are idempotent where possible."

Section 4: Tool Specifications. The meat. For each tool, a fixed structure:

For v1 you'd have probably 8-12 tools, each specified to this depth. A document of maybe 30-40 pages.

Section 5: Error Model. How errors are reported uniformly across all tools. Standard error envelope, standard error codes, standard recovery semantics.

Section 6: Versioning. How the tool taxonomy itself evolves. When tools are added, deprecated, modified. How implementations indicate the version they speak.

Section 7: Compliance Testing. What tests an implementation must pass to claim conformance. References the doc-driven test suite.

Section 8: References. MCP specification, LangSmith schema, Jupyter messaging protocol, related project documents.

This is the document Bell would have written before any equipment was built. It's the contract. Once written and reviewed, every implementer (kernel side, extension side, agent integrators) builds to it.

The key discipline is: the document is normative, not descriptive. It says what should happen, not what currently happens. Implementations that diverge from the document are wrong; the document doesn't change to match them.

For your project, I'd argue the v1 RFC should include these tools (subject to refinement during the writing):

Operator-targeted communication tools (agent → operator):

Status and reporting tools (agent → operator):

Coordination tools (agent → agent or agent → operator):

Possible additions for v1.x:

That's 9-12 tools for v1. Each gets a full section in the RFC.

The RFC itself is roughly 2-3 days of focused writing work. Probably worth doing it as a real document — markdown in the repo, version-controlled, with a clear "Status: Draft / Review / Final" header.

Bell Labs would assign one engineer to draft, others to review, and a release process before the document was authoritative. For your project, you draft, Claude Code reviews/critiques, you finalize. Same discipline.

A Bell System engineer asked "how do we connect a new switch to the existing network" wouldn't experiment. They'd pull out the relevant Bell System Practice (BSP), follow the documented installation procedure, verify each step against the test specification, and document any deviations as supplements to the BSP.

Applied to Claude Code integration: this is fundamentally a provisioning specification. Not exploratory work but a documented procedure that can be repeated reliably and verified.

The document — call it "Provisioning Specification: Claude Code Agent Integration with LLMKernel, v1.0" — has this structure:

Section 1: Prerequisites. What must be true before provisioning. "Claude Code CLI installed at version X.Y or later. LLMKernel installed and accessible. LiteLLM proxy endpoint reachable. Working directory exists and is writable. API keys configured per Section 4."

Section 2: Configuration Parameters. What's set during provisioning.

For each parameter: name, type, default value, valid range, effect.

Section 3: Provisioning Procedure. Step-by-step. "1. Verify prerequisites per Section 1. 2. Create configuration file at path X with content per Appendix A. 3. Launch Claude Code with command line per Appendix B. 4. Verify successful launch by checking for log line 'MCP connection established to <kernel endpoint>' within 10 seconds. 5. ..."

Each step has a verification action. If verification fails, the procedure says what to do (retry, abort, escalate).

Section 4: Verification Tests. A test plan that demonstrates the agent is correctly provisioned. "Test 4.1: Send a message to the agent; verify within 5 seconds the kernel sees an MCP tool call from the agent. Test 4.2: Trigger an LLM call from the agent; verify within 2 seconds the kernel's LiteLLM endpoint logs the call. Test 4.3: Operator requests approval; verify the agent receives the response..."

Section 5: Failure Modes. Documented failure scenarios with diagnoses and recovery actions. "Symptom: agent starts but doesn't connect to MCP. Likely causes: ... Diagnosis: ... Recovery: ..."

Section 6: Operations. How to monitor a running agent, restart it, terminate it, upgrade it.

Appendices. Sample configuration files, sample command lines, sample log outputs for verification.

This is what a Bell engineer would produce. The discipline matters because the Claude Code integration is the riskiest unknown — by treating it as a provisioning specification, you replace "let's try and see" with "here's the procedure; here's the test plan; here's what to do if it fails."

The document is partly written by reading Claude Code's documentation (what it accepts, what flags it supports, what behaviors it has) and partly by hands-on prototyping (what actually works versus what the docs say). The result is durable: it doesn't just verify v1 works, it serves as the spec for future agent integrations (a similar document for OpenCode, for Aider, etc.).

The work is roughly: half a day of doc-reading, 1-2 days of hands-on prototyping with documentation written as you go, half a day of cleanup. ~2-3 days total. Bell engineers would have allocated similar time for a "new equipment integration" task.

The Jupyter messaging protocol over ZeroMQ is, in Bell System terms, a signaling system — the part of the protocol that conveys control information rather than payload. Adding custom message types is exactly analogous to extending a signaling system to carry new types of information (think of how SS7 was extended over the decades to carry new call-control primitives).

Bell engineers extending a signaling system would write a specification with this structure:

"Signaling Specification: RTS Extensions to Jupyter Messaging Protocol, v1.0"

Section 1: Scope. What's extended (the Jupyter messaging protocol's `comm_msg` and custom message channels) and what isn't (the underlying ZeroMQ transport, the standard message types).

Section 2: Message Catalog. For each custom message:

For your project, the v1 message catalog might include:

State synchronization (kernel → extension):

Operator actions (extension → kernel):

Bidirectional:

That's about 10-12 messages for v1. Each gets a full specification.

Section 3: Channel Assignment. Which messages go on which ZeroMQ channel and why. (For your project: most go on iopub for kernel→frontend broadcast; commands go on shell for request/response; control channel reserved for lifecycle.)

Section 4: Reliability and Ordering. Which messages can be lost (idempotent ones can; commands cannot). Which must be ordered (run lifecycle messages must arrive in start/event*/complete order). What happens if ordering is violated.

Section 5: Versioning. How the message catalog evolves. Capability negotiation between kernel and extension at startup determines which messages are usable.

Section 6: Compatibility. Older clients receiving newer messages should handle gracefully (ignore, log warning, etc.). Newer clients receiving older clients' messages should not assume new fields exist.

Section 7: Compliance Tests. What an implementation must do to claim conformance. References the doc-driven test suite.

This document, like the tool RFC, is roughly 2-3 days of focused writing. It produces a stable contract between kernel and extension implementations.

The Bell discipline here matters specifically because messaging protocols are one of those things where it's tempting to "just add a new message type when needed." That's how protocols accrete cruft and become unmaintainable. Writing the catalog up front, treating new messages as RFC-revision events, keeps the surface clean.

This framing is actually quite tight. Let me unpack why and what it implies.

TCP/IP is a layered protocol stack:

Each layer has well-defined responsibilities and interfaces. Applications using HTTP don't think about IP packets; HTTP libraries don't think about Ethernet frames. The layers compose.

LiteLLM as TCP/IP for LLMs:

This is real. It's the right way to think about LiteLLM's role.

The implications for your architecture:

Treat LiteLLM as a stable abstraction. Don't poke through it to provider-specific features. If a feature isn't in LiteLLM's uniform interface, either don't use it or contribute it upstream. This preserves the layering.

Build your kernel against LiteLLM's interface, not against any provider. The kernel's LLM-call handling code should never reference "Anthropic" or "OpenAI" specifically. Every call is "LiteLLM call with provider X selected." Provider becomes a config parameter, not a code branch.

LiteLLM's proxy mode is the analog of a TCP socket. Just as a TCP socket exposes a byte-stream interface that applications use without thinking about IP routing, LiteLLM's proxy exposes an OpenAI-compatible HTTP endpoint that agents use without thinking about which provider is on the other end. Agents configure their `OPENAI_API_BASE` to your kernel's LiteLLM proxy URL; that's the socket-equivalent.

Errors map across layers. Provider-specific errors (Anthropic 529, OpenAI 429) become uniform LiteLLM errors (rate limit, server error, timeout). Your kernel handles uniform errors. You don't write code that branches on "if Anthropic and 529 then..." — you write code that handles "if rate limit then..."

Streaming is part of the abstraction. LiteLLM handles streaming responses uniformly across providers. Your kernel receives a stream from LiteLLM; doesn't need to know how the underlying provider streamed it. The events (token deltas, tool call deltas) are normalized.

Tool/function calling is part of the abstraction. LiteLLM normalizes tool calling across providers (each has its own format). Your kernel sees uniform tool calls in responses; agents send uniform tool definitions.

This is genuinely how you should think about it. LiteLLM is the layer between your kernel and the messy world of provider APIs. Trust the layer; build above it.

A specific design implication: the kernel's "LLM intercept" logic is operating at the LiteLLM layer, not at the provider layer. Every modification, every cache, every redirect happens in LiteLLM-normalized terms. This means it works for any provider LiteLLM supports, automatically. You add a new provider to LiteLLM (or wait for them to add it) and your kernel benefits without code changes.

The Bell System parallel: layered protocols allow independent evolution. The phone network's signaling system evolved independently from the payload (voice). Voice quality improved (analog → digital → packet), signaling evolved (in-band tones → SS6 → SS7), but the layering kept them separable.

For your project: LLM providers will evolve. New ones will appear. Old ones will deprecate features. Models will become capable in new ways. By layering through LiteLLM, your code remains stable as the layer below churns.

What this means concretely for blocker 4 resolution:

Don't treat "LiteLLM integration" as a custom integration. It's not. LiteLLM is an off-the-shelf component you adopt. Your kernel uses LiteLLM the way an application uses TCP — call its functions, handle its errors, trust its abstraction.

The "LiteLLM proxy server" is your kernel's outbound LLM endpoint. LiteLLM has a proxy server mode that's exactly this: an OpenAI-compatible HTTP server that agents point at. Your kernel either embeds this or uses it as a sidecar. Either way, you're not writing the proxy logic — LiteLLM is.

Your custom logic is what wraps LiteLLM. The interception (logging, modification, redirection) happens at the LiteLLM-call layer. Hooks, middleware, callbacks — LiteLLM has mechanisms for these. Use them.

Your testing happens at the LiteLLM layer. Mock LiteLLM responses; don't mock provider responses. The mock is at the right abstraction.

Implementation work for blocker 4 then collapses to:

Total: probably half a day to a day of work, not multiple days. It's a stable layer; you're using it, not building it.

The mistake to avoid: writing your own multi-provider abstraction. You'd be redoing what LiteLLM has done, worse and slower. Trust the layer.

The Bell System framing is good and clarifies the work substantially. The blockers I had as discrete items resolve into:

That's about 10 days of focused week-0 work. After this, you have a comprehensive normative spec set, verified integrations, and clear v1 acceptance criteria. Implementation in weeks 1-6 happens against stable contracts.

The discipline matters because the alternative — "let's just start coding and figure it out" — produces the kind of system that works once and breaks on contact with reality. Bell System engineers built the most reliable communication network in history by writing specs first. Treating this project the same way, even at small scale, produces a v1 that ships and survives.

Decisions to lock:

Want me to draft the actual RFC-1 (Tool Taxonomy) outline in detail, work through the Provisioning Spec structure for Claude Code integration, walk through the LiteLLM-as-layer integration design more concretely, or shift to the v1 acceptance criteria document?

- Tool name (canonical identifier)
- Purpose (one sentence)
- Description (one paragraph for the agent's system prompt)
- Input schema (formal: JSON Schema)
- Output schema (formal: JSON Schema)
- Error conditions (enumerated)
- Examples (at least two: typical use, edge case)
- Notes (anything implementers should know)
- Compatibility (versions this is supported in)

- ask — pose a question to the operator, await structured response
- request_approval — request authorization for a proposed action
- request_resource — ask for a specific input the operator must provide
- notify — fire-and-forget informational message
- escalate — flag a situation requiring operator attention urgently

- report_progress — incremental status update on ongoing work
- report_milestone — discrete achievement marker
- report_problem — non-fatal issue requiring guidance
- report_completion — task complete, summary provided

- request_handoff — transfer responsibility to another party
- propose_plan — submit structured plan for review

- request_clarification — narrower than ask, specifically for clarifying prior input
- request_collaboration — engage another agent
- present_artifact — surface a specific generated output for operator attention

- MCP server endpoint URL (where the kernel hosts MCP)
- LiteLLM API base URL (the kernel's OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
- API key for kernel authentication (not the provider's actual key — a kernel-issued credential)
- Working directory absolute path
- System prompt path or content
- Tool restrictions (--allowedTools flags)
- Environment variables to set
- Logging configuration
- Resource limits (timeout, memory)

- Message type (canonical name, e.g., rts.layout.update)
- Direction (kernel → frontend, frontend → kernel, or bidirectional)
- Channel (shell, iopub, control, or comm)
- Required header fields
- Required content fields with schemas
- Optional fields with schemas
- Semantics (what receivers must do)
- Acknowledgment requirements (does this expect a reply?)
- Ordering constraints (can it be sent during certain states?)
- Examples

- rts.layout.update — layout tree changed; here's the new state
- rts.agents.update — agent graph changed; here's the new state
- rts.run.start — a new run is beginning (LangSmith POST equivalent)
- rts.run.event — an event in an ongoing run
- rts.run.complete — a run has completed (LangSmith PATCH equivalent)
- rts.error — an error condition with classification (kernel-level vs. notebook-level)

- rts.layout.edit — operator modified layout (drag, save viewpoint, etc.)
- rts.operator.respond — operator's response to a pending tool call
- rts.command — operator triggered an action (spawn, kill, transfer, branch)
- rts.cell.execute — special cell execution requests (e.g., re-run with different parameters)

- rts.heartbeat — health check
- rts.capability.query — what does the other side support
- rts.capability.advertise — what this side supports

- IP (Layer 3): packet routing across networks; doesn't care about content
- TCP (Layer 4): reliable, ordered, byte-stream connections; built on IP
- Application protocols (Layer 7): HTTP, SMTP, etc.; built on TCP

- Provider APIs (analog: Layer 2/3): the actual provider-specific protocols (Anthropic Messages API, OpenAI Chat Completions, Google Gemini, etc.). Each provider has its own format, auth, error semantics.
- LiteLLM (analog: Layer 4): provides a uniform interface across providers. Translates between the unified format and provider-specific formats. Handles auth uniformly. Reports errors uniformly. Streaming, function calling, etc. all normalized.
- Your kernel using LiteLLM (analog: Layer 7 application): uses LiteLLM's uniform interface; doesn't think about provider-specific quirks; benefits from any provider LiteLLM supports.

1. Run LiteLLM proxy mode (or embed it in the kernel process) — configuration work, not coding
2. Configure the proxy to dispatch to providers your operators will use — config
3. Hook into LiteLLM's logging/middleware for the kernel's interception — small amount of Python
4. Configure agents to use the proxy as their API base — agent configuration

- Day 1-2: write RFC-1 (Tool Taxonomy)
- Day 2-3: write RFC-2 (Custom Messages)
- Day 3: write Provisioning Spec (Claude Code integration)
- Day 4: prototype the LiteLLM proxy + agent configuration
- Day 5: prototype end-to-end one tool call flowing through the system

- 3 RFC-style documents (~2-3 days drafting + review)
- 1 Provisioning Spec with prototyping (~2 days)
- 1 acceptance criteria document (~half day)
- LiteLLM integration verification (~half day)
- Test harness setup (~half day)
- Kernelspec packaging (~half day)

1. RFC discipline for protocol-level specifications. Tool taxonomy, custom messages, replay harness, error model — each gets its own normative document.
2. Tool taxonomy specifically as RFC-1, ~30-40 page document, drafted week 0.
3. Custom messaging specification as RFC-2, similar discipline.
4. Claude Code integration as a Provisioning Spec, with documented prerequisites, configuration parameters, procedure, verification tests, failure modes.
5. LiteLLM as a stable layer, used like TCP/IP. Not custom-integrated; configured and used.
6. LiteLLM proxy mode is the agent's LLM endpoint. Standard component, not bespoke.
7. Kernel-side interception happens at the LiteLLM-call layer, not at the provider layer. Any LiteLLM-supported provider works automatically.
8. Layered architecture preserved. Provider quirks stay below LiteLLM; agent code stays above LiteLLM; kernel logic stays in the middle.
9. Bell-style discipline of specification before implementation. Implementations conform to specs; specs are normative; deviation is a bug, not an acceptable variation.
10. Backward compatibility as a discipline from day one. Schema versioning, capability negotiation, graceful degradation — designed in, not bolted on.

- Three RFC documents (tool taxonomy, custom messages, possibly replay) — pure design work, ~6 days total
- One provisioning specification (Claude Code integration) — design + prototyping, ~2 days
- LiteLLM treated as a layer, not integrated as a custom component — configuration, ~half day
- Acceptance criteria document — ~half a day
- Standard packaging tasks — kernelspec, test harness — ~1 day total

2. All four are normative documents that implementations must conform to.
3. LiteLLM treated as TCP/IP-equivalent layer, used not built.
4. Bell-style discipline of specification, layering, backward compatibility, failure analysis.
5. Implementation starts week 1 against stable contracts.
